                                                   BUDGET NARRATIVE
     Mission Statement
     The mission of Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) Department is to provide stable and affordable housing and engage leaders to
     develop an integrated statewide policy that addresses poverty and provides opportunity for Oregonians. We do this by:
        • Creating economic opportunity and stability for Oregonians by using housing as a platform for their success.
        • Strengthening partnerships among public, private, and nonprofit organizations, as well as between state agencies, to effectively create more
            affordable housing and support services.
        • Maximizing and leveraging resources to advance housing and homelessness response goals.
        • Responding to housing inequities and addressing the disproportionate impacts of housing instability on people of color, protected classes,
            and underserved communities.


     Statutory Authority
     Oregon Revised Statutes (ORS) 456.555 establishes the Housing and Community Services Department (OHCS) and defines the agency’s governance.
     Additional statutes that authorize OHCS activities and describe responsibilities are in the following ORS chapters:


            90.643 - 90.671                                 294.187                                  456.250 – 456.270
            90.732 - 90.738                                 307.203                                  456.355 - 456.725
            90.767 - 90.775                            315.164 - 315.169                             458.215 - 458.740
            90.840 – 90.849                            315.271 - 315.272                             566.340 - 566.360
            131A.005                                        317.097                                  757.612 – 757.650
            183.530 – 183.534                               317.991                                            757.687
            279A.025, 279A.050                              426.506




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                               107BF02
                                                                           15
                                                    BUDGET NARRATIVE
     Agency Strategic Plan
     OHCS uses Oregon’s Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), a five-year strategic plan (2019-2024), to guide policy and funding decisions. The SWHP
     includes six priorities that reflect the areas where OHCS can grow and lead to better address housing needs. For each priority, the SWHP outlines
     the evidence supporting it, the goals associated with addressing it, the strategies OHCS will undertake to implement it, the funding opportunities
     and challenges associated with implementation, and our intention to work with partners to advance these priorities. The six priorities are:

                            Equity and Racial Justice – Advance equity and racial justice to address disparities in
                            housing and economic prosperity.

                            Homelessness – Build a coordinated and concerted statewide effort to prevent and end
                            homelessness.

                            Permanent Supportive Housing – Invest in permanent supportive housing.

                            Affordable Rental Housing – Work to close the affordable rental housing gap.

                            Homeownership – Expand homeownership for low- and moderate-income Oregonians.

                            Rural Communities – Unlock housing opportunities in small towns and rural communities.

     Despite the many challenges over the last five years, OHCS made considerable progress in advancing Oregon’s housing and homelessness response
     goals as outlined in the Statewide Housing Plan. For the six goals corresponding to each of the six priorities, we exceeded the goal for five of them.
     Even for the sixth goal, we achieved 93% completion. These outcomes demonstrate significant progress that can be seen across Oregon and felt,
     most particularly, by the people we serve. They also show there is more work to do and the need for continued investment.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                             16
                                                     BUDGET NARRATIVE
     Criteria for 2025-27 Budget Development
     Housing and homelessness response are complex issues that often seem daunting to address. However, as described previously, we can make progress
     in these areas and, in fact, have been making progress in Oregon. Governor Kotek has given clear direction to OHCS through Executive Orders 23-
     02, 23-02, and 23-04 and through the Homelessness Response Framework and the Housing Production Framework that housing and homelessness
     are priorities for the State, and OHCS (along with our partner agencies) must continue making progress. That is what this Governor’s Recommended
     Budget represents—it is the document outlining the continued progress for housing and homelessness response and shows what is possible in Oregon
     this next biennium with meaningful investments in key areas.

     Along with the Statewide Housing Plan, OHCS uses our core values of collaboration, compassion, dedication, equity, integrity, and leadership to lead
     our work. Additionally, we used the following principles:
        • Center underserved communities and make progress for all people.
        • Recognize that local governments and other partners are invaluable to our work.
        • Expand existing tools to support even more Oregonians.
        • Continue to embrace the expanding nature of our work, knowing that we must change, grow, and evolve in order to effectively serve
          Oregonians and the housing needs they face today.
     With those principles in mind, OHCS further refined requested investments through engagement with external partners. The agency held webinars
     and conducted surveys, hearing from local governments, service providers, and others from across the state. The 2025-27 Governor’s Recommended
     Budget is the result of extensive internal and external work to determine what is needed and what is possible in the next biennium.


     Agency Programs
     The vision statement that guides OHCS is “Through centering humanity in our investments, all Oregonians have the opportunity to pursue prosperity
     and live free from poverty in their community” validates the need for statewide clarity and focus on efforts to increase and sustain housing stabilization
     for Oregonians in need. The agency programs support the strategies that help all Oregonians have the opportunity to work toward self-sufficiency
     and increased personal well-being.
     The department’s programs address the continuum of housing needs of all Oregonians, from homelessness prevention and housing stabilization to
     affordable rental housing, to home ownership and foreclosure prevention. The services funded by OHCS are summarized in the following overview
     of the agency’s program units.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                              17
                                                    BUDGET NARRATIVE
     Housing Stabilization Programs
     This program unit comprises a continuum of services intended to help individuals stabilize their housing, including Homeless Services and Energy
     Services.
     Homelessness Services include programs that provide access to emergency housing, rental assistance, and other activities that assist homeless
     individuals or families, or that prevent homelessness from occurring.
     Energy Services programs mitigate high energy costs, address health and safety risks, improve energy efficiency in the homes of low-income
     Oregonians, and serve as housing stabilization tools. Services include utility bill payment assistance, health and safety improvements, heating system
     repair and replacement, energy conservation services, replacement of inefficient appliances and lighting, and energy conservation education.
     Related housing stabilization programs work to address poverty and help low-income Oregonians build assets. Programs include the Individual
     Development Account (IDA) program and landlord rent guarantee programs.


     Project-Based Rental Assistance
     The Project-Based Rental Assistance (PBRA) program was created in the 2021-23 biennium. The PBRA programs provide rental assistance that is
     associated with specific housing units rather than awarded to a tenant regardless of where the person lives. The Project-Based Rent Assistance efforts
     of OHCS are three-fold: HUD Contract Administration (HCA), HUD 811 PRA (HUD 811), and Permanent Supportive Housing Project-Based Rent
     Assistance (PSH PBRA). To support these programs, the department has also launched Risk Mitigation funds which serve to off-set the insurance
     impacts associated with serving permanent supportive housing populations.


     Affordable Rental (a.k.a. Multifamily Rental) Housing Programs
     OHCS facilitates the increased availability of safe, decent, affordable housing for low- and moderate-income Oregonians by administering federal
     and state-funded multifamily rental housing resources. The resources assist in developing new housing units, financing for acquisition and
     rehabilitation of existing housing units, and preserving affordable housing properties with project-based federal rental subsidies.
     The Portfolio Administration Section ensures that rental housing developed using OHCS funds complies with all of the requirements of the various
     funding sources and remains affordable to low-income households.


     Homeownership (a.k.a. Single-Family Housing) Programs




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                 107BF02
                                                                            18
                                                    BUDGET NARRATIVE
     The Homeownership Division consists of four sections, each driven by the central goal of engaging low to moderate income Oregonians who wish to
     attain or retain homeownership: Homeownership Programs, Manufactured & Marina Communities, Homeowner Assistance, and Homeownership
     Lending. Collectively, these programs expand access to affordable homeownership through competitive market rate mortgages, down payment
     assistance, and pre-purchase education and counseling, and by assisting homeowners to retain their homes through education, foreclosure counseling,
     and health and safety repairs, including resources specific to manufactured housing. In addition, Homeownership Development Programs increase
     the supply of affordable homes for purchase, addressing a dearth in affordable homes available throughout the state. These programs benefit
     homebuyers and homeowners who are typically not served by traditional lenders, and who would not otherwise have access to the housing market
     and homeownership services.


     Disaster Recovery and Resiliency
     The Disaster Recovery and Resilience (DRR) Division of OHCS was founded in response to the massive destruction of the 2020 Labor Day wildfires
     and straight-line winds. OHCS was funded by the Oregon Legislature to support housing and community recovery. The DRR division was identified
     as the implementing agency for the $422M Community Development Block Grant – Disaster Recovery (CDBG-DR) grant appropriated by Congress
     and granted by the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) in response to the 2020 Labor Day fires. DRR is focused primarily on
     implementing a series of programs, called ReOregon, that serve impacted individuals, families and communities in eight counties - Clackamas,
     Douglas, Jackson, Klamath, Lane, Lincoln, Linn and Marion. Government.


     Central Services
     The Central Services program unit provides essential leadership and business support crucial to achieving the vision of the agency: All Oregonians
     have the opportunity to pursue prosperity and live free from poverty. Central Services includes the Director’s Office, the Research Office, the Public
     Affairs Office, the Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Office, Human Resources Office, and the Central Services Division that consists of the Business
     Services Section, the Chief Finance Section, the Procurement and Grant Compliance Section, the Accounting Section, Project Management office,
     and Information Services.


     Bond Activities and Debt Service
     OHCS’ bond-financed loan programs finance safe and affordable rental housing for low-income Oregonians and provide opportunities for first-time
     homebuyers to finance their mortgages at competitive interest rates. Bond-related activities include disbursement of bond proceeds to finance
     multifamily and single-family mortgage loans, bond issuance costs, administrative expenses related to outstanding debt, and asset-protection costs
     associated with foreclosures and acquired properties. Debt service activities represent non-limited expenditures that repay investors and other parties’



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                             19
                                                   BUDGET NARRATIVE
     obligations owed on the outstanding debt issued by the agency to fund various bond financed loan program activities. The Capital Markets and Debt
     Management Team, which is the staff needed to manage the department’s bond indentures, is include here as well.
     Debt service for Article XI-Q General Obligation bonds and lottery-backed bonds issued on the department’s behalf is also in this program unit. Bond
     proceeds have been used for the Local Innovation and Fast Track (LIFT) Housing program, Permanent Supportive Housing program, providing
     housing for tenants with mental health and addiction issues, and preserving housing with federal rent subsidies and manufactured home parks.


     Capital Construction
     This program unit was created for the proceeds from Article XI-Q General Obligation bonds. The bond proceeds are categorized as Capital
     Construction with an expenditure limitation period of six years, and the funds are used for financing affordable housing development.

COMMUNITY PLAN for EQUITY, RACIAL JUSTICE, and BELONGING
 OHCS Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Action Plan for the State of Oregon




                                    June 2024

“As Oregon’s state housing finance agency our responsibility to all people of
Oregon is to solve complex housing challenges. This responsibility creates an
inherent mandate that we do not replicate previous ways of doing business that
inflicted generational harm. We intentionally place racial equity at the forefront
of our state action plan to resist and reverse discriminatory policies and practices
which lower the ceiling of shared progress.”
                                         – a message from OHCS Executive Director &
                                Director of the Office of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

                                                                22
Executive Summary
Our commitment to Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) began over five years
ago when we stepped out boldly with equity and racial justice (ERJ) initiatives as
the backbone of our 2019-2023 Statewide Housing Plan. In 2019, along with
several other state agencies and government entities in the Pacific Northwest, we
joined as members of the Government Alliance on Race and Equity (GARE) and
hired an equity consultant to conduct an organizational assessment to inform a
comprehensive organizational plan. We acted upon this plan and established our
agency’s first Office of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion to oversee and support our
ERJ work. Today, our internal structure of people and resources is robust and this
year (2024) we are ushering in a new EDI Community Organization. We have had
many successes and learned a lot since those early years and remain in gratitude
for our staff’s passion and unwavering dedication to mission. It’s because of them
we are well positioned and excited to rise to the challenges of tomorrow.


We are encouraged by Oregon State Government’s continued commitment to
embed diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) into our work and are pleased to
provide our first official state action plan. Our Community Plan for Equity, Racial
Justice, and Belonging (“OHCS DEI Action Plan”) follows GARE's approach to
Visualize, Normalize, Organize, and Operationalize which is intended to advance
racial justice initiatives in government. The operational component – our EDI
Implementation Plan follows a structure of Focus Areas, Objectives, Strategies,
and Key Results (“OSKRs”). This OSKR structure was adopted to better align with
our new OHCS Strategic Plan (launching in 2024) which will be focused on
organizational assessments and organizational improvements to support our staff



                                         23
and the communities we serve. We acknowledge and appreciate the Oregon State
DEI Action Plan is available as a standing resource and we anticipate returning to
it often as we further evolve our OHCS DEI Action Plan.


Our OHCS DEI Action Plan is covered in three parts. Part one: Equity, Racial
Justice, and Belonging at OHCS – describes our approach to visualize and
normalize and details our why, who we are, how we work, and our leadership
values. It also details how we live our commitment to ERJ, increase cultural
competency, and grow our internal community. Part two: OHCS’s Equity,
Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) Community Organization – describes our approach
to organize & operationalize and details how we care for our community, our
new EDI design, and our focus on learning and development. It also details all the
elements of our EDI Implementation Plan. Part three: On the Horizon – describes
next steps and provides closing remarks.


We believe our OHCS DEI Action Plan will ground us in our commitment and keep
us focused on what really matters which is to serve all our communities, as we
continue to pursue housing and economic justice in the national affordable
housing industry and right at home in Oregon.

Part One: Equity, Racial Justice, and Belonging at OHCS

A. Visualize. Before we can, “develop a vision with communities most negatively
   impacted by racial inequities [where] communities are no longer experiencing
   racial inequities, when ALL people are thriving with purpose and power,” we




                                        24
must fully comprehend the root cause of housing inequities and embrace a
greater understanding of our role as changemaking leaders in government.


The core of our “why” is grounded in the root causes of housing inequities.
Local and national housing policies have long been a product of structural
racism. To better understand current disparities in Oregon’s housing markets,
we must examine the lasting impacts of past discriminatory and exclusionary
programs and policies, which included explicit and specific exclusion of Black
people in the state. From the 1930s through the 1960s, Federal Housing
Administration policies explicitly limited loans to neighborhoods of color based
on race. Approximately 98% of FHA loans during this time went to white
applicants. This practice, combined with others such as segregation in schools
and a lack of access to housing for people of color through exclusionary
brokering, illustrate how past practices that shaped residential segregation led
to disproportionate generational wealth accumulation and racial inequities
today.


There have been attempts to mitigate the pervasive harm caused by
discriminatory housing policies, such as the Supreme Court outlawing of
restrictive residential covenants in 1948 and the passing of the Fair Housing
Act in 1968. This law expanded on previous acts and prohibited discrimination
concerning the sale, rental, and financing of housing based on race, religion,
national origin, sex, disability, and family status. Despite these efforts, people
of color experience both active discrimination in the housing market and are
impacted by the lasting impacts of past exclusionary policies. It is important to



                                       25
understand these historic discriminatory policies and how well-intentioned
laws have yet to address the disparities to design more equitable programs
and policies that move toward eliminating these inequities. Studies show that
intentional strategies that raise awareness of the root causes of systemic
inequities strategically help to sustain long-term inclusive policies and
practices.


Who we are. Our mission is to improve all Oregonians' quality of life through
investing in safe, stable, and affordable housing and engaging leaders to
develop an integrated statewide policy centered on racial equity. Our vision
believes that through centering humanity in our investments, all Oregonians
have the opportunity to pursue prosperity and live free from poverty in their
community. Our role intentionally focuses on serving Oregonians across the
housing continuum. This includes preventing homelessness, reducing energy
burden, providing housing stability support, innovating in affordable housing
financing and preservation, reducing the racial wealth gap through
homeownership and asset building, and strengthening community resiliency.


How we work. The shared values of our internal OHCS community are the
most vital aspect for sustaining success. OHCS leadership offers diverse
expertise and experience that ranges across disciplines. Collectively, we lead
by setting standards for how we are in the community, with each other, and
more broadly. These standards serve as guidelines for how we engage, achieve
decisions, balance priorities, and provide autonomy to leaders at each level.
Our leaders are empowered and expected to apply these standards in



                                      26
independently achieving decisions aligned with how we work collectively. We
are proud to be an organization of leaders in the national affordable housing
industry and we know we can do great things when we work together with
community.


OHCS leadership values
   • We are human-centered. OHCS puts the people we serve at the center
      of all we do. This approach requires recognizing systems of oppression
      that historically and presently harm black, indigenous, and people of
      color and acting boldly to upend them. We control more housing
      resources than any other organization in Oregon. We must center our
      collective humanity and understand the short and long-term impact of
      our policies and programs.
   • We are proactive. We all came to government because we believe in
      public service, and these are our institutions. We all know we have a
      housing crisis; our role requires us to think both short and long term,
      meaning modernizing processes and thinking holistically across OHCS
      resources.
   • We are convenors & thought leaders. We have a unique vantage point
      to understand what works across the state and nation. Our passion is
      embodied through our work, and it is our job to lead the conversation
      and to use our standing as Oregon's Housing Finance Agency to convene
      our partners to exchange ideas and move the work forward.




                                     27
      • We are committed to continuous improvement. We are partners in
         shared success. This begins with the effectiveness of our internal
         operations. Our collective responsibility is to act with trust and courage
         to challenge the status quo. This means speaking truth to power and
         making proactive improvements that advance our mission.
      • We are responsive to emergencies. OHCS works to reduce suffering and
         facilitates resiliency through our disaster recovery role. At times we may
         receive Federal funding to launch emergency-specific programs that
         sometimes require direct service approaches. Being close to disaster
         survivors is crucial to understanding and resolving their needs quickly.


B. Normalize. At OHCS we, “use a racial equity framework [of building culture
   and community] that clearly names the history of government in creating and
   maintaining racial inequities, envisions and operationalizes a new role, and
   utilizes clear and easily understood definitions of racial equity and inequity.”
   We build culture and community by living our commitment to equity and racial
   justice. We cultivate opportunities that encourage staff to increase cultural
   competency as we continue to grow our internal community of equity leaders
   and practitioners.


   OHCS is deeply dedicated to reversing the multigenerational effects of
   structural racism and inequity. We know that this work will take time and that
   there are many obstacles to overcome. This is why we are dedicated to
   cultivating a culture of belonging at the agency that embraces our agency




                                          28
“why” and provides support for one another as we learn and grow leadership
skills and abilities together.


How we build culture and community
Equity Leaders and Practitioners. In 2022 we contracted with the Center for
Equity & Inclusion (Portland, OR) to start a leadership cohort for our agency’s
equity leaders and supervising managers. We intentionally set out to grow
leadership skills and abilities within this collective to serve as a sustainable
resource for all agency staff. In 2023, the cohort expanded to include Human
Resource Services, additional equity leaders and practitioners, and targeted
training and consultation for our executive team. This year (2024) we can offer
all agency staff access to equity training courses through CEI’s online platform.


CEI is a specialized consulting agency focused on advancing racial equity,
diversity, and inclusion efforts within organizations driven by transformational
change and culture shifting. Their work, as should ours be, comes from
increasing racial and economic disparities experienced in communities, and
focuses on long term organizational change via training and leadership
development. At the outset of our partnership with CEI we strategically chose
to focus our work with this core group of positional leaders because, while we
have a firm belief that there are leaders across our organization, we also know
that staff who specifically hold leadership-level positions have an increased
responsibility to raise our consciousness and understand how we can and
should lead with racial justice and equity at the forefront of all that we do;
everything from how we listen to and engage with our staff on their



                                       29
employment experiences, to how we make decisions and put critical
investments in a way that is equitable and decreases disparities. This requires
practice, trust-building, and willingness to have uncomfortable and courageous
conversations.


Cultural Events. Cultural events are opportunities to create a sense of
community by bringing staff together around celebration, education, story-
telling, and compassionate relationship building. Staff have opportunities
throughout the year to see and support one another, increase their cultural
competency, and engage in meaningful discussion with colleagues. Through a
multicultural lens, a cultural events calendar has been created to celebrate
and acknowledge significant historical events in 2024 and we have added two
campaigns inspiring collective action hoping to leverage partnerships with
other state government organizations. Cultural events are organized in one of
two ways: (1) a cultural spotlight (video content or written statement sourced
internally with staff and outside resources) or (2) community coffee chat
(virtual meeting with guest speakers and attendee participants).


Affinity Groups. To best serve employees across the agency, OHCS promotes
and supports a diverse community of employees with a shared desire to
gather in affinity. The ability to convene on a regular basis goes a long way in
making employees feel connected, especially with a predominately virtual
workforce. Affinity groups are part of how we build culture and community.




                                      30
Equity Practice.
   1. The six agreements from Courageous Conversations are part of our
      equity practice and culture at OHCS. They may be used one-one and
      mostly used with groups to help develop trust, center relationships, and
      cultivate mutual respect and accountability.
            Stay engaged             Speak your truth           Listen to
                                           responsibly        understand
        Be willing to do things     Expect and accept       Confidentiality
            differently and             non-closure
        experience discomfort



   2. My “why” equity story. Creating a personal why unlocks the potential to
      align yourself and agency values with your work because stories connect
      us individually and collectively with our shared humanity. Using the
      model learned from CEI, we facilitate creative workshops for staff to
      write their own personal equity story. Here are a few staff quotes
      reflecting on the value of this equity practice:


         • “Equity stories celebrate our differences and invite us to recognize
            our commonalities, especially when it comes to how we align who
            we are with the work we do in partnership with others.”
         • “It helps me build, maintain, and sustain engagement in the
            workplace. It makes equity work less academic, less abstract and
            connects me to my personal stake in what we are doing.”
         • “It is anchoring for me. It builds a connection and takes away the
            abstraction, especially as a white person. Reframes it on yourself,



                                      31
               not something you're doing for someone else (altruism). It
               recognizes that I'm in it too. Equity stories teach us we can all be
               so different as we work towards the same goal. Understanding
               stories told by others connects us to identities we share, a common
               ground.”
            • “My why is a whom and brings me empowerment,
               encouragement, and creativity with fresh ideas. Sometimes a why
               is a better way to connect with people who don't understand
               equity work. Stories are personal and intently non-divisive. When
               others can feel your why it can help reconnect people.”

Part Two – Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) Community Organization
C. Organize. “To achieve racial equity, local and regional governments must work
   both internally and externally with a network of partners - ranging from
   jurisdiction departments/bureaus/offices to nonprofit organizations,
   educational institutions, businesses, and philanthropic foundations. Most
   importantly, government must partner with, and center, racial equity work
   with communities of color.” With our new EDI Community Organization our
   staff are better aligned and resourced across the agency and there are new
   interconnections between staff, our partners, and the communities we serve.


   How we care for our staff
   At OHCS, we hold a firm belief that as our communities' shift, so too must we
   as a government entity. This shift requires us to move DEI work forward
   intentionally and diligently in our work, and OHCS believes in doing so, we



                                        32
cannot separate this from a specific and strategic focus on racial justice and its
direct impact on employee wellness and belonging. A focus on our collective
wellness with a strategic focus on the wellness and engagement of Black,
Indigenous, Latinx, Asian-American, Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander, and
other staff of color and culture, is critical to ensure that we truly operationalize
our racial justice work, both internally and externally.


Over the years, we have seen that staff of color and culture have experienced
triggers, trauma, micro and macro-aggressions in their work life. Many times,
they are expected, whether spoken or unspoken, to engage in racial justice and
equity work, but fear being marginalized because of engaging in the work
and/or being passionate about it. Staff of color and culture may not get
mentorship opportunities or tapped for promotions in the same way white
staff do; therefore, they may feel the need to look for opportunities to advance
outside of the agency.


It is with this understanding that we have set out, as an organization and
within our leadership teams, to deepen our learning and raise our
consciousness around race equity as the foundation of who we are, what we
do, and who we want to be to each other and in communities around the
state.




                                       33
Equity-focused support for staff
EDI Office staff offer ongoing support, education, and guidance to all
employees, and provide:
   • Consultation and support services for departments, sections, and teams.
   • Additional support and services on a one-to-one basis.
   • Initial and ongoing support for new employees and participate in our
      new employee orientation process.
   • Guidance and recommendations for beginning and/or sustaining
      employees’ own equity practice.


Communication and connection

On February 14, 2024, the EDI Café was established as a virtual space created
for staff as our consistent and sustainable form of maintaining our connection.
The EDI Café is a Microsoft SharePoint intranet page, and the architecture
includes:
   • A menu offering a selection of podcasts, interviews, books, and other
      resources focused on diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging tied to
      the vision and mission of OHCS.
   • Doorway to diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging (DEIB) learning
      and staff development, offering various training opportunities.
   • A calendar updated annually with our internal cultural events and
      campaigns to build community culture and encourage DEIB focused
      learning and development of OHCS employees.




                                      34
   • Highlights OHCS external partnerships that align OHCS priorities with
      being in service to enterprise-wide goals of other state agencies and
      community partners.
   • Provides employee resources to inform and educate employees on
      OHCS’s equity practices and other tools that build cultural competency
      and inter-relational skills.
   • Promotes the health and well-being of a primarily remote employee
      workforce though a variety of virtual engagement opportunities to be in
      community with teammates and colleagues that work across the
      agency’s enterprise.
   • Introduces and connects employees with our Office of Equity, Diversity,
      and Inclusion as a resource OHCS departments, sections, and teams
      while continuing to service the equity needs of all agency employees.


New EDI Community Organization
First, Gratitude. We have greatly appreciated the passion and commitment of
all the staff who have served as part of our equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI)
infrastructure which launched in the fall of 2020. Over the years we have
learned so much about what equity work can do and at what cost.


We’ve also said goodbye to cherished colleagues whose legacy lives on at
OHCS. We have grown into an impressive community of equity leaders and
influencers and as we begin a new year in 2024, it’s time to take what we’ve
learned, grow from it, and embrace a few changes contemplating a new EDI
community organization.



                                       35
The vision for our new EDI design – for the first time ever – invites the
communities we serve to be part of our new internal community organization
of employee led Inclusion Council (IC) and employee-based action teams:
   • Diversity, Inclusion, & Belonging (DIB)
   • Professional Equity, Growth, & Empowerment (PEGE)
   • Program & Policy for Racial Justice (PPRJ)


New Inclusion Council
The new Inclusion Council (IC) will be a part of the Global Diversity, Equity,
and Inclusion Benchmarks (GDEIB) FOUNDATION GROUP authorized to drive
the GDEIB strategy forward in partnership with OHCS Executive Team and the
Housing Stability Council. The IC will also be a strategic partner for the
Diversity, Inclusion, & Belonging Action Team for support and to ensure
transparency of agencywide initiatives and outcomes.
Focus areas for this group are:
   • Develop a strong rationale for DEI vision, mission, and strategy and align
      it to organizational goals.
   • Hold leaders accountable for implementing the organization’s DEI vision,
      setting goals, achieving results, and being role models.
   • Provide visible, dedicated support and structure with authority and
      budget to effectively implement DEI.




                                       36
Professional Equity, Growth, and Empowerment (PEGE) Action Team
The PEGE Action Team will be part of the GDEIB BRIDGING GROUP, authorized
to align and connect the GDEIB strategy in partnership with OHCS
Communications Team and staff who specialize in employee learning and
development.
Focus areas for this group are:
   • Ensure that all assessments, measurement, and research guide DEI
      decisions.
   • Make communication clear, simple to understand and a crucial force in
      achieving the organization’s DEI goals.
   • Educate all to achieve the level of DEI competence and confidence
      needed to create a diverse, equitable, and inclusive organization.
   • Connect the organization’s DEI and Sustainability initiatives to increase
      the effectiveness of both.


Diversity, Inclusion, and Belonging (DIB) Action Team
The DIB Action Team will be part of the GDEIB INTERNAL GROUP authorized to
align and connect people across the enterprise in partnership with OHCS
Human Resource Services Team and the Inclusion Council.
Focus areas for this group are:
   • Ensure that attraction, sourcing, and recruitment is done through the
      lens of DEI.
   • Ensure that DEI is integrated into professional development,
      performance management, advancement, and retention.




                                     37
   • Ensure that job design and classification are evaluated for bias and that
      compensation is equitable across key dimensions of diversity.
   • Achieve work-life integration, flexibility, and equitable benefits. Flexible
      work options are widely available and accessible.


Program & Policy for Racial Justice (PPRJ) Action Team
The PPRJ Action Team will be part of the GDEIB EXTERNAL GROUP authorized
to listen to and serve all communities in Oregon in partnership with OHCS
Strategic Action Leadership Team (SALT), OHCS Procurement Team, staff who
serve as equity advisors and staff who specialize in community engagement.
Focus areas for this group are:
   • Be proactive in working with community, public and private
      partnerships, government, society at large, and through philanthropy.
   • Embed DEI in services and products development to serve diverse
      customers and clients.
   • Integrate DEI into marketing and customer service.
   • Practice responsible and ethical sourcing. Develop and nurture
      underrepresented suppliers.


Community engagement
What does it look like? Community outreach is a central part of our OHCS DEI
Action Plan. The joint efforts of our EDI Office and PPRJ Action Team will
ensure we are in dialogue, collaboration, and co-creation with housing
partners, service provider organizations, advocacy groups, and Oregonians
most impacted by our policies and programs. Our PPRJ Action Team will



                                      38
include staff who specialize in community engagement and staff who serve as
division-wide equity advisors. Every step of the way we will commit to living
our values as we build trust and relationship with our partners.


Main priority
The main priority of the EDI Office in 2024 is to continue work on our OHCS DEI
Action Plan in collaboration with our equity leaders and in partnership with the
communities we serve. Our plan will document our continuous work to
advance equity and racial justice goals and it will include new initiatives to help
ensure our plan is comprehensive and aligns with our mission and values. The
creation of our new EDI Community Organization will 100% support our main
priority and better align outcomes with other enterprise-wide strategic
initiatives. This year (2024) we will begin to conduct an organizational
assessment using the GDEIB to further inform operational aspects of the OHCS
DEI Action Plan and the focus areas for our EDI Community Organization’s
Inclusion Council and Action Teams.


Global Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Benchmarks (GDEIB)
GDEIB is a global standard of 275 actionable benchmarks, developed by 112
experts, for organizations committed to diversity, equity, inclusion, and
belonging (DEIB). It is a tool to evaluate performance in achieving goals and
intended outcomes; a way to identify gaps and opportunities for
improvement. For OHCS, GDEIB will be our main method of accountability and
measure of DEIB outcomes for the next five years. Following is an overview of




                                      39
the GDEIB structure and our extended timeline for the assessment. To learn
more see the 2024 Edition of GDEIB Groups & Benchmarks – Attachment 1.


GDEIB assessment groups, purpose, and categories

                                    Groups


   Foundation            Internal              Bridging          External

                                    Purpose

    Drive the          Attract and         Align and          Listen to and
    Strategy          Retain People         Connect           Serve Society
                                 Categories

     Vision            Recruitment           Assessment        Community
   Leadership            Benefits          Communications       Services &
                                                                 Products
    Structure        Compensation              Learning         Marketing
                       Benefits &            Sustainability    Responsible
                        Flexibility                              Sourcing



Our GDEIB strategy timeline
                Begins Jan. 2024 (Qtr. 1) - Ends June 2025 (Qtr. 2)




                                      40
GDEIB Next Steps. In 2024, we will begin a series of survey and analysis to
establish a baseline of agency performance in year one of our five-year
horizon. Our GDEIB assessment will show us which benchmarks we have
achieved, so we can learn where we have room to grow as an organization. We
will begin by assessing ourselves and at the end of our timeline we will engage
with our external partners to share our analysis and invite their feedback as
well. Feedback is a gift when shared with good intentions and met with a
commitment from OHCS to learn and turn that feedback into positive action
and outcomes to better serve our communities.


EDI learning and development
Our steady approach to learning and development for staff involves the many
ways we build culture and community at the agency – by creating and
participating in cultural events, being a member of an affinity group, and
leaning into our equity practices. These experiences help create a supportive
environment that values relationships. In the process we become better
listeners, we learn to keep an open mind to new perspectives and because we
are learning from each other, we understand the value of lived experience. This
approach both humbles and empowers us to put our mission to work in service
to community.


The foundation for our learning and development includes several resources.
We provide memberships for education and networking opportunities,
affiliations to enhance our EDI training plans and tools, and professional
contracts for foundational and targeted training objectives. OHCS invests in



                                      41
   acclaimed leaders in the field of DEI, and we invest locally to support Oregon-
   based enterprise. In addition, we leverage resources provided by the state.

                   OHCS Foundation for EDI Learning and Development

    Memberships          Government Alliance on Race & Equity (GARE)
                         Partners in Diversity, Portland, OR
    Affiliations         Race Forward
                         Othering & Belonging Institute
    Contracts            Center for Equity & Inclusion (Portland, OR)
                         Paradigm REACH (D&I Training)
    Other Resources      Workday Learning
                         State of Oregon Library



D. Operationalize. “Racial inequities are not random—they have been created
   and sustained over time. Inequities will not disappear on their own. Tools must
   be used to change the policies, practices, and procedures that are perpetuating
   inequities and contribute to the development of new policies and programs.”
   OHCS resources two specific tools/processes to operationalize our equity and
   racial justice initiatives. These are described below along with our EDI
   Implementation Plan.


   Racial Equity Impact Statement (REIS). The REIS is a set of key questions staff
   will plan for and respond to during the process of developing legislative policy
   packages on behalf of the agency. Implementation of the REIS will begin this
   year (2024) resulting in a direct action to help ensure our proposals and agency
   request budget is responsive to our commitment to equity and racial justice.




                                          42
Racial Equity Analysis Tool (REAT). The REAT has been three years in the
making, and we are excited to scale implementation in 2024. This tool is a
variation of GARE’s racial equity toolkit that is inspired by OBI’s concept of
targeted universalism, and a variety of other sources and industry standards.
Fundamentally, REAT is a stable structure that lends itself to continued process
improvements and it is a pathway to engage and cocreate with community.
The REAT also has the aspirational goal of driving positive cultural change at
the agency because it allows us to shift our mindset and approach to our policy
and program work. When we invest time and resources on REAT
implementation – across the agency – we will achieve more equitable
outcomes for Oregonians and live up to our commitment to racial justice for
all. To learn more see the 2022 Edition of the OHCS Racial Equity Analysis
Toolkit – Attachment 2.


EDI Implementation Plan
This plan serves as our main operational component and includes our Focus
Areas, Objectives, Strategies, and Key Results (“OSKRs”). The plan can be
considered in two phases – concept and implementation. The concept phase
has been completed and this year (2024) we have transitioned into the
implementation or “ongoing” phase of our EDI Implementation Plan.


Concept Phase (complete) – Identify a set of four focus areas and challenges
associated with the work of OHCS’s EDI Office. Detail an objective for each
focus area. Working with objectives will prepare for the operational phase,
which is to attach strategies for each objective, based on desired key results.

                                    44
Implementation Phase (in progress) – Building from each set of objectives,
develop and propose strategies and key results for the four focus areas
identified during the concept phase. This year (2024) the EDI Office will
collaborate with equity leaders and influencers, and other agency staff to
finalize and approve at least three strategies and corresponding key results for
implementation.


OSKRs are intended to be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant,
and Time-Bound) and responsive to the results of GDEIB staff and community
surveys and will be implemented alongside the agency’s new strategic plan, in
development, and to be launched in 2024.


Based on our ongoing and current work to center racial justice and equity and
in effort to keep momentum moving while centering accountability, we believe
our four areas of operational focus represent the core work for our agency to
ensure we are truly living our values.

                                       49
Part Three – On the Horizon
Next Steps
In the coming months we will focus on these activities:
   • Follow our GDEIB timeline which includes cycles of staff survey, analysis,
      and community engagement.
   • We will recruit for and onboard new members to our EDI Community
      Organization. A well-sourced group of equity leaders and influencers is
      essential to the success and progress of our OHCS DEI Action Plan.
   • We will work to finalize, approve, and implement our objectives, strategies,
      and key results (OSKRs).
   • We will continue to work with professional consultants involved in
      developing our new OHCS strategic plan to help ensure cohesion between it
      and our OHCS DEI Action Plan.


Our EDI Implementation Plan will be updated on 9/30/24 with an eye on the
target completion date for all OSKRs (12/31/24). The next edition of our OHCS DEI
Action Plan will include these resources:
   • EDI Implementation Plan Report (9/30/24 update)
         o GDEIB Survey Report
         o REIS Summary Report
         o OSKR Progress Report
   • REAT User Guide


Closing Remarks. As our focus on equity and racial justice has deepened over the
last five years, we continue to see our staff lean into their own learning and



                                            50
curiosity and we anticipate this growth to continue because of the current
structure of support we are providing; our staff are eager to continue their
learning and consciousness raising.


As this desire deepens, we see our main challenge being that organizationally we
are unpracticed with clearly and confidently identifying and articulating our "why"
for centering race equity as a housing institution in every bit of our work. It does
not come as easily as articulating the need for housing affordability, safe and
stable housing, or the need to invest in homeownership opportunities.


While we have made great strides, there is more culture building and shifting
work to be done to build our organizational confidence. We consider our OHCS
DEI Action Plan as a prime driver to build this confidence and clarity over time.

Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks for Organizations Around the World




       THE
       FOUNDATION GROUP
           Drive the Strategy

       The three foundational categories build a strong DEI

       initiative. They are necessary for the effective

       operation of all other categories. The authors and

       Expert Panellists believe that it is dif cult to have an

       impactful DEI program without being at least a Level

       3 in all of the categories in the Foundation group.

Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks       Standards for Organizations Around the World




    Drive the Strategy
• Develop a strong rationale for DEI vision, mission, and strategy and
align it to organizational goals.
• Hold leaders accountable for implementing the organization’s
DEI vision, setting goals, achieving results, and being role models.
• Provide visible, dedicated support and structure with authority and
budget to effectively implement DEI.

                                              54
Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks              Standards for Organizations Around the World




     CATEGORY 1: VISION, STRATEGY,
     AND BUSINESS IMPACT
                  Action: Develop a strong rationale
                      for DEI vision and strategy
                 and align it to organizational goals.

     DEI is embedded in the values, mission, culture, and processes of the
     organization and plays an integral part in achieving organizational growth
     and success. There is a clear DEI vision and mission, and an explicit
     understanding of the business impact, which allows for the development
     of measurements to track progress towards meeting DEI goals. The
     organization’s strategy includes numerical goals for equitable representation.
     It also includes goals that create inclusion and belonging. The organization is
     a known leader in DEI and is frequently benchmarked by other organizations.




    ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                              55
        Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

      CATEGORY 2: LEADERSHIP
      AND ACCOUNTABILITY
                   Action: Hold leaders accountable for
      implementing the organization’s DEI vision, setting
          goals, achieving results, and being role models.

      Leaders and board members view the accomplishment of DEI goals
      and objectives as an important part of their management and oversight
      responsibilities. They publicly support internal and external diversity-
      related activities and are seen as DEI change agents. Leaders are held
      accountable for implementing the DEI strategy. They position DEI as a
      key component of their organization’s sustainability agenda and provide
      DEI coaching to those they manage, creating both a psychologically
      and physically safe workplace. Leadership development includes DEI
      competency building and this is evaluated when determining succession
      pools for leadership.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

      CATEGORY 3: DEI STRUCTURE
      AND IMPLEMENTATION
              Action: Provide visible, dedicated support
                and structure with authority and budget
                          to effectively implement DEI.

      As a re ection of the importance of DEI, there is a dedicated person with
      DEI expertise on the executive management team and at the Board level.
      These leaders interact with and have full access to other leaders and the
      rest of the board, and, if the organization’s size merits it, has a professional
      staff dedicated to DEI. In addition, there are teams, committees or networks
      within the organization that champion DEI initiatives. DEI professional staff
      have an adequate budget and resources to implement the strategy.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

       THE
       INTERNAL GROUP
            Attract & Retain People

       The four categories in the Internal Group focus

       primarily on strengthening policies, systems and

       processes to advance DEI. Many DEI programmes

       have emphasized categories in the Internal Group.

       One reason is that this group is part of the Human

       Resources function, which is where DEI has

       traditionally been placed on the organization

       chart. Consideration should be made to position

       DEI where it can effectively work with all other

       functions and departments to facilitate change.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               61
Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World




     Attract & Retain People
• Ensure that attraction, sourcing, and recruitment is done through
the lens of DEI.
• Ensure that DEI is integrated into professional development,
performance management, advancement, and retention.
• Ensure that job design and classi cation are evaluated for bias and
that compensation is equitable across key dimensions of diversity.
• Achieve work-life integration, exibility, and equitable bene ts.
Flexible work options are widely available and accessible.

           CATEGORY 4: RECRUITMENT
                              Action: Ensure that attraction,
                                   sourcing, and recruitment
                              is done through the lens of DEI.

           A conscious effort is made to attract applicants from diverse groups to
           achieve and maintain a workforce that is equitably representative across
           levels and functions. Advertising and recruitment are targeted to diverse
           communities. Diversity on interviewing panels is standard, and staf ng/
           hiring managers are educated on the impact of conscious and unconscious
           bias. The recruitment process is regularly reviewed to ensure it is equitable
           and fair. Hiring of underrepresented groups is roughly proportionate to
           their representation. There are clear DEI measures of success throughout
           the recruitment process.




          ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

      CATEGORY 5: ADVANCEMENT
      AND RETENTION
               Action: Ensure that DEI is integrated into
                      talent development, performance
             management, advancement, and retention.

      A conscious effort is made to maintain a workforce that is diverse across
      levels and functions. High-potential employees from underrepresented
      groups are provided with coaching, mentoring, and sponsorship opportunities
      to advance within the organization. The onboarding process results in all
      new employees feeling valued and included. Promotion and advancement
      of underrepresented groups is proportional to the representation of those
      groups in the organization. Performance reviews, involuntary and voluntary
      turnover of underrepresented groups are measured and analyzed using
      interviews, employee engagement surveys, and so forth.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

      CATEGORY 6: JOB DESIGN,
      CLASSIFICATION,
      AND COMPENSATION
       Action: Ensure that job design and classi cation
       are evaluated for bias and that compensation is
          equitable across key dimensions of diversity.

      The organization systematically reviews job requirements, classi cations,
      technology and compensation for potential bias and adverse impact. Job
      descriptions and requirements are understandable and do not include non-
      job-related factors. The organization designs work to accommodate—as
      much as possible—individual needs as well as the organization’s needs.
      Remuneration is based on job requirements and performance. The
      organization aspires to pay at minimum a living wage, in accordance with
      locally agreed upon standards. Compensation analyses are conducted
      regularly to ensure that biases based on race, ethnicity, age, gender,
      gender identity, gender expression, sexual orientation, function, and other
      potential areas of discrimination are signi cantly reduced.




    ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                              67
       Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

      CATEGORY 7: WORK-LIFE
      INTEGRATION, FLEXIBILITY,
      AND BENEFITS
      Action: Achieve work-life integration, exibility,
          and equitable bene ts. Flexible work options
                   are widely available and accessible.

      Work-life integration, exibility, and equitable bene ts are encouraged, actively
      promoted, recognized as enhancements of productivity, and are not career
      limiting.    To   this   end,     the   organization’s      performance        management
      focusses on output, contribution, and impact. Benefits and services that are
      specific to the diverse needs of employees are provided based on ongoing
      assessments of employee needs. Some examples are: subsidized dependent-
      care, parental leave, extended family consideration, eldercare, emergency
      care, fitness programs, and paid leave. Accommodations for religious
      practices, persons with disabilities, and others are achieved with care
      and consideration and go beyond legal requirements. Organizations and
      their leaders prioritize psychological safety, security, and wellness within their
      employees’ work environment.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               69
        Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

       THE
       BRIDGING GROUP
           Align & Connect

       The four categories in this group provide critical

       linkages that bridge foundational work with the

       internal and external focus of DEI in the organization.

       It would be dif cult for any of the benchmarks in

       the other Groups to be achieved without effective

       work in the Bridging Group.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               71
Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World




     Align & Connect
• Ensure that all assessments, measurement, and research guide
DEI decisions.
• Make communication clear, simple to understand, and a
crucial force in achieving the organization’s DEI goals.
• Educate all to achieve the level of DEI competence and confidence
needed to create a diverse, equitable, and inclusive organization.
• Connect the organization’s DEI and Sustainability initiatives to
increase the effectiveness of both.

Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World




      CATEGORY 8: ASSESSMENT,
      MEASUREMENT, AND RESEARCH
       Action: Ensure that assessments, measurement,
              and research guide DEI decisions.

      DEI measures are explicitly linked to strategy and have an impact on
      leaders at all levels, including their performance appraisals, recognition, and
      rewards. The organization has a dashboard or scorecard for reputational
      risk assessment on DEI issues.

      The views of stakeholders are an important factor in measuring DEI and
      sustainability performance for both the organization and individuals. The
      measurements include stakeholder opinions and experiences, and a mix
      of quantitative and qualitative measures. Information on all aspects of DEI
      is gathered and evaluated using practices such as 360-feedback and/or
      multifaceted, culturally sensitive, and linguistically appropriate feedback,
      focus groups, opinion and engagement surveys, and organizational audits.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               73
         Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

 CATEGORY 8: ASSESSMENT, MEASUREMENT, AND RESEARCH

      CATEGORY 9:
      DEI COMMUNICATIONS
        Action: Make communication clear, simple to
       understand, and a crucial force in achieving the
                  organization’s DEI goals.

      Communications professionals are educated about DEI and its link with
      sustainability. All internal and external communication is fully accessible
      and available in multiple formats and, if applicable, in locally spoken
      languages. The organization has made and communicated a decision on the
      use of inclusive language to account for various diversity dimensions. The
      communication sent out both internally and externally does not prejudice
      or harm any group intentionally and where this occurs, it is corrected quickly
      and clearly. DEI topics are easy and quick to                  nd on the organization’s
      websites and social media platforms. DEI communication is frequent,
      ongoing, innovative, and contributes to an enhanced reputation for the
      organization. Progress on reaching DEI vision and goals is reported to the
      public regularly.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

       Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

      CATEGORY 10: DEI
      LEARNING AND DEVELOPMENT
         Action: Educate all to achieve a level of DEI
        competence and con dence needed to create a
        diverse, equitable, and inclusive organization.

      Leaders and employees throughout the organization receive DEI training
      speci c to their job and level geared to achieving the organization’s goals.
      Learning and education is also offered to other stakeholders such as vendors,
      and the public at large. A DEI lens is used to integrate all learning and
      education programs. The organization uses blended DEI learning and
      continuously updates and customizes this to changing stakeholder needs.
      Programs may focus on general DEI and on speci c issues such as systemic
      and structural racism, sexism, ageism, ableism, white supremacy,
      internalized oppression, classism, heterosexism, prejudice, discrimination
      (including harassment and bullying), privilege, micro-aggressions, and
      conscious and unconscious bias, all of which are addressed with sensitivity,
      conviction, and compassion, and in ways that enhance inclusion and equity.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

        Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

    CATEGORY 11: CONNECTING
    DEI AND SUSTAINABILITY
              Action: Connect the organization’s DEI and
               Sustainability initiatives to increase the
                        effectiveness of both.

    The organization connects and aligns DEI and sustainability initiatives. There
    is a clear link between the organization’s sustainability strategy (based on ESG
    - environmental, social, and governance – dimensions or factors) and the UN’s
    Sustainable Development Goals. The strategies for each initiative support the
    other, and opportunities for collaboration make both initiatives stronger. Leaders
    and practitioners in sustainability participate in the DEI initiative and vice versa.
    Both work with diverse stakeholders and report progress to the public.


   THE SUSTAINABILITY DEFINITION FOR THE GDEIB IS:
   Sustainability is the process of simultaneously pursuing:
                        • societal equity • workplace inclusion
             • economic prosperity & • long term environmental health.
   Additional de nitions and key factors of Sustainability:
   For the purposes of the GDEIB, we view the values and desired outcomes of DEI work
   (See GDEIB page 2, The Ultimate Goals of DEI) as similar to the goals of Sustainability.
     1. The de nition of Sustainability adopted by the United Nations is “Meeting the
        needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations
        to meet their own needs.” To date, 193 world leaders agreed to achieve the
        United Nations 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDG’s) by 2030 as the
        framework to achieve sustainability. The 5 Ps that shape the SDGs are People,
        Planet, Prosperity, Peace, and Partnership.




     2. Another de nition comes from an African conference delegate: “Enough—
        for all—forever.”
     3. Another related term used by some corporations and the investor community
        in their advocacy for Sustainability is ESG – Environmental, Social, and
        Corporate Governance. This refers to the three central factors in measuring
        sustainability and social impact.
   These factors embrace the UN concept of “leave no one behind” and invite us to
   consider this meaning of success: “I succeed when you succeed.”


     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               79
       Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

       THE
       EXTERNAL GROUP
           Listen to & Serve Society

       The four categories in this group relate to how

       the organization offers its services and products

       to and interacts with its customers, clients, com-

       munities, and other stakeholders. The External

       Group is critically important because it is through

       an emphasis on these categories that the most

       direct results of the organization's DEI rationale/

       business impact will be shown.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               81
Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World




     Listen to & Serve Society
• Be proactive in working with community, public and private
partnerships, government, society at large, and through philanthropy.
• Embed DEI in services and products development to serve diverse
customers and clients.
• Integrate DEI into marketing and customer service.
• Practice responsible and ethical sourcing. Develop and nurture
underrepresented suppliers.

      CATEGORY 12: COMMUNITY,
      GOVERNMENT RELATIONS,
      AND PHILANTHROPY
       Action: Be proactive in working with community,
        public and private partnerships, government,
          society at large, and through philanthropy.

      The organization is a recognized leader for supporting and advocating for
      DEI interests in communities and society, and in working with government.
      The organization is socially responsible and leverages efforts with industry
      or sector peers. It is generous in partnering with or supporting other
      organizations in their DEI initiatives to advance DEI in the community.

      Where appropriate, special efforts are made to acknowledge indigenous
      communities and to respect their territory and adhere to legal and cultural
      requirements when entering their space. Employees are encouraged to
      participate in and support various community projects, share DEI learning
      from such activities, and reinforce the organization’s commitment to DEI.
      These efforts are explicitly linked to the organization’s sustainability strategy.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

      CATEGORY 13: SERVICES AND
      PRODUCTS DEVELOPMENT
          Action: Embed DEI in services and products
       development to serve diverse customers and clients.

      DEI considerations are integrated into the services and products development
      cycle to leverage the diversity of the communities they serve. The process
      contributes to social and economic progress and eliminates or reduces
      inequities. Service or product development teams are diverse and include
      customers, stakeholders, and community representatives. Recognition is
      also given to the value of DEI in innovation, and the organization consistently
      leverages the diversity of cultures, lived experiences, perspectives, and
      thought for product and service improvement.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

          Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

      CATEGORY 14: MARKETING AND
      CUSTOMER SERVICE
                  Action: Integrate DEI into marketing
                         and customer service.

      Marketing and customer service strategies meet the needs of diverse groups.
      Sophisticated market analysis techniques are deployed on an ongoing basis
      to understand the organization’s diverse customer and stakeholder base. The
      organization uses DEI relevant marketing and customer service approaches
      within and across countries, regions, cultures, generations, and languages.

      Marketing and advertising strategies are inclusive and challenge stereotypes.
      While outside DEI expertise may sometimes be sought, the organization
      leverages the expertise of its diverse staff. Accessibility and cultural
      sensitivity are incorporated into the process of design and development of all
      marketing materials and customer service strategies.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.

      CATEGORY 15:
      RESPONSIBLE SOURCING
        Action: Practice responsible and ethical sourcing.
        Develop and nurture underrepresented suppliers.

      Responsible and ethical sourcing refers to a commitment to purchase a
      signi cant percentage of goods and services from organizations committed
      to high standards in DEI, sustainability, ethical behaviour, and Fair Trade.

      Further, the organization supports its current and potential suppliers in
      maintaining high standards through education and systems that enable
      them to compete equitably with other suppliers—the ultimate goal being
      to create sustainable, successful, and prosperous societies.




     ©2024 Nene Molefl, Julie O’Mara, Alan Richter. All Rights Reserved. Permission to use required.
                                               89
          Global Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Benchmarks Standards for Organizations Around the World

CATEGORY 15: RESPONSIBLE SOURCING
Note: This category is impacted by legal regulations in many countries. In this category, underrepresented groups may include Fair Trade,
Direct Trade, or other similar suppliers.
