Housing Stabilization Executive Summary

Program Contact:       Liz Weber, Housing Stabilization Assistant Director




Program Overview
Housing insecurity and homelessness continue to pose severe challenges for Oregon across multiple sectors and all parts of the state.
Cities, large and small, continue to grapple with the challenges of providing enough shelter, services, and supports for people living
outside. Both rural and urban areas of the state struggle to ensure families have heat and electricity, pay their rent and utility bills, and
have warm, weatherized homes to live in during the increasingly harsh seasons Oregon has experienced in recent years. Investing in
housing and homelessness prevention supports has multiple benefits for population health as well. For instance, through the lens of social
determinants of health, homelessness is a key driver of poor health, while homelessness itself can result from accumulated adverse social


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF01
                                                                     187
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
and economic conditions. Housing instability encompasses acceptable housing conditions, such as the lack of a clean environment and
adequate heating and cooling, inadequate safety and structural soundness, exposure to allergens and pests, homelessness, living on the
streets or in a shelter, rent and/or energy burden, and food insecurity.
The 2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget seeks to build upon the impactful and transformative work of the last few years. This
must include changing the systems that helped create and exacerbate disproportionate impacts on Black, Indigenous, Latinx and other
communities of color. System change will take time and many biennia, and OHCS is committed to this work. In order to seize this
moment, the agency will seek to create new systems that are regenerative and healing and not solely premised on growth. We will
emphasize collecting and analyzing program data, designing consistent system-wide outcomes, regularly evaluating the effectiveness of
programs in advancing equity and racial justice, and our ongoing commitment to authentic, peer-led community engagement—as we
often are reminded, “nothing for us, without us.” These priorities are reflected in policy package requests and will continue in the agency’s
ongoing work connected to the implementation of Governor Kotek’s Executive Order 24-02 and 24-03, which seek to respond to the rise
in unsheltered homelessness through swift, decisive actions as well as establishing critical infrastructure across the enterprise focused on
coordinating homeless services.
Responding to and preventing homelessness requires a range of strategies to meet the needs of Oregonians through an individualized,
equity-based practice. One agency is not able to be everything for everyone. To that end, the Housing Stabilization Division’s packages
reflect a system of care. This approach uses interagency collaboration, individualized programming, racially equitable communications,
race-explicit strategies, and community-based service provisions. The programs we administer provide critical services to the lowest-
income Oregonians by addressing housing instability and helping them access safe, stable, and affordable housing options and services.
The programs address many aspects of the needs of those we serve: affordable housing, energy bill payment assistance, weatherization
assistance, and connections to other services such as health care, education, and nutritious food, to help Oregonians build financial
independence and find pathways out of poverty. The division administers asset-building programs that help Oregonians save for financial
goals while also learning skills to stabilize their households. It also distributes federal and state resources to the state’s community action
network, other key community-based organizations, local government entities, and continuums of care to ensure a system of care for
each unique community across Oregon.
Program Funding Request
The Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) funding for 2025-27 reflects the unprecedented challenges of:
   •   Continuing to invest in an equitable response that prioritizes eliminating racial disparities and other system-wide
       inequities.
   •   Increasing access to asset-building and other modes of ensuring wealth-building opportunities are available to Oregonians
       with lower incomes.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                         107BF01
                                                                      188
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
   •   Investing in the pre-existing and now exacerbated humanitarian crisis of homelessness that is visible across the streets of
       Oregon and is also at risk of worsening without concerted efforts to prevent involuntary displacement through eviction.
   •   Investing in strategies to stop the inflow of families and individuals into homelessness.
   •   Facing the challenges of rapid increases in unsheltered homelessness in our State by continuing to support the Executive
       Order 23-02 investments to increase year-round shelter beds and rehouse more households experiencing homelessness.




Program Description
Housing Stabilization programs include three major program areas: Homeless Services, Housing Retention, and Energy Services.
Eligibility varies depending on the funding source, but all programs serve low- and very-low-income Oregonians.
Homeless Services programs are delivered statewide by Oregon’s community action network and their partner agencies, other networks
of culturally specific organizations, community-based organizations that are culturally responsive, and tribal entities. During the 2023-
2025 Biennium, OHCS also expanded to begin working with Continuums of Care (CoC) across the State under Governor Kotek’s
Executive Order 23-02 and Executive Order 24-02. Program services reduce and prevent homelessness, reduce poverty, and increase
economic prosperity. Assistance includes outreach, shelter, rapid access to permanent housing, landlord incentives to house low-income
persons, case management and other supportive services, direct financial and rental assistance, linkage to other basic needs programs,
and program data collection and evaluation.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF01
                                                                   189
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Housing Retention programs deliver eviction prevention funding, including specific set-asides for culturally specific organizations, youth
and family focused stabilization programs, legal and tenant protection programs, and asset building through Individual Development
Accounts. Programs are delivered by community-based organizations, culturally responsive organizations, and community action
agencies.
Energy Services programs are also delivered by community action agencies. Clients of these programs are households earning either 60
percent or less of state median income or 200 percent or less of the federal poverty level, with priority given to seniors (60 years of age
and older), people with disabilities, and households with children. The programs provide utility bill payment assistance, prevent
disconnection, or restore home energy services, home health and safety improvements, heating system repair and replacement, energy
conservation services, and base load measures (including replacement of inefficient appliances and lighting). In 2021-23, assistance with
household water bills was provided by federal COVID response legislation.
Program Justification
OHCS’ Housing Stabilization Programs provide services that assist vulnerable households to stabilize, moving as many as possible
toward greater and sustainable self-sufficiency. These programs are designed to prevent and end homelessness, reduce the housing
burden by lowering rents, help households maintain utility services, address home health and safety issues, and reduce high home energy
costs for low-income Oregonians.

Funding Streams


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF01
                                                                   192
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Housing Stabilization Programs are funded by General Fund, Other Funds, and Federal Funds. The division receives administration
charges and interest earning from the various programs. Other funds for the Homeless Services Programs are derived from document
recoding fees that are transferred in from Department of Revenue. Oregon Health Authority transfers funds supporting 1115 Medicaid
Waiver program. Energy Services programs receives revenue from the public purpose charges from PGE and PAC customers. The
division receives several federal grants to support its programs such as Weatherization grants, Community Services Block Grants and
Emergency Shelter Grant programs.


Program Unit Narrative
Housing Stabilization Division (HSD) Programs Description
By addressing the economic and life stability of Oregonians, HSD programs assist in preventing and ending homelessness and ensure
that Oregonians maintain access to affordable housing that meets their basic needs. National studies indicate that while a small portion
of the homeless population needs intensive, long-term services, most people experiencing homelessness or who are at risk of
homelessness can retain housing with rent assistance and other housing stabilization services, including employment assistance, and
financial assistance programs.
Improving housing stability also impacts economic, safety, and education outcomes. Chronically homeless Oregonians who are provided
permanent, supportive housing services see an average 50 percent rise in earned income, and a 40 percent rise in employment. Adults
experiencing homelessness also face significantly higher rates of chronic illness, emotional distress, disability, and premature death than
the general population. Homelessness is also linked to poor physical health in children including low birth weight, malnutrition, exposure
to environmental toxins, and chronic illness. Children experiencing homelessness are also more likely to be mobile and/or experience
barriers to attending school, thereby impacting educational trajectories and access to viable and stable employment. The impacts of being
even temporarily homeless are significant.
HSD programs include homeless assistance, rental assistance, individual development accounts, low-income utility bill payment
assistance, and weatherization assistance programs. These programs are delivered statewide by Oregon’s community action network and
their partner agencies. These providers create an extensive network that ensures coordination and leveraging of services for at-risk
Oregonians.
Homeless Assistance Programs work to prevent and end homelessness. Homeless services are delivered through local providers to
enable households that are homeless or at risk of homelessness to regain or maintain stable housing. Through a variety of state and
federal funding sources, qualified Oregonians have access to services including emergency shelter, rapid re-housing, transitional or
permanent supportive housing, rental assistance, case management, and other assistance needed to stabilize housing. Services also assist

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF01
                                                                    193
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
low-income households to attain economic self-sufficiency and meet their basic needs. OHCS continues to improve the homeless
management information system’s ability to report on outcomes and services that are provided to clients through concerted investments
in data capacity, systems architecture, and data governance with continuums of care.
Oregon Rehousing Initiative:
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI) is a statewide rehousing program to rehouse additional households experiencing homelessness.
Funds are administered from OHCS to Multi-Agency Coordination (MAC) groups in Executive Area regions and Local Planning Groups
(LPGs) throughout the Balance of State. Additionally, ORI includes a 25% set aside for Culturally Responsive Organizations. Rehousing
programs are delivered through local providers to enable households that are homeless to regain stable housing. Household eligibility
includes the following categories of homelessness: Category 1: Literally Homeless, Category 3: Homeless Under Other Federal Statues,
Category 4: Fleeing/Attempting to Flee Domestic Violence, and Category 6: Unsheltered Homelessness. ORI programs include the use
of progressive engagement to tailor services being offered to the individual needs of each household served. While some households may
require the maximum amount of rental assistance allowable (up to 24 months), others may need shorter durations in order to stabilize.
OHCS recognizes the critical importance of services such as housing navigation, housing counseling, intensive case management,
retention supports, and other wraparound services to support the housing stability of a highly vulnerable population. These services are
all allowable through ORI programming. The ORI program anticipates rehousing over 1,000 households by June 30, 2025.
Long Term Rental Assistance Program:
OHCS was tasked with creating a Long-Term Rent Assistance (LTRA) Program to ensure that households rehoused through EO 23-02,
and HB 5019 rehousing investments have continued services and long-term rental assistance after Jan. 10, 2024, if needed.
The LTRA program will help with the cost of rent to increase housing access and long-term housing stability for people exiting
homelessness. This type of assistance has proven to be a key strategy to support housing stability and, for some people, is vital to their
ability to stay housed. Investing in LTRA resources is an avenue for fewer people living in shelters, motels, on the street, or doubled up
in unstable arrangements. Fewer families, seniors, and people with disabilities would have to choose between paying the rent and buying
needed medicine or food.
$39.7 million was appropriated for the 2023-2025 Biennium to OHCS for this program. OHCS funding allocation will utilize MAC
Regions’ EO 23-02 rehousing accomplishments as well as the identified need for ongoing assistance from those that were rehoused under
the EO 23-02.
Emergency Order Shelter Capacity Expansion helps sustain investments made under HB5019 EO 23-02 for continuing the operational
costs of managing shelters that were created using HB5019 resources. Under EO 23-02, Multi Agency Coordination (MAC) groups



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF01
                                                                   194
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
created 1,047 new shelter beds by January 10th, 2024, investing approximately $20 million in HB 5019 funds- excluding administration
costs. In the Balance of State, Local Planning Groups were allocated $7,913,156 to create 347 new shelter beds by June 30th, 2025.
To sustain efforts made under EO 23-02 for MAC groups in the designated EO region, of the $160.3 million in funding from Senate Bill
5511 to sustain the efforts funded by HB 5019, $45.8 million from SB 5511 was allocated to MACs to sustain HB 5019 shelters. An
additional 1.5 million was allocated to two Balance of State Opt in MAC groups, in total, sustaining over 1100 beds until June 2025.
Under EO 23-02, and programmatic outcomes established under HB 5019 for the Balance of State, over 1300 new shelter beds have been
created across the state. Sustaining these beds and investment in emergency shelter will allow regions to continue to focus on communities
experiencing unsheltered homelessness.
Rental Assistance Programs are available to Oregonians earning at or below 80 percent of area median income (AMI). These programs
offer rental assistance and refundable security deposits paid directly to property owners on the tenants’ behalf. All households receiving
rental assistance must participate in self-sufficiency planning to receive benefits. The Housing Choice Landlord Guarantee Program is
designed to provide financial assistance to landlords to mitigate damages caused by tenants as a result of their occupancy under the HUD
Housing Choice Voucher Program, also known as Section 8.
Rental Assistance for Youth and Families are available to youth and families with children who are interacting with the following
systems: foster care, corrections, behavioral health or actively facing homelessness or housing instability. These programs offer rental
assistance, move-in costs, case management services and other supportive services as determined by the case manager in conjunction
with the family/individual being served.
Eviction Prevention programs are available to households that are at or below 80 percent AMI. These programs exist to provide rent
and utility assistance, moving costs, mediation services, rapid rent assistance for eviction prevention, and a variety of other services to
help families retain their homes.
The Individual Development Account program is available to households with incomes equal to or less than 80 percent of median
income or 200 percent of federal poverty guidelines. Eligible participants and designated fiduciary organizations jointly develop personal
development plans to support progress toward participant wealth building goals, with resources to provide account holders with financial
and asset training, counseling, career or business planning, and other services. Participant-generated funds are matched with program
funds to create a savings investment toward asset goals such as home ownership, small business start-up and growth, education, rent
assistance, vehicles, and adaptive technologies. The program is funded through an Oregon tax credit that has a cap of $7.5 million per
year. OHCS provides oversight of the contract administrator of the program and the fiduciary organizations that provide program services
but does not receive tax credit revenues or make payments to participants.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF01
                                                                    195
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
The Low-Income Household Water Assistance (LIHWA) program is a temporary, federally funded water assistance program for
lower income households at or below 60% of state median income. Oregon’s LIHWA program provides eligible households, who have
high water burden, with on-time financial assistance paid directly to their water and sewer utilities to cover arrearages and current
charges.
Energy Assistance Programs help ensure that low-income households can maintain heat in their homes during winter months. In
warmer regions of the state, energy assistance can also help Oregonians keep cool during life-threatening heat waves. These services are
especially critical among vulnerable households, such as the elderly, disabled, and families with young children, who are particularly
sensitive to variations in temperature.
   •   In 2021, approximately 214,000 of Oregon’s households lived with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty
       level. Oregon households with incomes below 50 percent of the federal poverty level paid 23 percent of their annual incomes
       simply for their home energy bills. A short-term lack of hot water, refrigeration, and cooking capacity can result in lost wages,
       evictions, and in some cases, homelessness.
   •   High energy burden and deferred home maintenance increase the likelihood that low-income families will experience unhealthy
       housing conditions, including lack of heat, water leaks, mold, and lead paint.
The impacts of energy assistance go well beyond staying warm or cool. Households that receive energy assistance are less likely to
accumulate excessive arrearages or experience utility disconnection, thereby reducing the risk of eviction or homelessness. Studies from
the Boston Medical Center also indicate that many low-income households are making dangerous trade-offs between food and energy,
resulting in increased rates of food insecurity, lower weight, and decreased growth rates among children, particularly within families of
color. Energy assistance has a proven protective effect against this “heat or eat” phenomenon, with recipients less likely to demonstrate
food insecurity and slowed physical growth. The living environment itself is also a significant health driver.
Low Income Weatherization Programs provide a unique opportunity to address household health and safety. When weatherization
crews visit a home to complete energy upgrades and repairs, they also perform important health and safety assessments. For example,
lead safe work practices are utilized to prevent the spread of lead dust. All homes are visually inspected for mold. Ventilation systems
are upgraded to ensure adequate airflow, improving indoor air quality and all homes receive carbon monoxide alarms. Additionally,
repair and replacement of dysfunctional heating systems prevent health and safety risks associated with combustion by-products, lack of
heat, and fire hazards. Air conditioning and heat pumps may be installed to ensure a home is left climate ready. Research indicates that
every dollar invested in weatherization programs yields a return of four and a half dollars, including avoided costs associated with
uninsured medical expenses and lost work.
As a result of weatherizing a home, the energy costs of the low-income Oregonians are also reduced. National research indicates that
low-income households that receive weatherization services experience energy savings of $283 or more per year. These savings make it

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF01
                                                                   196
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
easier for families to pay for other necessities such as rent, medical care and food, subsequently increasing their likelihood of maintaining
stable housing.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF01
                                                                     197
                                                      BUDGET NARRATIVE
Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level.
Phase-Outs are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27
biennium.

Policy Package 090 Analyst Adjustments
This package reduces General Fund by $0.4 million and reduces Other Funds expenditure limitation by $0.3 million by
increasing vacancy savings by two percent, eliminating Inflation on certain services and supplies accounts, and eliminating
Housing Choice Landlord Guarantee funds.

Policy Package #501 Maintaining Shelters
Package Description

Policy Package (PP) #501 provides a crucial influx of funding in the amount of $217.9M to support a statewide network of emergency
shelter projects, ensuring that state-funded shelter capacity across Oregon during the 25-27 biennium maintains at least the current
number of beds created and/or supported during the 23-25 biennium. OHCS currently estimates that 4,884 beds will be supported with
state resources during the 23-25 Biennium. Funding will be targeted to support shelter operations, housing-focused supportive activities,
and necessary administrative costs. PP #501 also provides $375k to conduct a statewide shelter study. These investments represent a
continued commitment from OHCS to collaborate with partners in building upon a homelessness response system that provides
impactful interventions and supports Oregonians toward obtaining stable housing.

Purpose

This package represents the continuation and consolidation of multiple distinct funding sources from the 23-25 biennium. These funding
sources included $24.1M from Senate Bill (SB) 5511 and $65M from SB1530, which provided funding for the continued operations,
services, and administration of emergency shelters, Project Turnkey sites and Navigation Centers. The package also continues the work
began under Executive Order 23-02 and 24-02 (EO) funding that additionally funded the creation of new shelter beds and included
housing-focused services. Throughout June 2024, OHCS performed an analysis to examine cost projections for those shelter providers
who received any funding from the above sources, developing an estimate of the costs to sustain these essential shelter projects, some
of which opened their doors for the first time during the 23-25 biennium, through the 25-27 biennium. OHCS conducted outreach to
the statewide network of shelter providers, including Multi-Agency Coordination (MAC) Group regions, the Balance of State (BoS)
Local Planning Groups (LPGs), the Community Action Agencies (CAAs), and many community-based organizations to perform this
analysis. The response rate for the information gathering phase was 90%. For regions that did not provide sufficient responses, OHCS
used a combination of averages, historical data and the following assumptions to predict maintenance of shelter bed costs for 25-27:

   • Housing-focused activities were added in at the same rate for shelters, whether EO-funded or otherwise
   • Allocations for shelters in each region were based on the average costs observed throughout the state
   • Accordingly, estimates were also made for programs that had not yet become operational, and
   • A standard inflation rate of 4.2% for services and materials and 6.8% for staffing was determined appropriate, given the wide
      variation in inflation related figures from our partners


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                   214
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
Regions have also identified the potential impact of not receiving funding in the 25-27 biennium. Without sustained OHCS funding,
numerous shelters in rural and urban areas would be forced to close. Preliminary estimates show that Oregon could lose upwards of
1300 shelter beds almost immediately without sustained funding in the new Biennium, but the sustained risk for other shelters closing
could prove to be much higher over time. Several regions in the Balance of State would lose their only emergency shelters, including
many counties in Eastern Oregon, where people experiencing unsheltered homelessness would be exposed to hazardous winter
conditions and high heat and smoke exposure due to wildfires, which can lead to chronic medical conditions or even death. Even small
reductions in funding could lead to closure of shelters that serve our state’s most vulnerable populations and their families. These regions
also shared that supportive and wraparound services, at minimum, would be reduced and in some cases, eliminated if funding is not
sustained. These regions identified that the supportive wrap-around services were critical for their ability to help households locate
suitable housing and move out of the shelter, allowing the shelter bed to serve a new member of the community in need.

The June 2024 cost analysis identified a total estimated funding need of $217.5M to continue operating the 4,884 shelter beds supported
with OHCS funds in the 23-25 biennium; however, OHCS will conduct additional process steps prior to deployment of funds under this
Policy Package to ensure that funds are distributed based on the most accurate and up-to-date information. There are a variety of non-
inflation factors also driving the need for ongoing shelter funding dedicated at the state level: many regions and shelters have experienced
significant funding reductions throughout the 23-25 biennium, as major resources originating during the COVID-19 pandemic (CARES,
ARPA, etc.) were phasing out. OHCS has heard from local governments across the state that they invested heavily in shelter systems
during the pandemic period and do not have adequate resources to continue to sustain the shelters. This shelter funding package provides
crucial funding to bridge these massive shelter operations budget gaps.

Homelessness imposes high costs on families and individuals. It is harder to find and keep a job, treat or manage medical and/or
behavioral health conditions, and learn in school when experiencing homelessness. These challenges are particularly acute for children
and veterans who are experiencing homelessness. Oregon has the highest rate of families experiencing unsheltered homelessness in the
nation according to the 2023 Point in Time count, with 59% of families or 2,288 households experiencing homelessness were found to
be unsheltered. People experiencing homelessness are also vulnerable to violence and abuse, especially women and members of the
LGBTQ+ community. Partnerships and coordination across several intersecting economic, social, and health systems are critical to
effectively address homelessness. OHCS is focusing an intentional commitment towards ensuring shelters remain open and operational
in the 25-27 biennium, leveraging the 23-25 investments into a sustainable sheltering system that addresses Oregonians’ basic need for
a roof over their heads, a safe place to sleep, and somewhere they can access services to move out of shelters and into permanent housing.
OHCS will use these funds to provide people with immediate access to shelter and crisis services with low barriers to entry when



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                    215
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
homelessness occurs. Funds will also be used to quickly connect people experiencing homelessness to housing assistance and services
tailored to their unique needs and strengths to help them achieve and maintain stable housing.

This effort aligns with OHCS and the Governor’s priorities around ensuring continued access to shelters for those in need and providing
the services needed to obtain permanent housing. Additionally, this funding package will be informed by the priorities and strategies
that emerge from the newly formed Shelter Workgroup, convened by the Governor’s Office and Representative Pam Marsh (Southern
Jackson County). This workgroup held its first meeting on July 23rd, 2024, and represents a coalition of state, city, and county
government agency representatives, legislators, direct service providers and shelter operators, culturally specific organizations,
Community Action Agencies, and Continuums of Care. This collaboration will work toward defining and implementing a coherent,
effective, and sustainable structure that will support the existing shelter system over the long term.

PP #501 also provides funding for a statewide shelter study that will be conducted via partnership with Portland State University’s
Homelessness Research & Action Collaborative (HRAC). OHCS commissioned a 2019 Statewide Shelter Study that helped to inform
the shelter system’s needs over the past 5 years and seeks to take stock of our progress and the needs for the system ahead. This study
will formally begin in August 2025 and is expected to be completed within 18 months (approx. January 2027). One of the main areas of
focus of this project will be to analyze the unmet need for shelter and transitional housing by geographical region and subpopulation.
OHCS will also seek to utilize mixed methods approach to incorporate qualitative feedback and best practices analysis into the results;
to truly understand the way the shelter system operates across the State from the ground up. When complete, this report will also serve
as a useful tool in determining long-term funding and policy strategy for the statewide shelter network.

How Achieved
This shelter funding package will achieve the desired outcomes by:
   •   Providing necessary funding to ensure that previously funded shelters (Project Turnkey sites, Navigation Centers, EO-funded
       shelters, and other OHCS-funded shelters) can continue operating at full capacity, and that the statewide shelter network growth
       experienced in the 23-25 Biennium can be sustained.
   •   Aligning these funds with current, existing programs, minimizing the need to develop new or different programs. This will ensure
       that the investment focuses on strengthening a system in which readily accessible shelter beds will stay online and are available
       for those experiencing a housing crisis, leveraging previous investments into a sustainable system.
   •   Streamlining reporting and data collection so that OHCS maintains a comprehensive understanding of the shelter system,
       including, but not limited to: # of available and utilized state-funded beds in each community over time, and shelter outcomes


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  216
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
       focused on exit destinations to permanent housing and other service interventions within the continuum of the homelessness
       response system.
   •   Providing well-documented guidance that includes, but is not limited to: standards on Confidentiality, Grievance and Appeal,
       Nondiscrimination, Limited English Proficiency, Lived Experience-informed Program Improvement, and Conflict of Interest.
       This will ensure that all grantees have policies that comply with basic state requirements that ensure the equitable and fair
       delivery of services.
   •   Ensuring through clear program guidance and monitoring follow-up that shelters meet low barrier standards and basic habitability
       standards for the safe and sanitary provision of shelter beds that meet guests’ needs and provide a safer alternative than living
       on the street.
Operations funding will ensure that shelters have sufficient funding for: facility lease/rent payments, utilities, security and janitorial
expenses, facility staffing to manage day-to-day operations, minor repairs and maintenance, furnishings and appliances, food and
services for congregate meal delivery, in-shelter childcare when needed, and pet care/supplies for guests’ pets and/or service animals to
encourage guest participation and reduce barriers to entry.
Funding for housing-focused activities will ensure that shelters are able to support guests with connection to stabilizing resources.
Housing-focused activities are different than the critical services provided in formal rehousing programs such as Rapid Rehousing or
Long-Term Rental Assistance programs in that they are focused on the most immediate needs shelter residents have rather than ongoing
months of assistance after the initial engagement. Many shelter residents just need a small intervention to connect them back to housing,
employment or other support systems and these housing-focused services enable those connections. In many communities, these services
will be deployed alongside one another as a cohesive strategy to match the right intervention with the right households. These activities,
covering both service delivery and direct client financial assistance components, include:
   •   Rental housing costs such as rent payments (inclusive of first month, last month, pet rent/deposit, manufactured home rental
       space “lot rent” if used for primary housing, and forward rent if accepted by landlord as incentive), security deposit, and
       application fees
   •   Utility deposits, payments, and arrears (inclusive of water, sewer, garbage, gas, electricity, phone, and internet)
   •   Moving costs (inclusive of storage, van/truck rental, and one-time purchase of move-in necessity basics)
   •   Housing-focused transportation costs such as bus/train passes, gas vouchers, and rideshare/cab fares
   •   Housing-focused case management costs such as assessment, development of individualized housing service plans, housing
       search and placement, coordinating access to other resources essential to obtaining and retaining housing, and conducting follow-
       up and re-evaluation


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   217
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
By providing both housing-focused services and financial assistance, shelter guests will receive not only a safe and secure bed, but also
access to the critical supports needed to transition out of shelters and into permanent housing. Additionally, supportive housing-focused
activities are directly linked to effective shelter operations, as shorter shelter stays, and improved client housing outcomes lead to
increased shelter bed availability and shorter wait lists for others in need of these services.
To best meet the ongoing need for a responsive and adaptable shelter system, a variety of factors will be considered when determining
the funding formula and distribution for this package, including but not limited to:
   •   Effectiveness of previous direct grantee pools pertaining to shelter operations from the 21-23 and 23-25 biennia (i.e. individual
       shelter providers, MAC/LPG/CoC, CAA, etc.)
   •   Current and anticipated budget gaps and funding needs on an individual shelter and regional basis
   •   Regional data on housing burden, poverty rates, homelessness data such as PIT counts, and census data
   •   Regional and individual shelter bed capacity relative to population

Staffing Impact

No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results
Statewide Shelter Network Funding Outcomes
The $217.5 shelter funds package will achieve outcomes across two categories of fund utilization: Shelter Operations and Housing-
focused Activities. These Key Performance Indicators (KPI’s) will be monitored, tracked, and reported via the Homeless Management
Information System (HMIS) utilized by each shelter provider and/or regional coordinating entity. The metrics will be compared against
the 23-25 biennium’s outcomes to ensure maintenance of or improvement upon the indicators described below:

   1. Shelter Operations Outcomes
      • Maximizing Capacity & Beds Available: Maintaining full capacity at each shelter receiving funding throughout the grant
         performance period, as measured by the number of beds available for use per night compared to the number of beds shelter
         facility is capable of offering at full capacity per night.
      • Maintaining Bed Utilization & Nightly Occupancy Rates: Measure the number of beds utilized by individuals/families
         compared to the # of beds available for use.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  218
                                              BUDGET NARRATIVE

   2. Housing-focused Activities Outcomes
      • Reducing Length of Stay: Measure the average length of a household’s shelter stay (in days).
      • Improving Housing Outcomes for Shelter Guests: Measure as percentage increase of shelter guests exiting shelter into a
         stable housing situation.
      • Reducing Returns to Homelessness: Measure as percentage increase of people who retain permanent housing for at least
         6 months after exiting shelter.
Statewide Shelter Survey Outcomes
This study project will result in a final deliverable report that will include the following topics:
   •   Point-in-Time (PIT) Count analysis to better understand unmet need looking at geography and subpopulation
   •   Catalogue all existing shelters across the state using Housing Inventory Count (HIC) data and qualitative data collected from the
       region/CoC
   •   Best practices and emerging trends in the shelter system
   •   A mix of focus groups and feedback sessions with shelter staff and current/previous shelter guests from different regions to
       inform strategy planning
   •   Identification of shelters participating in HMIS and non-HMIS shelters
   •   Equity analysis around who is being served/not served within our shelter system
   •   Examination of feasibility and performance outcomes of different sheltering models (non-congregate vs congregate, low barrier
       vs high barrier)
   •   Analysis of statewide shelter funding approaches and options (regional awards by formula to Continuums of Care, individual
       shelter awards based on projected funding need/gaps, etc.)


Racial Equity Strategy
This Policy Package aims to address racial disparities within the populations served by prioritizing racial equity in program delivery.
Oregon’s homeless population exhibits significant racial disparities, particularly affecting communities of color who experience higher
rates of homelessness and housing instability. Indigenous, Black, and Brown Oregonians are disproportionately impacted by systemic
barriers that affect access to safe shelter and affordable housing. A recent report from Portland State University’s HRAC utilizing Point-
in-Time Count data from 2023 showed that communities of color in Oregon experienced homelessness at a significantly higher rate than


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                     219
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
white communities/households (over 3x their share of the state population). OHCS understands that this is a result of historic and even
present-day policies and practices by governments and institutions, including the state of Oregon, that disproportionately harm
communities of color. This policy package aims to reduce and prevent inequitable outcomes through a shelter system with a strong racial
equity strategy and lens.

For shelter operations under this policy package, OHCS will actively and intentionally monitor the racial and ethnic demographics of
those being served in the shelters to identify where communities of color continue to be disproportionately impacted and represented in
homelessness systems. OHCS will also actively and intentionally monitor the racial and ethnic demographics of those experiencing
unsheltered homelessness, whether on a wait list for shelter or not actively utilizing the shelter system. In order to achieve this, OHCS
will coordinate with regions/organizations who are receiving shelter funds to gain a strong understanding of demographics and
disparities across all regions and communities. OHCS will seek to approach the analysis and monitoring from a regional system
perspective, recognizing that shelters function as one part of a complex system of basic housing supports.

Under EO 23-02, Multi Agency Coordination (MAC) Groups and Local Planning Groups (LPGs) were required to utilize state and local
data to identify what groups are over-represented within their homeless services systems as having a disproportionately high risk of
experiencing homelessness. These groups were determined as priority populations and MACs/LPG’s implemented strategies to ensure
equitable service delivery. Some regions required further support and technical assistance to ensure they had adequate practices to reduce
racial disparities in their communities. For many regions, this included offering tools for grantees to help build partnerships with
culturally specific and culturally responsive organizations that serve those disproportionally impacted in their region. With shelter
operations under this policy package, OHCS will ensure the same strategies are used to gain a clear understanding of overrepresented
communities within homeless systems across the state, and that the demographics of guests served in shelters are aligned with the data
identifying disproportionately affected communities of color within our homelessness system. This framework builds on prior evidence
from the field of targeted universalism, which argues that prioritizing, or targeting, the needs of those with the greatest barriers will
nonetheless produce universally beneficial outcomes. In short, designing homeless interventions that work for communities of color will
produce a homeless response system that works better for all.

OHCS will coordinate with regions to implement policies/procedures that center shelter guests and services that are culturally
responsive. This will be imperative in improving outcomes for communities of color across the state that are experiencing higher rates
of homelessness. OHCS will request plans from regions receiving shelter funds that will demonstrate how they will work toward
equitable outcomes through shelter operations and program structure. If a region needs additional assistance with their plans to meet
racial equity outcomes, OHCS will deploy support including internal OHCS staff support and the utilization of technical assistance from
an OHCS-contracted provider if necessary. OHCS believes that a combination of accountability for system leaders to achieve their goals
and training and support for shelter operators to actualize the changes is most likely to yield the changes we need to see. Additionally,


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   220
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
recipients of this funding package will demonstrate how they collect and incorporate feedback from individuals and families with lived
experience of homelessness as they seek to improve services and program delivery on an ongoing basis.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
All shelter projects funded by OHCS are required to utilize HMIS to track demographics and outcomes on both a client and program
level, which gives OHCS visibility in monitoring trends in shelter access and exit destination according to a client’s race/ethnicity. With
the extensive amount of shelter providers receiving funding through this package and utilizing HMIS accordingly, this package will
increase the amount of available statewide homelessness data, resulting in more complete and accurate reporting to be used for gauging
progress toward stated goals. Ultimately, we are working toward a long-term goal of reducing the specific racial disparities that exist in
Oregon’s homeless population.
Administration
Through this shelter funding package, OHCS will continue to invest in Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs) and Culturally
Responsive Organizations (CROs), with the understanding that it is crucial to have dedicated funds for people facing the highest rates
of homelessness in Oregon. Investing in strong partnerships with culturally specific and culturally responsive community-based
organizations works to create equity within housing stability and systems that address homelessness. These partnerships will help create
an avenue to allocate shelter funds directly to communities of color- prioritizing racial equity in program delivery for those experiencing
higher rates of homelessness across the state.

OHCS will continue to reinforce and build upon specific expectations of our partners as they deploy these resources. This includes
ensuring that the local regional coordination groups have capacity, expertise and dedicated and actionable plans to reduce disparities in
their communities while supporting the local homeless services system planning and deployment efforts.

     General Fund: $217,918,652

Policy Package # 502 Sustaining Re-Housing
Package Description

The sustaining rehousing initiative policy package is proposing a $188.2 million investment aimed at addressing homelessness and
housing instability across our state for an estimated 2,870 households. This comprehensive package focuses on creating sustainable
housing outcomes by continuing the development of a robust state rehousing program – the Oregon Rehousing Initiative – to rehouse
more community members experiencing homelessness. This policy package represents a bold and necessary investment in our state’s
future by creating a more resilient and supportive housing and homelessness system. It aims to provide individuals and families with
the stability they need to thrive, ultimately contributing to a healthier and more prosperous state, with long-term housing solutions for
those experiencing homelessness.

Purpose

Oregon continues to face a profound and persistent homelessness crisis, with the state having the second highest rate of unsheltered
homelessness in the nation. According to the most recent Point in Time Count (PIT), the number of people experiencing homelessness
in Oregon has increased by 44% since 2017, with an estimated 20,100 individuals experiencing homelessness. The primary driver behind
this alarming statistic is economic instability, underscoring the urgent need for targeted and sustainable rehousing solutions.

In response to this crisis, the state has demonstrated significant progress through the work accomplished under the Emergency Order
(EO) 23-02 and continued through EO 24-02. This initiative successfully created 1,047 low-barrier shelter beds, rehoused 1,426
households, and prevented 9,024 households from becoming homeless in the first year. These achievements highlight the effectiveness
of strategic intervention and the potential for even greater impact with a sustained and expanded effort.

From these efforts OHCS was allocated $39 million in a Special Purpose Appropriation via Senate Bill (SB) 5701 (2024). This funding
is designated for a statewide rehousing program, the Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI), to help rehouse more community members
experiencing homelessness, with 25% allocated to Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs). The ORI program is intended to house
1,137 households experiencing homelessness, above and beyond the work initiated by EO 23-02 and House Bill (HB) 5019 (2023). ORI
will support placement into permanent housing throughout the remainder of the 23-25 biennium, with rehousing placements taking place
by June 30, 2025, ensuring that individuals and families in crisis have immediate access to safe and supportive housing environments.
Rehousing efforts under this Package will be for new households being placed into housing, not the sustaining of those previously



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                     107BF02
                                                                  223
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
rehoused during the 23-25 Biennium. The households being rehoused during the 23-25 Biennium will continue to receive assistance
after June 2025 by moving already appropriated funding under SB 5701 (2024) into an Other Funds account.

The purpose of this policy package is to build on the momentum initiated in the 23-25 biennium under the ORI program by investing in
rehousing an additional 2,870 new households in the 25-27 biennium. Efforts must be continued to rehouse additional households,
ensuring pathways to safe, stable housing for Oregonians experiencing homelessness. This program will continue to be designed with a
balanced approach to meet the diverse needs of Oregon communities, including urban and rural areas. The program will report out on
measurable housing outcomes, ensuring that we maintain the added capacity to the state’s housing system that will rapidly move
households along the continuum from homelessness to permanent, stable, and safe housing.

Under Executive Order 23-02 with resources from HB 5019, OHCS and its partners used targeted rehousing investments in the EO 23-
02 regions of the State and ultimately rehoused 1,426 households within 12 months of the origination of the EO 23-02. The current ORI
rehousing program, as part of EO 24-02, aims to rehouse another 1,137 households before June 30, 2025, and then sustain those
households during the 25-27 Biennium. Of note, OHCS has requested funding within Policy Option Package #503 to expand the Long-
Term Rental Assistance program to support households whose subsidy will end as a result of one-time funding. These ORI households
who are rehoused this biennium may not need additional Long-Term Rental Assistance supports until later in the Biennium when the
24 months of assistance under the ORI rehousing program ends. The services in Policy Option Package #503 are designed first to support
those households rehoused under EO 23-02 and prior programs deployed in the 23-25 Biennium who will have no ongoing assistance
after the 23-25 Biennium without additional allocations. These efforts demonstrate that with the right strategies and dedication, we can
make a substantial difference. However, our work is far from complete. With 2023 PIT data reporting 20,100 Oregonians experiencing
homelessness as of 2023, the goal of this Policy Package through the rehousing initiative to move 2,870 additional households into safe,
stable housing will have a significant impact statewide. Committing $188.2 million to a sustained and structured rehousing program will
not only help address the immediate needs of those experiencing homelessness, but also provide hope and stability to those most in
need.

How Achieved

This request extends the statewide rehousing program – the Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI) – which was funded via Senate Bill
5701. The Oregon Rehousing Initiative built upon the infrastructure established as a result of Executive Order (EO) 23-02 and House
Bill 5019 investments. On Jan. 9, 2024, Governor Kotek signed EO 24-02 to maintain the added capacity to the state’s shelter system
and expand goals to rehouse and prevent homelessness for additional households. ORI was established to serve as a statewide rehousing
program in alignment with EO 24-02 and to serve as a continuation of the rehousing initiatives already in place.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  224
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
OHCS is seeking ongoing funding to continue this momentum by investing in local communities statewide and continuing to rehouse
more community members experiencing homelessness. Employing the existing Continuum of Care (CoC) framework, Multi-Agency
Coordination (MAC) Groups and Local Planning Groups (LPGs) developed through EO 23-02, homeless systems infrastructure, and
partnerships from recent legislative investments, the resources from this package will continue to support this work at a coordinated
community level. The homeless services systems will foster deeper collaborations with key stakeholders, including local governments,
nonprofit entities, and private sector partners, to create a cohesive and comprehensive support network.

Below is a detailed description of the rehousing investment OHCS hopes to build on into the coming biennium:

Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI) | $188.2 million
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI) is a statewide rehousing program that was initiated through SB 5701 in the 23-25 biennium.
ORI is meant to provide local communities a pathway to move households experiencing homelessness off the streets or out of shelters
and quickly into a permanent rental unit. The program provides robust supportive services alongside up to 24-months of rental assistance
to ensure each household is offered the supports that they need to end their experience of homelessness and remain stably housed. The
implementation of the program included engagement with communities to inform policy decisions as the program framework was
developed. Aligning with the legislative intent of the program, these conversations and best practices have informed the following
program elements of ORI:

   -   Oregon’s homelessness crises reflect racially disparate and harmful outcomes. Targeted universalism is a strategy often utilized
       to implement rehousing programs to prioritize those communities disproportionately impacted by homelessness. In general, the
       targeted delivery of services to those communities disproportionately impacted by homelessness within regions has yielded
       positive results. Providers will utilize state and local data sources to identify over-representation of racial demographic groups
       in their homeless service system and will identify strategies to address inequities.
   -   Providers are encouraged to utilize progressive engagement to tailor services to the individual needs of each household served.
       The type, level, and duration of wraparound services; including rental assistance, and other supportive services will vary from
       household to household. Progressive engagement is an approach to helping households end their homelessness as rapidly as
       possible, despite barriers, with individually tailored financial and support resources. Through progressive engagement, assistance
       may be provided to households entering the homelessness system at a level needed by that unique household. In many cases, a
       household may need a small amount of assistance to stabilize, while others may need more resources and tailored assistance.
   -   Rehoused households are eligible for a maximum of 24 months of rental assistance.
   -   Household eligibility criteria has been expanded from HB 5019 rehousing programs to include the following categories of
       homelessness: Category 1: Literally Homeless, Category 3: Homeless Under Other Federal Statutes, Category 4:
       Fleeing/Attempting to Flee Domestic Violence, and Category 6: Unsheltered Homelessness. (Legislative intent of HB 5019


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                     107BF02
                                                                  225
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
       rehousing programs was to support unsheltered homelessness, and therefore, eligibility only included Category 6: Unsheltered
       Homelessness.)
   -   Performance measures will go beyond just the number of households rehoused. In addition to meeting their regional rehousing
       goal, regions will report on key performance indicators aligned with reducing the time participants spend homeless and increasing
       the housing stability of those rehoused.

In the 23-25 biennium, the Oregon Rehousing Initiative has a goal of rehousing a minimum of 1,137 households statewide, in addition
to those previously rehoused through EO 23-02 and HB 5019 investments. ORI goals are broken down as follows: 665 households in
the EO 24-02 designated regions; 277 households across the Balance of State (BoS); and 195 households by Culturally Specific
Organizations (CSO). In recognition of local expertise, a collaborative process was utilized when determining regional rehousing goals.
Regions submitted to OHCS plans detailing how they would implement the ORI program in their region based on the funding being
allocated. These plans included identification of priority populations, strategies that would be implemented, and the total number of
households that they would commit to rehousing through ORI programming. In the 25-27 biennium, a similar process will be followed
to determine rehousing goals within each region to reach a collective total of 2,870 households. The percentage of households served
by current EO regions, BoS regions, or CSOs may be consistent with the breakdown as reflected in the 23-25 biennium.

Staffing Impact

No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
OHCS values the importance of data systems and the impact that outcomes have on program development. OHCS-funded program data
is reported into a Homeless Management Information System (HMIS) implementation and then shared with OHCS monthly. Such data
includes demographics, project and provider information, service transactions, and outcomes. This data is then visualized into
dashboards that can be filtered by demographics, exit destinations, length of stay, etc. allowing for basic comparisons down to the
provider and client level in HMIS.
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative has a goal of rehousing 1,137 households experiencing homelessness in the 23-25 biennium. While
being a new program, the initiative is a continuation of rehousing efforts initiated through EO 23-02 and HB 5019 investments. Through
HB 5019, 1,426 households were rehoused by MAC groups by January 2024, with BoS regions actively working to rehouse an additional



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  226
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
450 households by June 2025. With the continued funding of $188.2 million, we anticipate being able to serve an additional 2,870
households through ORI in the 25-27 biennium.
In addition to the outcome of rehousing those 2,870 households, the Oregon Rehousing Initiative includes the following performance
indicators to measure success within programs:
   •   Reducing the length of time participants spend homeless as measured by the average length of time from participant
       identification and program enrollment to moving into housing being 45 days or less; and
   •   An increase in housing stability by ensuring 80% of all participants who exit the program either maintain their permanent
       housing or relocate to another permanent housing destination.
These indicators strive to align rehousing efforts with best practices towards long-term stability of household participants.

Racial Equity Strategy
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative aims to address racial disparities within the population served by prioritizing racial equity in policy
implementation and program delivery. Oregon’s population exhibits significant racial disparities, particularly affecting communities of
color who experience higher rates of homelessness and housing instability. Indigenous, Black, and Brown Oregonians are
disproportionately impacted by systemic barriers such as limited access to affordable housing and economic disparities. A recent report
from Portland State University’s Point-in-Time Count from 2023 showed that Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander Oregonians
experienced homelessness at a rate 3.58 times higher than their overall share of the state population. Black, African American, or African
Oregonians experienced homelessness at a rate 3.27 times higher than their share of the population. American Indian, Alaska Native, or
Indigenous Oregonians experienced homelessness at a rate 2.43 times higher than their share of the population.
MAC Groups and LPGs were required to utilize state and local data sources to identify what groups are being over-represented within
their homeless services systems as having a disproportionately high risk of experiencing homelessness. Regions determined strategies
to effectively address these inequities within regional rehousing programs to ensure equity and targeted universalism within service
delivery, housing placements, and housing retention. OHCS will utilize data collection to analyze the effectiveness of programs
statewide. In general, EO 23-02 and HB 5019 investments found that the targeted delivery of services to those communities
disproportionately impacted by homelessness within regions yielded positive results. When provider networks committed to serving a
specific group in their plans, they generally were able to provide more services to those demographics. The table below shows the data
from EO 23-02 resources and demonstrates that in general, providers were able to serve the households who were most
disproportionately impacted at similar rates as they show up in unsheltered counts across the State:



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                      107BF02
                                                                   227
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE




In addition to regional commitments to reduce local disparities, the Oregon Rehousing Initiative includes a commitment of continued
investment in Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs). This investment prioritizes the provision of culturally competent services and
support tailored to the unique needs of diverse communities. This includes language access, culturally appropriate programming, and
community driven solutions to housing challenges. ORI implementation utilizes an equity lens, by prioritizing the removal of barriers
and the promotion of flexibility within how programs are executed through CSO partnerships.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                   107BF02
                                                                 228
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
This funding would commit to advancing racial equity by addressing the systemic barriers and disparities faced by communities of color.
By integrating racial equity strategies into policy implementation and investing in Culturally Specific Organizations, ORI aims to ensure
that all Oregonians, regardless of race or background, have equitable access to stable housing and supportive services. This funding
promotes social justice but also contributes to building more inclusive and resilient communities across the state.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative encourages subgrantees to proactively ensure that programs are designed and implemented with
racial justice at the forefront of service delivery, which includes:
   -   Ensuring programs are accessible to all eligible individuals, regardless of protected class status;
   -   Engaging with communities that have been historically underserved or marginalized;
   -   Ensuring programs prioritize racial equity and justice; and
   -   Incorporating the lived experience of homelessness into program delivery.
Additionally, subgrantees may use funds provided through ORI to allow for data collection expenses, specifically through the Homeless
Management Information System (HMIS), to support necessary expenses to collect and report participant services, demographic data,
performance outcomes, and various other reporting requirements.

The reporting of this data to OHCS, especially as it relates to program outcomes disaggregated by race, ethnicity, and gender, helps the
agency to make informed decisions in serving some of the most vulnerable populations in Oregon and helps to guard against potential
disparate impacts. Future plans for a racial equity dashboard will emphasize demographics as the primary grouping for analysis,
beginning with race and geo-location, percentage of the total population, percentage of those in poverty, and other data via census and
PIT (Point-in-Time) counts to highlight disparities. The dashboard would offer multiselect capabilities for intersectionality with age,
gender, veteran status, domestic violence, and disability and report out on conditions such as relationship to household, family type,
prior living situation, and income level. It would track enrollment, service access, and outcomes like the length of time homeless, average
length of stay in projects, exit destinations, employment, income growth, access to non-cash benefits, and successful placements or
retentions. Moreover, it would track the ratio of a particular race/ethnicity group's share of poverty, homelessness, etc. to that group’s
share of the total population, ensuring that disparities are accurately presented through ratios that account for disproportionate burdens
and experiences faced by different racial and minority groups. Such longitudinal analyses are planned for recent investments into
rehousing including the Oregon Rehousing Initiative.

Administration


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                        107BF02
                                                                   229
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
The Oregon Rehousing Initiative prioritizes the continued investment in Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs). In the 23-25
biennium the program included a 25% set-aside to invest directly in CSOs. These organizations are in the process of implementing
their rehousing programs through specialized and culturally specific services.
It is more important than ever to have focused funds to rehouse households facing the highest rates of homelessness in Oregon. The
costs of these programs are offset by the extreme, well-documented, and continuing challenges faced by Indigenous, Black, and Brown
households historically and in present day, as well as long-term benefits to the intentional supports for housing sustainability and equity
over time.
CSO’s rehousing and culturally specific services are necessary to correct long-standing wrongs and ensure that all Oregonians have
access to an affordable place to call home. State funding should acknowledge and respect the value of these costs as justice-seeking
investments in a more fair and equitable society for all Oregonians. By leading with equity outcomes (such as using targeted universalism
or equity-based decision making as a racial equity framework for funding and policies), OHCS can ensure that investments lead to
meaningful, lasting change that benefits all.
MAC and LPGs are required to identify priority populations within their regions based on disproportionate representation within
homeless data, and as such, are encouraged to work directly with culturally specific providers in their local communities. In addition,
ORI will continue to seek to provide pathways for culturally specific organizations to directly apply for set aside resources.

Regions reported to OHCS that statewide programs would be greatly impacted if current service levels are not maintained. It would
significantly negatively impact the ability to move additional households from homelessness to housing and would impact staffing levels
for housing navigation and case management affecting rehousing systems at large. Additional impacts include destabilization of
wraparound services including behavioral health supports, disruption to data collection and reporting efforts, and support for higher
needs households would become inaccessible without funding for ongoing intensive case management and stabilization, leading to
individuals or households who are chronically homeless becoming deprioritized for services.

This policy package represents a critical investment in our state’s future by providing individuals and families with the stability they
need to thrive. Through an investment of $188.2M in the 25-27 biennium, an additional 2,870 households can be supported with long-
term housing solutions. These rehousing efforts must be continued to ensure pathways to safe, stable housing for Oregonians
experiencing homelessness, ultimately contributing to a healthier and more prosperous state.
