Policy Package # 503 Sustaining Long Term Rental Assistance (LTRA)
Package Description
This policy package is in response to Oregon's ongoing need for a statewide Long-Term Rent Assistance (LTRA) program that includes
access to longer-term rental subsidy and robust supportive services options to ensure those exiting homelessness have the tools they
need to effectively end homelessness. To ensure Oregon does not lose ground on the achievements already made this biennium, by
addressing homelessness through Governor Kotek’s Executive Order 23-02 Homelessness State of Emergency through the associated
investments of HB 5019 funds, OHCS has designed a long-term rent assistance program to support households who were rehoused
under that work. This package requests funding to sustain that successful work with $105.2M toward continued stabilization of 2,069
households with long-term rent assistance and intentionally designed supports for the most vulnerable Oregonians.

Through a collaborative effort with statewide housing provider networks, OHCS received diverse and thoughtful input in the design of
Oregon’s first statewide Long-Term Rent Assistance Program funded through SB 5511 funds in the amount of $39.7 Million. The design
process included community partners who represent the Continuums of Care (CoC) and Multi-Agency Coordination (MAC) Regions
along with the expertise of Portland State University’s Homeless Research & Action Collaborative (PSU), and with the lived experience
provided through efforts from the Mental Health Addiction Association of Oregon’s (MHAAO) platforms. The LTRA program (23-25
biennium) is currently implemented within the statewide framework of the CoC/MAC regions through June 30, 2025, in support of
approximately 1,225 households who continue to require long-term rental assistance and comprehensive wrap-around supportive
services. The current program is only being operated in the EO 23-02 Regions and this policy option package requests an expansion of
funding into the 25-27 biennium, along with geographical areas to serve additional households across Oregon in the Balance of State
regions. This expansion and continuation of the existing program ensures these individuals and families across Oregon continue to
receive the supports they need to stay housed.

Purpose

The Long-Term Rental Assistance (LTRA) program was created to ensure that those rehoused through Executive Order 23-02 and HB
5019 rehousing investments have access to long-term rent assistance and services. The allocation of $105.2 million toward rehousing
2,069 households through the LTRA program is essential to maintain stability and dignity for low-income tenants and considers the cost
inflation to support these households over the 25-27 biennium. These services are critical to continue the progress we have made during
the 23-25 Biennium in providing pathways out of homelessness in Oregon and without ongoing investment, these households will be


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                 232
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
vulnerable to losing their housing and falling back into homelessness. This funding provides significant opportunity to support the
Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI), which is Rapid-Rehousing Program designed to support households exiting homelessness for up to
24 months and facilitate individuals transitions to the LTRA program. By reaching households through Local Planning Groups (LPGs)
and Multi-Agency Coordination (MAC), OHCS can effectively collaborate across regions to implement comprehensive rehousing
strategies and maximize efforts throughout the entire state. Critically, LPGs serve the Balance of State, which is a 26-county region
across some of the most rural parts of Oregon. Of the 2,069 households served, 844 households receive support through LPGs, while
1,225 households benefit from assistance provided by MAC regions.

This coordinated assistance is a key element in our Agency strategy to address the needs of the 20,100 individuals experiencing
homelessness, as identified in the point-in-time count data from 2023. The LTRA subsidizes rent for eligible tenants, enabling them to
access apartments and homes at fair market rates. The LTRA program serves households who may initially earn up to 80% of Area
Median Income (AMI) or below and those experiencing homelessness or those at risk of homelessness. This type of assistance has
proven to be a key strategy to support housing stability and for some is vital to their ability to stay housed.

By continued investment in LTRA resources, fewer individuals and families will reside in shelters, motels, streets, and unstable living
arrangements. This funding aims to alleviate the financial burdens of a rental market out of alignment with household budgets, ensuring
that fewer households, including seniors and people with disabilities, face the dilemma of choosing between rent payments and essential
needs like medicine and food.

It is critical to note that without this necessary funding, the 1,225 households supported through LTRA will lose their vouchers and 844
households will lose their benefits when their rehousing services expire. The reduction of rental assistance after this current Biennium
could result in these households falling back into homelessness and being unable to pay the rent without state support.

Beyond immediate relief, these investments promise significant long-term benefits by mitigating economic hardships and reducing
trauma experienced by low-income households. Moreover, this initiative preserves the state’s investment in rehousing efforts under
HB5019 (2023) and SB5511 (2023), thereby enhancing community stability and prosperity. Maintaining the LTRA program will ensure
continuous rental assistance and support services, promote housing stability through locally coordinated regional administration, and
foster sustainable community development and resilience.

How Achieved
LTRA is a tenant-based rental assistance program that enables households at risk of or exiting homelessness to afford market-rate rental
units by providing supportive services and long-term rental subsidy. Program participants are responsible for finding suitable housing
of their choice, but the property owner must also agree to rent under the program. Additionally, rental units must meet HUD-standard


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  233
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
health and safety inspections. Local program administrators in each region across the State pay the monthly housing assistance directly
to the landlord on behalf of the participant, and the participant pays the difference directly to the landlord. Typically, tenants pay no
more than 30% of their income toward rent. Additionally, this program is designed to provide on-going housing assistance, thus
providing long-term stability to at-risk populations. The length of assistance varies but is generally available to a household as long as
they continue to demonstrate need based on the local administrators’ criteria, which looks at factors such as income or the availability
of other sources of rental subsidy potentially through the federal government. By contrast, many state and federal emergency rental
assistance programs offer assistance for a short period of time depending on funding availability, leaving many households without the
needed ongoing assistance to stay in their housing once the initial subsidy runs out. OHCS is requesting that this program be funded as
a current service level investment to ensure the stability of the households enrolled in the program between biennia.
OHCS received $39 million in General Funds during the 2023-2025 Biennium to launch the Long-Term Rental Assistance program.
This investment was specifically for the second year of the Biennium to ensure ongoing rent assistance was available for households
rehoused in the EO 23-02 regions under the first year of the Biennium. The funding being requested in this Package is to sustain the full
two years of the program in the EO 23-02 regions as well as ensuring the expansion of the program throughout the entire state to the
Balance of State regions, which represent 26 counties across much of the rural parts of Oregon. OHCS has also factored in cost inflation
factors to ensure the rising costs of rent and services are factored into the investment moving forward.
The Core Program elements that are universal for a successful LTRA program will continue as the basis for on-going and sustaining
program efforts and include:
   o   Supportive services required as part of the intervention
   o   Leading with racial equity
   o   Flexible strategies for supporting tenants with rental needs
   o   Landlord engagement strategies to secure available units
   o   Inspection requirements to ensure high quality housing

Multi Agency Coordination (MAC) Groups – Continuum of Care Regions
The LTRA program is designed to drive toward measurable outcomes by balancing standardization and local flexibility, mirroring the
successful approach of services funded through HB 5019 under Executive Order 23-02. By aligning with these executive order strategies,
the LTRA program ensures ongoing local adaptability. OHCS has engaged with Continuum of Care (CoC) Regions, which are the
geographic basis for the organization of the MAC groups, empowering them to adapt their LTRA programs to meet their individual
community needs while staying within designated budget limits and statewide program guidance requirements and expectations. The



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   234
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
program allows for a high level of decision-making at the local level while having key elements that are universal statewide.
Additionally, the CoCs are responsible for ensuring rental assistance, supportive services, and other LTRA resources are deployed to
prioritized households.
Local Planning Groups – Balance of State

Utilizing the existing continuum of care framework, including utilization of Multi-Agency Coordination (MAC) Groups as noted above
and Local Planning Groups (LPGs), smaller, geographically or demographically similar subregions within the Balance of State ,
infrastructure, and partnerships from recent legislative investments, the resources from this package will expand LTRA programming
to support LTRA work in the Balance of State.

Including LPGs in the LTRA program in efforts to address homelessness in our state is crucial to OHCS efforts to address homelessness
across Oregon, thereby supporting some of the most rural areas of Oregon. LPGs play a pivotal role in understanding and responding to
local housing needs, and dynamics. They have the local knowledge and connections necessary to tailor interventions to specific
communities, ensuring that solutions are relevant and effective. By including LPGs in this funding, we can foster community-driven
initiatives that are more likely to gain local support and participation. This involvement also enhances the coordination of resources and
services, optimizing efforts to rehouse individuals experiencing homelessness. Ultimately, integrating LPGs into our strategy empowers
communities to take ownership of homelessness solutions, leading to more sustainable outcomes and stronger community resilience
statewide. Currently, the LPGs are rehousing community members experiencing homelessness using time-limited resources, but we
know that many of these households will be unable to sustain their housing without the ongoing rental assistance proposed in this policy
package by transitioning to the LTRA program.

Staffing Impact

No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
The Long-Term Rent Assistance (LTRA) package will provide stable housing for approximately 2,069 households, significantly
contributing to reducing the number of unsheltered individuals. This number represents the 1,225 households who will be served in the
EO 23-02 regions as well as an additional 844 households who are being served in the Balance of State (BoS) using the time limited
rapid-rehousing funding allocated in the 23-25 Biennium. This program is a critical step in continuing to address the housing crisis,


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   235
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
currently affecting 20,100 unsheltered individuals in Oregon, as indicated by the point-in-time data from 2023. These funds will help
maintain and expand the Long-Term Rent Assistance Program to ensure that approximately 2,069 households are able to continue to
stay housed and have access to long-term rent assistance and services through the MAC Regions and LPGs.




The LTRA investment will enable us to increase access and prioritize households across the state effectively by identifying and focusing
on those most in need, particularly individuals experiencing homelessness and those with low income. This funding can also ensure that
households participating in the Oregon Rehousing Initiative (ORI) program will sustain a structured pathway to transition to LTRA. As
participants progress in ORI, they are assessed for their ongoing needs after the ORI program. Since the ORI program is a time-limited
program to a maximum of 24 months, OHCS recognizes that some households will need an option for an ongoing subsidy and supportive
services, which is where households can transition from ORI into LTRA, which offers longer-term rental assistance to ensure sustainable
housing solutions. Each local administrator is required to have a plan in place to determine their intake process for new households
coming into LTRA, which allows the region to prioritize and establish a process for determining This transition empowers households
to achieve long-term housing stability and independence and resources are allocated where they are most urgently required. The
flexibility of this funding helps meet the diverse needs of households. This approach allows communities to adapt strategies to different
circumstances and requirements, ensuring that assistance is both relevant and effective for each household.

LTRA prevents individuals from re-entering homelessness by maintaining their housing stability over the long-term. This is part of
OHCS’ commitment to build systems for addressing homelessness across the State. A critical part of any continuum of services is to
have a range of services available, from street outreach and shelter to long term rental assistance for those that need it. The LTRA
program supports this critical part of the system by ensuring that households who need the assistance to exit homelessness have the
supports they need to stay housed.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  236
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Racial Equity Strategy
Long-Term Rent Assistance is a crucial program designed to provide sustainable housing solutions for Oregonians, a critical component
of addressing the racial disparities that exist in housing and related services. This strategy outlines our commitment to ensuring racial
equity within the LTRA program, aiming to sustain an inclusive, fair, and effective housing assistance system.
Racial disparities in Oregon are evident across various sectors, including housing, healthcare, education, and employment. Some key
disparities are access to housing, substance use treatment, and economic opportunities.
According to findings from the Taskforce on Homelessness and Racial Disparities in Oregon created through House Bill 2100 the
number of Individuals identifying as Black, African American or African experiencing homelessness decreased in sheltered locations
by 2,246 people (or 3%) but this was offset by an increase in the number of unsheltered individuals who identified as Black (by 2,170
people or 4%). Asians and Asian Americans also experienced a large percentage increase in sheltered and unsheltered individual
homelessness, both of which rose by 13 percent (or 768 people). Individuals who identified as American Indian, Alaska Native, or
Indigenous experienced the largest percentage increase in sheltered homelessness, at just over 11 percent while 16% more people
identifying as Hispanic or Latin(a)(o)(x) experienced homelessness as individuals in 2022 than in 2020 (12,410 more people). This
overall increase reflects an 18 percent increase in unsheltered Hispanic individuals and an 11 percent increase in sheltered Hispanic
individuals. It is important to state, due to systemic and long-standing barriers, racial minorities frequently experience higher
unemployment rates and lower income levels, further limiting their ability to afford and maintain stable housing.
As with all state and federal program funding, LTRA is accessible to all individuals and organizations regardless of their protected
classification. Further, the program requires grantees to proactively ensure that LTRA is accessible to all individuals and organizations,
regardless of protected class. Additionally, grantees must proactively reach out to and engage with communities that have been
historically underserved or marginalized. Lastly, program operators may use funds for expenses tied to accommodations and efforts to
ensure the program is delivered equitably based on both quantitative and qualitative data. As part of the program design, OHCS will
continue to require each local region to submit a community plan that identifies the underserved populations in their region and specific
actions the region plans to take to reduce the rate of homelessness in those communities. OHCS has used this approach to deploying the
resources under EO 23-02, to ensure an equitable response to homelessness.
Advancing racial equity and justice is part of OHCS’ commitment as an agency, by building programs and advancing strategies that
center those who are disparately impacted by the problems of housing affordability and homelessness. OHCS’s racial equity work is
informed by national promising practices and lived experience of communities of color. OHCS and its grantees commit to an intentional,
data driven approach to reducing disparities in housing and social service provision. Additional guidance and information continue to
be developed to further identify how OHCS and grantees can advance equity and racial justice within state and federally funded


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   237
                                              BUDGET NARRATIVE
programs. To achieve racial equity in the community, government (local, regional, and state) must be working in partnership with
communities and other institutions.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The commitment to racial equity within the Long-Term Rent Assistance program is unwavering. We aim to create an equitable housing
system in Oregon. Grantees must proactively consider steps to ensure the programs are designed and implemented in a representative
fashion in the communities they serve with racial justice at the forefront of their service delivery plan. This ensures that the LTRA
program is accessible to all eligible individuals and organizations, regardless of race, color, national origin, age, religion, gender, familial
status, disability, victims of domestic violence, marital status, sexual orientation, gender identity, or sources of income.
The LTRA program is making a tangible difference in the lives of Oregon’s most vulnerable residents. By increasing housing stability,
improving financial conditions, ensuring long-term housing retention, and reducing recidivism to homelessness, the LTRA program
proves its efficacy and necessity by these key performance measures:
    1. Increase housing stability as measured by the percentage of total program participants who reside in permanent housing at time
       of their exit from the program: This measure underscores the program’s effectiveness in moving individuals from temporary or
       unstable housing situations into secure long-term living arrangements.
    2. Maintaining or increasing household income and benefits from all sources: Households enrolled in the LTRA program will work
       towards maintaining or increasing their household income and befits from all potential sources during their participation with
       the assistance of the Support Services available in conjunction with the rental subsidy. Increasing financial stability helps to
       prevent future homelessness and ultimately contributes to the overall well-being and self -sufficiency of program participants.
    3. Number of households who have been enrolled in the LTRA program and successfully maintain their housing placement for 12
       months: This metric highlights the programs’ ability to provide sustainable housing solutions that endure beyond initial
       placement, offering long-term security for vulnerable populations. The assistance under this program can be provided longer
       than 12 months but this metric allows OHCS to understand the first-year retention rates, in alignment with best practices for
       rehousing support programs across the nation.
    4. Percentage of households who do not re-enter into homelessness or request additional housing assistance withing 12 months of
       exiting the program: This indicates the program’s success in offering permanent solutions rather than temporary fixes, leading
       to lasting housing stability for participants.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                          107BF02
                                                                      238
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Reporting on these key performance measures will be disaggregated by race so that we can accurately assess racial equity impacts of
this program. Further, OHCS will work with local administrators to understand and act when disparities in outcomes are present for
specific subpopulations. This analysis and ongoing evaluation work is critical to an equitable implementation of the program.
Administration
LTRA program has established expectations in the guidance and contract for grantees to implement comprehensive outreach and
engagement activities to ensure Culturally Responsive Organizations (CRO) are engaged in the EO efforts through their established
relationships and programming capacities, to serve disproportionately impacted homeless population, including individuals and
households who are covered by state and federal civil rights classifications including race, disability, sex/gender, income instability,
family homelessness, homeless youth, and individuals fleeing or impacted by domestic violence or human trafficking. Several LTRA
grantees have established informal and formal agreements with CROs to assist with engaging with disproportionally impacted homeless
populations they have identified within their communities and shared their further commitment to grow their networks to include more
providers who specialize in engaging with homeless veterans, transitioning youth and households fleeing domestic violence. Further,
LTRA grantees have stated how their communities will partner with local organizations and community leaders to provide targeted
support and resources, streamline access to services, and offer technical assistance to enhance capacity. This inclusive approach aims to
address the unique barriers these populations face, ensuring equitable access to housing and support services.

Policy Package # 504 Continuing to Prevent Evictions (Prevention Infrastructure $63.5
million)
Package Description

This policy package requests $63.5 million in ongoing General Funds for homelessness prevention operations and staffing infrastructure
to sustain eviction prevention efforts funded by the Oregon Legislature in the 23-25 biennium to build a more robust eviction prevention
and housing retention system. This funding will be used to continue critical eviction prevention programs to maintain a safety net in the
25-27 biennium for the most vulnerable renters to ensure that more low-income Oregonians remain safely housed. This type of
investment must be ongoing year over year to ensure community partners and providers can maintain the operations and staffing
necessary to prevent evictions and the inflow of households into homelessness statewide.

Note: Policy Option Package #504 is only one portion of two corresponding funding packages. This Policy Option Package
includes funding for the operations, staffing and infrastructure for preventing evictions and Policy Option Package #505
provides the client financial assistance portion of the overall package.

Purpose
Eviction impacts tens of thousands of Oregonians every year, putting families at risk of homelessness and an innumerable list of short-
term traumas and longer-term hardships associated with displacement. While financial assistance in the form of rent payments is a
critical intervention that can immediately prevent an eviction, the services and supports provided by staff across the Community Based
Organizations (CBOs), Community Action Agencies (CAAs), Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs), Legal Services and Tenant
Services networks provide the necessary underpinnings to deliver this assistance and provide the services that can support longer-term
stability in addition to immediate crisis intervention. These services support behavioral health needs, community building, landlord
mediation, legal interventions and support, and are optimally tailored by community-based providers for the individual(s) facing housing
instability. While these services are not funded or intended to be as intensive as those in permanent supportive housing or other
supportive housing models, we depend on them to implement best practices to meet the holistic needs of individuals and families, not
just the financial pieces.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                             107BF02
                                                                   241
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
Housing stability is essential to strong and healthy communities and necessary for people to address their challenges and pursue their
goals. Substantial evidence indicates that when people experience homelessness or other forms of housing instability, their prospects
for future educational attainment, employment growth, health stability, and family preservation are significantly reduced.

How Achieved
Funding in the amount of $63.5 million in the Eviction Prevention infrastructure package for the 25-27 biennium will provide operations
and services to support and prevent evictions for 25,325 households statewide who we anticipate being served with financial assistance
requested in Policy Option Package #505. The organizations that will be eligible for this funding include the Community Action Agency
network (CAA) and their subgrantees, Culturally Specific Organizations (CSO), other community-based organizations, and tenant
education and advocacy services organizations. To maintain the current level of eviction prevention services, funding will be allocated
through five existing funding streams. These programs include the Oregon Eviction Diversion and Prevention Program (ORE-DAP),
Eviction Prevention Rapid Response (EPRR), Culturally Specific Organization eviction prevention programs, Legal Services, and
Tenant Services. OHCS engaged with current eviction prevention grantees in the 23-25 biennium to determine maintenance of efforts
(MoE) in operations and staffing costs to serve the same number of households in the 25-27 biennium. The MoE analysis and the amount
of funding requested is a result of the engagement and 23-25 outcomes to date. The deployment of these funds is intended to stem the
inflow into the homelessness response systems and help households to self-actualize housing stability over the long term.

Please note that this Policy Option Package funds only the operations and administrative costs for providers of eviction prevention
investments. The corresponding Policy Option Package #505 provides funding for the financial assistance portion of the package.

Oregon Eviction Diversion and Prevention Infrastructure (ORE-DAP) ($38.5M) – Supporting 17,201 Households (HHs)
ORE-DAP infrastructure funding, $38.5 million dollars, will allow for the continued successful implementation of programs around
the state of Oregon that address the eviction crisis the state is currently experiencing. Dedicated funding will allow for continuation of
services for operations, staffing and administration. Successful eviction prevention is comprised of more than just financial assistance
for households in need. Appropriate infrastructure funding ensures organizations can continue to operate efficiently. The financial
assistance to support the ORE-DAP program and those most at risk of homelessness are included separately in Policy Option Package
#505.

Community Action agencies who received ORE-DAP funding for eviction prevention programs report that uncertainty about future
funding impacted their planning process for disbursing relief. Half of the agencies OHCS spoke with reported being understaffed which
impacted their ability to administer eviction prevention funding efficiently and effectively. There is a continued need for eviction
prevention case management services that offer supportive services and referrals to additional resources that meet the often complicated
set of needs people at risk of eviction face in a trauma informed and responsive way. Additionally, operations and administration funding

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   242
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
allow for continuity in service provision in support of achieving outcomes. Providing agencies the ability to plan for their services with
infrastructure funding, is necessary to achieve the goal of 17,201 households receiving assistance.

Community Action Agencies have unique and established regional systems to successfully reach performance milestones. Agencies are
aware of the best way to serve their communities and primarily employ people from their communities, some of whom have lived
experience of housing instability. Providing sufficient and ongoing operational funding allows agencies to maintain the workforce need
to deliver programming that is responsive to the individual needs, strengths, and challenges of their program participants to continue to
build upon past success to serve Oregonians in need.

(CSO) Culturally Specific Organizations Infrastructure ($16.4M) – Supporting 4,592 Households (HHs)
Culturally specific providers are embedded in and center around the lived experience of their community members. These often-
marginalized communities experience the compounding effects of over-representation in negative indicators like poverty and eviction
filing rates while at the same time experiencing more barriers to services. These factors are a result of discriminatory practices, systemic
racism and decades of government policy that has excluded and marginalized many communities of color across Oregon. While only
about 3% of Oregon’s population is Black (2020 Census), 20% of all renters are Black, and they make up 32.7% of all eviction filings
in Oregon. Likewise, 14% of all Oregonians identify as Latinx, and 21% of all renters, but make up 19% of evictions filed.

In addition to the inherent instability associated with being a low-income renter, many people who are also members of non-dominant
cultural group face additional challenges to maintaining housing stability and experience barriers to the services that can help. Language
access barriers, eligibility limitations for immigrant and refugee communities, and lack of culturally relevant services can all prevent
people from accessing supportive services and are key elements culturally specific service provision. It is critical that the staff of
culturally specific organizations have the specialized language skills, familiarity with unique complexities of navigating systems as a
refugee, and cultural background similarities to help mitigate these barriers. Ensuring this type of specialized staffing takes resources.

The types of programming and how that programming is offered and accessed is also important. Providing a deeper level of service
connection beyond simple referrals is critical for success in employment and education programs that can positively impact the long-
term stability of households. Providing additional services like childcare and transportation assistance and translation services to help
facilitate program participation can be particularly important for some cultural groups who have less access to these resources.

The requested investment of $16.4 million in infrastructure funding will help ensure that the related financial services investments in
Policy Option Package #505 are delivered in a way that truly can support the unique needs of different culturally specific communities
within Oregon.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                               107BF02
                                                                    243
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE

Eviction Prevention Rapid Response (EPRR) Infrastructure ($1.1M) – Supporting 3,532 Households (HHs)
The EPRR program prevents evictions by quickly issuing rent payments for people who have scheduled eviction court dates. Because
OHCS does not provide direct services, we contract with a third-party financial organization to process the time-sensitive rent assistance
checks. The $1.1 million requested in infrastructure funding to support the EPRR program includes paying for this processing, the
associated expense tracking, regular reporting, and monitoring and other financial and system administration. The rent assistance
available through the EPRR program is being requested through Policy Option Package #505.

Legal Assistance Infrastructure ($3.7M) – 11,200 Legal Assistance Touchpoints
The Oregon statewide eviction moratorium ended on June 30, 2021, and in the wake of the lifting of the moratorium, evictions have
skyrocketed across the state of Oregon. In 2023, 24,860 eviction cases were filed in Oregon’s circuit and justice courts, representing
one in twenty-five renter-occupied households in Oregon. Only 8% of defendants had legal representation, while close to 40% of
landlords and property owners had legal representation. In response to the high-need to avoid and stay evictions in Oregon to keep
Oregonians in their homes and safe from houselessness, a legal services eviction defense program was established by OHCS. These
efforts, in the 21-23 and 23-25 biennia, have helped stay over 70% of the evictions accepted by this program in the Oregon courts and
in other negotiations.

Eviction defense includes representation in the courtroom but also requires more than just attorneys. Done well, it entails a continuum
of services to meet a variety of tenant needs. Robust eviction defense services involve a range of coordinated legal and legal-adjacent
services to support household stabilization, including direct legal services, early diversion programs, mediation services, community
education and outreach, and ancillary services such as rental assistance. This range of services requires a diversity of system partners,
such as non-profit legal services providers, private attorneys, landlord-tenant courts, community-based organizations, Community
Action Agencies, tenant organizations, mediation providers, rental assistance providers, and landlords/housing providers. Engaging a
diversity of partners enables the leveraging of each partner’s expertise and resources to collectively and optimally address tenant needs.

The requested $3.7 million for Legal Services infrastructure funding will provide staffing and operations support for these critical
services and partnerships to provide 11,200 touchpoint services including legal advice, limited scope representation, full scope
representation, mediation, and referrals to community and other resources.

Tenant Services Infrastructure ($3.7M) supporting 8,940 Households (HHs)
Tenant Services organizations currently serve close to 9,000 Households (HH) through the Biennium, or 4500 HH per year through
hotline calls, rent-well classes, legal assistance, referrals, workshops and hosting collaborative problem-solving meetings to provide

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   244
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
recommendations on the needs for eviction prevention strategies for low-income tenants and vulnerable communities. Outreach to
communities most impacted include on-the-ground outreach efforts, engagement with culturally specific organizations, and virtual
community organizing to get the word out about services and resources, as well as marketing and other forms of media to ensure high
visibility and availability of services.

Tenant Service organizations provide an array of services that help support eviction prevention through landlord mediation, tenant
advocacy and education, translation services, and managing hotlines to address tenant questions and emergencies.

Staffing Impact
No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results

Outcomes
This eviction prevention package represented by this Policy Option Package in conjunction with the financial assistance funding
requested in Policy Option Package #505 will provide supportive services to over 25,000 Oregonians in the 25-27 Biennium, providing
funding to stem the tide of households who are at risk of falling into homelessness. The funding of this network of statewide providers
to provide these services a critical step in continuing to mitigate the crisis of homelessness on our streets. Eviction prevention services
ensure that more people are not falling into homelessness at the same time as we make historic efforts to rehouse families. Supportive
Services allow programs to ensure that program participants receive additional services that they want and need, such as but not limited
to, referrals to physical and behavioral healthcare, support in reaching education goals, and accessing income attainment and/or
increasing that income. The homeless services system does this by coordinating deeply with various other support systems to build
pathways to long term stability.

In the current Biennium, ninety five percent (95%) of the grantees funded with housing retention dollars are utilizing the Homeless
Management Information System (HMIS), to track and report on outcomes. Grantees that do not use this system report on their efforts
to ensure an accountable and outcomes-oriented system. OHCS monitors types of services being provided, the cost of services and
outcomes for each household assisted for most of the programs. We can see the efficacy of the programs by race, gender, age, and
ethnicity. Currently, over 90% of Households being served are maintaining their permanent housing at the time of exit from the program.
OHCS is focused on ensuring performance outcomes such as providing rapid responses for those facing eviction and improving the rate
of people who exit the programs that are still housed. We are committed to working with partners across the State to ensure the resources
are part of a cohesive and aligned strategy to prevent homelessness.

Racial Equity Strategy

Oregon’s population is 80% White, 3% Black/African American/African and12% of Oregon’s overall population experience poverty.
However, of Oregon’s White population, 11% experience poverty whereas in Oregon’s Black/African American/African population,
24% experience poverty. Therefore, Black/African American/African Oregonians experience poverty at more than 2x the rate of
White Oregonians. While only about 3% of Oregon’s population is Black (2020 Census), 20% of all renters are Black, but they
receive 32.7% of all eviction filings in Oregon. Likewise, 14% of all Oregonians identify as Latinx, and 21% of all renters, but make
up 19% of evictions file and 17% of judgments on behalf of the landlord. Eviction prevention is fundamental to addressing housing
equity and racial justice. Evictions disproportionately affect communities most affected by systemic oppression and households with
lower incomes. Even within those groups, disproportionately, Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal
communities experience disparities more acutely. OHCS is working to ensure that we are utilizing opportunities to build systems that
center those most impacted by historic inequitable housing policies and investments. This includes the need to invest in organizations
and initiatives that will work with people who have lived experience of homelessness, housing instability and the impacts of systemic
racism while we ensure that this voice is elevated in policy decisions.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes

To evaluate and improve outcomes and racial equity through quantitative and qualitative data, central research questions must be
developed and focus on the experiences of minority and vulnerable populations and how non-white/BIPOC communities are being
served by OHCS programs. The goal is to identify degrees of disparities in services and outcomes among relevant client-level groups
using outside (census and other) datasets.

In the 25-27 biennium, OHCS’s Performance and Evaluation team, we will improve outcome and reporting capabilities, capturing long
term housing retention for eviction prevention programs, as well as compare populations being served vs. the region's population,
especially by race, gender, age, ethnicity and disabilities. In the past, we have looked at the demographics of the total population in
poverty without comparing the share of a particular group in poverty to that group’s share of the total population. This method shows
whether a group is underrepresented or overrepresented in poverty, unsheltered homelessness, or experiencing rent burden, for example.
We then compared this to demographics in certain programs or project types but did not examine the rate at which certain groups were
served, enrolled, and achieved outcomes or the degree of disparities between white and BIPOC communities.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                           107BF02
                                                                 246
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
In addition to regional commitments to reduce local disparities, the Housing Prevention/Eviction Prevention work includes a
commitment of continued investment in Culturally Specific Organizations and their subgrantees. One current CSO grantee funds 25+
Community Based Organizations across Oregon who provide language access, culturally appropriate programming, and community
driven solutions to housing challenges. While OHCS continues to fund culturally specific organizations led by Black, Indigenous,
Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal communities, we must do more to further this critical work. Historically, eviction
prevention work funded by the state has been through the Community Action Agency network. Currently, only one culturally specific
organization is part of the CAA network., In the 25-27 biennium we will be developing an application process that will make more
culturally specific organizations eligible for funding and we will continue to build relationships with regular meeting and program tours
to grow our network of providers and to learn and provide flexible contracting based on their expertise on how best to serve the people
reaching out to them for assistance.

OHCS is currently developing a racial equity dashboard will emphasize demographics as the primary grouping for analysis, beginning
with race and geo-location, percentage of the total population, percentage of those in poverty, and other data via census and PIT (Point-
in-Time) counts to highlight disparities. The dashboard would offer multiselect capabilities for intersectionality with age, gender, veteran
status, domestic violence, and disability and report out on conditions such as relationship to household, family type, prior living situation,
and income level. It would track enrollment, service access, and outcomes like the length of time homeless, average length of stay in
projects, exit destinations, employment, income growth, access to non-cash benefits, and successful placements / retentions. Moreover,
it would track the ratio of a particular race/ethnicity group's share of poverty, homelessness, etc. to that group’s share of the total
population, ensuring that disparities are accurately presented through ratios that account for disproportionate burdens and experiences
faced by different racial and minority groups.

Program disaggregated data will be used to compare outcomes to respective subpopulations, ensuring our outcomes reflect the needs in
communities. Furthermore, publicly available qualitative research and qualitative data available through partnerships will be leveraged
to provide direction for quantitative analysis, generate meaning and application of findings, verify results, and identify opportunities for
training and policy changes.

Administration
It is crucial that those accessing the services and programs offered through this Policy Option Package reflect those who are disparately
impacted. These partnerships will help create an avenue to allocate eviction prevention funds directly to communities of color, -
prioritizing racial equity in program delivery for those experiencing higher rates of housing instability across the state.

Through current and requested eviction prevention funding OHCS contracts directly with culturally specific organizations, and through
the CAA network directly and indirectly through subgrantees. Portions of the 25-27 biennium allocation will continue to support and

 Policy Package # 505 Continuing to Prevent Evictions (Prevention Financial Assistance $109.7 million)
Package Description
This policy package requests $109.7 million in ongoing General Funds for homelessness prevention client assistance funding to sustain eviction
prevention efforts funded by the Oregon Legislature in the 23-25 biennium to build a more robust eviction prevention and housing retention system.
This funding will be used to continue critical eviction prevention assistance to maintain a safety net in the 25-27 biennium for the most vulnerable
renters to ensure that more low-income Oregonians remain safely housed. This type of investment must be ongoing year over year to ensure community
partners and providers can continue to provide flexible, responsive, short-term financial assistance to keep people in their homes and slow the inflow
of households into homelessness statewide.

Note: Policy Option Package #505 is only one portion of two corresponding funding packages. Policy Option Package #504 includes funding
for the operations, staffing, and infrastructure for preventing evictions and this Policy Option Package #505 provides the client financial
assistance portion of the overall package.

Purpose

In 2023, 24,860 eviction cases were filed in Oregon’s circuit and justice courts. One in twenty-five renter-occupied households in Oregon had eviction
cases filed against them that year and approximately 85-90% of evictions in the state are filed for nonpayment of rent. The primary drivers of eviction
are economic (e.g., rising rents, stagnant wages) and structural (e.g., insufficient affordable housing). In 2023, Oregon had the 14th highest housing
wage in the country defined as the necessary wage a household must obtain to afford a 2-bedroom rental at $27.65 an hour. Half of Oregon Renters
spent more than 30% of their income on rent and as rents continue to outpace wages, low-income renters remain at increased risk of eviction and
longer-term housing instability. Clearly, too many Oregonians are one missed paycheck or one unexpected bill away from not being able to pay their
rent or are faced with the choice of doing without food or utilities to keep a roof over their heads.

This is especially true for Black, Indigenous, and other People of Color (BIPOC) households whom national and state data shows are overrepresented
in the population of renters facing eviction proceedings. While only about 3% of Oregon’s population is Black (2020 Census), 20% of all renters are
Black, and they make up 32.7% of all eviction filings in Oregon. Likewise, 14% of all Oregonians identify as Latinx, and 21% of all renters, but make
up 19% of evictions filed.

The purpose of eviction prevention financial assistance is to provide one-time or short-term assistance to help households address an immediate shortfall
in household income to avoid eviction. The majority of eviction prevention funding is utilized for rent payments, including current rent due and arrears.
Other critical categories of assistance include utility assistance, food, move-in costs to maintain housing by moving into a new housing situation, and
legal assistance for renters currently in an eviction process that can be stopped through providing back rent and court fees. Intervening with this limited
duration assistance before someone loses housing avoids the human costs of the trauma of the experience of homelessness and prevents in-flow into
an already overwhelmed and costly sheltering and homeless services system

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                      250                                                                107BF02
                                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
How Achieved

Funding in the amount of $109.7 million in the Eviction Prevention financial assistance package for the 25-27 biennium will provide rental and other
assistance to 25,325 households statewide. The organizations that will be eligible for this funding include the Community Action Agency network
(CAA) and their subgrantees, Culturally Specific Organizations (CSO), other community-based organizations, and tenant education and advocacy
services organizations. To maintain the current level of eviction prevention services, funding will be allocated through four existing funding streams
in the same proportion as in the 23-25 biennium. These programs include the Oregon Eviction Diversion and Prevention Program (ORE-DAP),
Eviction Prevention Rapid Response (EPRR), Culturally Specific Organization eviction prevention programs, and Tenant Services. OHCS engaged
with current eviction prevention grantees in the 23-25 biennium to determine maintenance of efforts (MoE) in financial assistance and households to
serve in the 25-27 biennium. The MoE analysis and the amount of funding requested is a result of the engagement and 23-25 outcomes to date.

Please note that this Policy Option Package funds only the financial assistance portion and Policy Option Package #504 provides funding for the
infrastructure of the investments in the form of operations, staffing and administrative costs.

Oregon Eviction Diversion and Prevention (ORE-DAP) ($60M) Financial Assistance – Supporting 17,201 Households (HHS)

ORE-DAP funding is currently contracting statewide through the Community Action Agencies (CAAs) and their network of subgrantees. Their deep
experience has been an effective conduit to help households avoid eviction. Based on a statewide analysis of 23-25 actual and projected outcomes
through the Maintenance of Effort (MOE) research, a continuation of current service levels in ORE-DAP using fundamentally the same program design
requires $60M to reach 17,201 households. It is important to state that this funding level is not adequate to serve the full eviction prevention need, even
of those most urgently at risk of losing their homes. This represents OHCS and our partner’s estimate of what it would take to sustain a similar level
of service to the 23-25 Biennium. To extend available funding across the biennium, providers will often limit the acceptance of new applications at
different periods during the year. It is ensured that some funding is available for each quarter and season of the year to best respond to emergency
needs.

Allowable uses of ORE-DAP financial assistance include rental assistance payments, move-in deposits food, pet rent or security fees, and other services
that increase long-term stability for renters.

The existing prioritization of program funds to deliver financial assistance to those most in need across a spectrum of vulnerable and marginalized
communities will remain in place in the 25-27 biennium program. For the purposes of this program, priority will be given to households with active
eviction notices and those with scheduled court appearances. Special outreach and advertising publicize the program to populations most at risk of
experiencing houselessness such as those with low, very low, or no income, those who are aging and/or experiencing disabilities, and communities of
color, as groups overrepresented in the landscape of homelessness in the state of Oregon. The staffing, infrastructure, and operations costs to support
this outreach to those most at risk of homelessness are included separately in Policy Option Package #504.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                       251                                                               107BF02
                                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
Eviction Prevention Rapid Response (EPRR) ($28.3M) Financial Assistance for 3,532 Households (HHs)

The Eviction Prevention Rapid Response (EPRR) program is dedicated to directly preventing evictions that are pending on the court docket as civil
cases in the state of Oregon. As we know from data reported through eviction research at Portland State University, 25,473 such eviction cases were
filed in the state of Oregon in the twelve months between July 2023 and June 2024. The Eviction Prevention Rapid Response program is a final
intervention point to financial assistance for many tenants facing the most imminent risk of eviction and, ultimately, imminent risk of entering temporary
or chronic homelessness. OHCS will contract with a third-party financial organization to administer the rent assistance funds directly to the landlords
for those households with eviction court dates. The infrastructure and operations costs to support the EPRR program are included separately in Policy
Option Package #504.

To maintain efforts from the 23-25 biennium, OHCS is requesting $28.3 million in financial assistance to support EPRR in addressing and avoiding
the non-payment evictions of 3,532 households throughout the state. Allowances within the EPRR program guidance include the ability to financially
settle stipulated agreements and landlord supplied ledgers encompassing rent arrearages, forward rent within payment caps, security deposit, pet rent,
utilities, storage and other fees tied to the rental ledger. However, for some households who are experiencing concurrent issues like unemployment,
unmet behavioral healthcare needs, and other system involvement like with child welfare, one month of rent support may not be sufficient to avoid
eviction in the long term. We are working to develop more robust referral network and access to other support networks for these households. Currently
referrals to tenant resource organizations and the Community Action Agencies across Oregon are in place, though system capacity remains limited due
to funding constraints.

Culturally Specific Organizations (CSO) ($21.3M) Financial Assistance, 4,592 Households (HHs)

For the 25-27 biennium, OHCS is requesting $21.3 million in direct financial assistance support to aid in maintaining and bolstering the housing
stability gained by vulnerable communities during the current 23-25 biennium. Culturally Specific Organizations received allocations of OHCS’
housing retention and eviction prevention funding in the 23-25 biennium and this package would continue to fund CSOs in the 25-27 biennium.
Programming led by CSOs will serve 4,592 households with a variety of financial interventions, most commonly, rental assistance, but additionally
with supportive services intended to build long-term stability. Working within communities of color, these eviction prevention efforts seek to meet
people facing eviction where they are and provide responsive, flexible services that meet the unique needs of the community.

Culturally specific organizations funded in the 23-25 biennium provided services to Black, Latinx, agricultural worker, migrant worker, immigrant,
refugee, and other communities of color. Financial assistance models designed by and for these communities provide immediate rental assistance but
also consider holistic housing stability needs, highlighting the needs of the entire household both in the short term and by preparing for long term
stability. Examples of financial assistance to support households on the journey to stability include employment and educational assistance and
connection to financial literacy and home ownership education programs. The operations and staffing costs associated with the CSO financial assistance
are included in Policy Option Package #504.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                      252                                                               107BF02
                                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
Tenant Services ($156,300) Financial Assistance – 11,200 Services Provided
Funding for tenant services declined in the 23-25 biennium, reducing the amount of financial assistance provided by tenant services organizations.
Policy Option Package #504 requests funding to continue funding for operations for tenant services organizations providing landlord mediation, rent-
well, tenant advocacy and education, and managing hotlines to address tenant questions and emergencies. Because these organizations are client-
facing, they have the ability to intervene in emergency situations by providing food, hotel stays, and hygiene items, or able to provide rent or deposit
assistance on a small scale to respond to an immediate opportunity to move into a new apartment or avoid eviction. This requested financial support
would allow them to be able to continue these direct, meaningful interventions. The staffing, infrastructure, and operations costs to support tenant
services to those most at risk of homelessness are included separately in Policy Option Package #504.

Staffing Impact

No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to the
maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results

Outcomes
This eviction prevention package will provide financial assistance to over 25,000 Oregonians in the 25-27 Biennium, contributing significantly to
reducing the number of households moving into homelessness. The funding of this network of statewide providers is a critical step in continuing to
mitigate the crisis of homelessness on our streets. Eviction prevention services ensure that more people are not falling into homelessness at the same
time as we make historic efforts to rehouse families. When families can stop worrying about the crisis of being evicted, they are able to focus on other
needs and goals, such as but not limited to, their physical and behavioral health needs, education goals and possibly other programs to help increase
their income.

In the current Biennium, ninety five percent (95%) of the grantees funded with housing retention dollars are utilizing the Homeless Management
Information System (HMIS), to track and report on outcomes. Grantees that do not use this system report on their efforts to ensure an accountable and
outcomes-oriented system. OHCS monitors types of services being provided, the cost of services and outcomes for each household assisted for most
of the programs. We can see the efficacy of the programs by race, gender, age, and ethnicity. Currently, over 90% of Households being served are
maintaining their permanent housing at the time of exit from the program. OHCS is focused on ensuring performance outcomes such as providing rapid
responses for those facing eviction and improving the rate of people who exit the programs that are still housed. We are committed to working with
partners across the State to ensure the resources are part of a cohesive and aligned strategy to prevent homelessness.

access, and outcomes like the length of time homeless, average length of stay in projects, exit destinations, employment, income growth, access to non-
cash benefits, and successful placements / retentions. Moreover, it would track the ratio of a particular race/ethnicity group's share of poverty,
homelessness, etc. to that group’s share of the total population, ensuring that disparities are accurately presented through ratios that account for
disproportionate burdens and experiences faced by different racial and minority groups.

Program disaggregated data will be used to compare outcomes to respective subpopulations, ensuring our outcomes reflect the needs in communities.
Furthermore, publicly available qualitative research and qualitative data available through partnerships will be leveraged to provide direction for
quantitative analysis, generate meaning and application of findings, verify results, and identify opportunities for training and policy changes.

Administration
It is crucial that those accessing the services and programs offered through this Policy Option Package reflect those who are disparately impacted.
These partnerships will help create an avenue to allocate eviction prevention funds directly to communities of color, - prioritizing racial equity in
program delivery for those experiencing higher rates of housing instability across the state.
OHCS contracts directly with culturally specific organizations and reaches these organizations through the CAA network via subgrantee relationships.
As part of this package, OHCS will be developing an application process in the 25-27 biennium to expand our current network of CSO grantee partners.
The services funding requested in this Policy Option Package are intended to work in conjunction with the infrastructure funding requested in Policy
Option Package #504 to support ongoing operations of this critical culturally specific capacity.

Revenue Sources

General Fund: $109,685,262
