2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget          256         107BF02
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE

Policy Package # 506 Maintaining Tribal Homelessness Investments

Package Description

This package will build upon and extend funding for the nine federally recognized tribes of Oregon through the current tribal investment:
By and For Initiative, Native American Tribes of Oregon (BAFI-NATO). The BAFI-NATO program was funded in the 21-23 and 23-
25 biennia; however, these funds were one-time in nature and lacked long-term investments dedicated to tribal homelessness and housing
initiatives. Until recently, many Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) programs like BAFI-NATO tended to be limited by
the biennium, were incremental, did not include tribal preference or set asides, had limitations in scope, and insufficient resources to
meet the ongoing housing needs of tribal communities in a way that honors tribal sovereignty. The current program allows for the grant
to be utilized for homelessness response and prevention activities and the proposed expansion will include eligible activities in affordable
rental housing and homeownership programs and would create a dedicated and on-going funding opportunity for the nine federally
recognized tribes of Oregon

Purpose

Prior to 2019, only a few of the tribes of Oregon received nominal funding through various OHCS funding streams, even though our
research and data shows that Native Americans in Oregon are 4x more likely to experience unsheltered homelessness than any other
population within the state. In 2022, OHCS engaged with tribes to understand the unsheltered homelessness crisis happening in tribal
communities and through intentional engagement, created a program and funding to specifically address the increase in Native American
homelessness in Oregon. The By and For Initiative: Native American Tribes of Oregon (BAFI-NATO) was the first of its kind for the
agency and was implemented in the 2021-2023 biennium to address tribal homelessness. The allocations were distributed by direct
award to the Federally Recognized Tribes of Oregon through a non-competitive application process.

The BAFI-NATO program design process elicited the full participation of all nine of the federally recognized tribes of Oregon. However,
tribal leaders have continually noted the challenges and barriers of utilizing state funded programs. In addition to the BAFI-NATO
shelter and homelessness funding, most tribes also own and operate affordable housing units and have homeownership programs.
Whether it is shelter beds, transitional housing, affordable rental housing or homeownership opportunities, tribes have been effectively
dissuaded from applying for OHCS funding by way of exclusionary practices that include limitations on administrative costs and the
inability to fund projects that are located on trust lands.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                    257
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
The continual funding and expansion of BAFI-NATO would be a comprehensive solution to addressing the breadth of housing needs
within tribal communities. This concept aligns with recommendations from the Task Force on Homelessness and Racial Disparities
created by House Bill 2100 and implemented through Senate Bill 893 in that this funding will directly allocate program funds to the
nine federally recognized tribes of Oregon. The new BAFI-NATO program design would uplift tribal sovereignty, provide critical
housing funding, and honor tribal self-governance by consolidating program funds to give tribes more flexibility. While similar in
intent, it would not exclude tribes from current and on-going OHCS programs and opportunities.

The new BAFI-NATO structure would create a dedicated and on-going funding opportunity for the nine federally recognized tribes of
Oregon with the intention that it will span across multiple biennia. A resource of this nature is critical to address tribal housing needs
that encompass all areas of housing. This new program structure includes homelessness and prevention funding, affordable rental
housing development and operations, and homeownership programs. This funding would allow for consistent and reliable long-term
planning investments for projects and resources tailored to tribal community needs. It is essential that the state actively engages and
supports tribal communities in a manner that respects tribal sovereignty and self-determination.

Housing Stabilization: Homeless Services and Housing Retention
Tribal Members are overrepresented in current homelessness counts by more than 4x their share of the state population. People who
identify as American Indian or Alaska Native are more likely to experience unsheltered homelessness than other populations in Oregon.
OHCS has also learned through consistent engagement with the nine federally recognized tribes that the data from Homeless
Management Information System (HMIS), Point-in-Time counts (PITC), and Census Bureau surveys does not fully capture the extent
of housing instability in tribal communities. Many of these data collecting efforts do not adequately coordinate with tribal communities
to obtain the most accurate information. Overcrowded or substandard housing circumstances which put tribal members at significant
risk of homelessness are often not reflected in these numbers. While the BAFI-NATO program has provided much-needed funding to
create and/or support local programs designed to prevent and reduce homelessness and housing instability in tribal communities across
Oregon, there is a greater need for ongoing, predictable, and flexible resources, so that tribes can plan for and implement sustainable
programs that have the most long-term impact.

Affordable Rental Housing (ARH) and Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH)
Although Native Americans represent only 1.1% of the Oregon population, 5.1% of the homeless population in Oregon identify as
American Indian or Alaska Native in the last Point-in-Time Count (PITC). It is important to note, that the PITC is just an estimate and
can frequently result in undercounts while failing to capture overcrowded homes, temporary homelessness trends, and other factors that
disproportionately impact Native Americans. This indicates the disproportionate need for safe, stable housing for the Native populations
of the state. In recent years, several different attempts have been made by OHCS’s Affordable Rental Housing Division to target
resources to Tribal Nations, including a 10% set-aside in our 9% Low Income Housing Tax Credit allocation, access to capacity building

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   258
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
resources, and competitive priority across the affordable rental housing resource offerings through OHCS Notices of Funding
Availability. Despite these efforts, very few tribal affordable or permanent supportive housing developments have been funded by
OHCS. The need is there, but there is a disconnect between the way that OHCS’ ARH Division traditionally funds ARH and PSH
developments and what the Oregon tribes need from us to develop more housing. Through outreach, it has become clear that our
programs are too full of bureaucracy, reliant on institutional knowledge, and carry too much upfront risk for tribes to feel comfortable
applying.

Homeownership Programs
In Oregon, only 59% American Indians or Alaska Natives own homes compared to 67% for white Oregonians. Tribes and tribal members
face multifaceted barriers when it comes to homeownership. Access to mortgage products can be difficult depending on the type of land
the home sits on (tribal trust land, tribal fee land, fee simple, etc.). Limited savings for down payment and low credit scores also hinder
mortgage access or make mortgages more expensive. Tribes seeking to build on their own land often face prohibitive infrastructure costs
due to the geography and characteristics of the land. There is limited consistent funding for homeownership development year to year.
OHCS’ main homeownership development funding program currently excludes tribal participation since construction must be done on
Oregon land and the state must maintain a lien on the land.


How Achieved

Tribal communities face substantial barriers to accessing housing stabilization, homeownership development, and affordable rental
housing development funding. While not a comprehensive list, some of the barriers include: staff capacity, hiring difficulty, geographic
barriers, time it takes to finalize contracts, biennial budget barriers, and the requirement to meet expenditure deadlines instead of
spending on actual tribal goals. An expansion of our current funding eligibilities would address most of these barriers by establishing a
long-term regular source of funding that can be spent on tribal housing priorities. Each of the nine federally recognized tribes of Oregon
also have different housing priorities and flexible funding would allow for them to focus on their areas of greatest need.

Through targeted engagement with tribal grantees, OHCS has heard from tribal communities that the solution is not to provide more
and more grants, but to consolidate our offerings into a singular, easy to report and use, program. This is due to several factors including
capacity to apply for, monitor and report out programs, tribal planning needs, and lack of funds to help bridge tribal budgets. The new
BAFI-NATO funding would streamline developments while reducing the administrative burden on tribal staff and partnering with tribes
on their continuum of housing needs. Eligible activities under BAFI-NATO would be expanded to include affordable rental housing
and homeownership activities and are designed to focus on areas of priority based on the needs of each individual tribe.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                    259
                                              BUDGET NARRATIVE
BAFI-NATO Program Structure

Application/Tribal Housing Plan
Applications will be non-competitive with direct awards to each of the nine federally recognized tribes of Oregon based on formula
funding factors. At the beginning of every biennium, Tribes will be required to complete a Tribal Housing Plan which will include the
proposed scope of work over the next two years. The application will largely focus on each individual tribe and their community housing
needs. The application will state their housing plan and incorporate details such as their scope of work, budget, reporting metrics, and
projected outcomes. Tribal Housing Plans will be approved by OHCS staff before the program and funding can be accessed by the
grantee. For housing plans that involve capital development projects, additional requirements and documentation may be required.

Funding Formula/Allocations
Funds for BAFI-NATO will be renewed every biennium in the amount of $12.8 Million for the continual and ongoing investment to
address tribal homelessness and housing production needs. Funding to grantees will be based on a formula method with input from each
of the nine federally recognized tribes of Oregon. For example, in the 21-23 and 23-25 biennia, formula funding was allocated to tribes
with a base amount with an additional factor of tribal enrollment numbers. OHCS will continue to engage with tribal grantees to achieve
input on variables and formula calculations. The formula should be reassessed every biennium as to update tribal enrollment numbers or
any other variables that are associated with the funding allocations. Negotiated Indirect Cost Rates (NICRAs) will be submitted as part
of their Tribal Housing Plan and the primary way of calculating administrative costs for the tribes. Grantees can also choose the standard
administrative rate of 15% in lieu of using their NICRAs.

Program Eligibilities
Eligible uses of the funding will include homeless services and housing retention allowances, affordable housing activities and
homeownership programs. Each of the divisions within OHCS will outline eligible activities and use of funds in detail under each of
these categories in a program manual.

Housing Stabilization activities can include, but are not limited to: Street outreach, shelter or transitional housing acquisition, renovation,
rehabilitation, and conversion projects, operations and wrap-around services of shelter, transitional housing, and supportive housing sites,
homelessness prevention programming, rehousing assistance, long term rental assistance, capacity building, and housing retention, and
eviction prevention programs. All housing stabilization programs can provide financial assistance in the form of rent, utility payments,
move in/out costs and deposits.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                         107BF02
                                                                     260
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
Affordable Rental Housing projects can include, but are not limited to: Capacity building, predevelopment activities, matching funds for
current affordable housing projects that will be completed within the expenditure period (hard and soft costs), fees or charges, rental
assistance, supportive services, operations of affordable rental housing sites, administration and acquisition of properties.

Homeownership programs can include but are not limited to: Pre-development and development of new homes for purchase (only for
projects that will be completed in the expenditure period), mortgage assistance, downpayment assistance, home repairs, foreclosure
avoidance support and resources, and education and counseling services.

Contract Management
The central point of contract administration within OHCS will be the Housing Stabilization Division in consultation with the Equity
Diversion and Inclusion (EDI) Office, the Affordable Rental Housing Division and Homeownership Departments. The program analyst
assigned to BAFI-NATO will oversee the coordination and implementation of the program including the application process, housing
plan review, and will serve as the programmatic contact for the tribes and be responsible for the daily operations of the grant. The Tribal
Liaison (EDI office) will serve as the primary in communicating any program updates, changes and facilitating the monthly OHCS Tribal
Housing Work Group meetings. OHCS fiscal and compliance staff will play a critical role in monitoring the program, ensuring adherence
to program guidance through regular reviews and compliance checks.


Staffing Impact


No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.


Quantifying Results

Outcomes
All tribal grantees are required to submit a comprehensive annual report at the end of each fiscal year. This report must detail the projects
undertaken, goals set, and the extent to which these goals were achieved. The report will include quantitative and qualitative data
including persons served in each of the project areas, successes, challenges, and lessons learned and other requested outcomes depending
on funded project types. The report will demonstrate how funding was used to meet tribal housing needs, while reducing administrative
burdens for the tribes and OHCS.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                        107BF02
                                                                    261
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
To ensure continuous progress and provide necessary support, individual tribes will meet with OHCS program staff for check ins on a
quarterly basis or more frequently upon request of the grantee. These meetings will serve as a platform for tribal representatives to
discuss ongoing projects, seek advice, and receive guidance on upcoming initiatives. Programmatic and fiscal compliance will be
monitored bi-annually. This monitoring will involve a thorough review of both the programmatic elements of the tribal housing
initiatives and their financial management. The aim is to ensure that all funded projects are aligned with the stipulated guidelines and
ensure appropriate spend-down timelines.

Racial Equity Strategy
The intent of the enhanced BAFI-NATO funding is to address deep inequalities that the federally recognized tribes of Oregon face when
it comes to housing and funding opportunities from OHCS. The premise of this ongoing funding stream is to ensure that OHCS meets
its racial equity goals by providing a dedicated investment to a community that has been disproportionately affected by the homelessness
and the unaffordability of housing within the state. OHCS will use tribal housing plans and annual reports to help measure and gauge
success, while also being sensitive to the fact that tribes are unique, sovereign entities that prioritize tribal equity and supporting their
members, families and communities.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The current BAFI-NATO program utilizes the Homelessness Management Information System (HMIS) to track and collect data on
programs created with BAFI-NATO funds. In the 23-25 Biennium (one fiscal year as of this report FY 23-24), BAFI-NATO has served
926 individuals for a total of 427 households. Of these households, 82% are people that identify as American Indian, Alaska Native or
Indigenous, 4% identify as white, 6% are multi-racial, and 1 % Black or African American. Household types served include Families
with Children at a total of 53% of households, Families Without Children at a total of 44% of households, and 3% of household types
are unknown.
The same demographic data sets will be tracked for the 25-27 Biennium and will expand with the additional included program areas of
Affordable Rental Housing and Homeownership activities. OHCS will create a consolidated Annual Report that will encompass all
project areas and activities disaggregated by race and ethnicity.


Administration
Tribes often face multiple barriers when it comes to contracting. This includes rural locations, hesitation from contractors to work on
reservations, increased costs and limited number of contractors. A small number of Oregon tribes operate their own construction or
maintenance teams, many do not. OHCS will continue to support tribes in following their own contracting rules and procurement


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                        107BF02
                                                                    262
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
processes, which may include utilizing and allowing preferences for Native owned businesses. This is in alignment with federal positions
on tribal housing work.

Policy Package # 508 HSD Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

For Housing Stabilization, this package restores 16 permanent full-time positions and transfers a half-time support position from
another program unit to provide essential support for the proposed funding of shelter maintenance, rehousing, rental assistance and
eviction prevention; plus reauthorizes a limited duration full-time position for youth homelessness; and adds two project management
positions to support implementation of homeless services initiatives.

For Energy Services, this package establishes two permanent full-time positions supported by Federal Funds to meet increased
workload and mitigate overtime.

The second part of this policy package provides funding for costs allocated to support the Central Services Division. This program's
portion is represented as an Intrafund transfers out to support a proportionate share of Pkg 508.

Staffing Impact

Quantifying Results
As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly
around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Project-Based Rental Assistance Programs Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:         Healthy and Safe Communities, A Thriving Statewide Economy
Program Contact:              Natasha Detweiler-Daby, Affordable Rental Housing Division Director




Program Overview
The Project-Based Rental Assistance (PBRA) program unit was created with the 2021-23 biennium Agency Request Budget. The PBRA
programs provide rental assistance that is associated with specific housing units rather than awarded to a tenant regardless of where the
person lives. The staff that implement these programs report up to the Director of Affordable Rental Housing.
Program Funding Request
This program unit includes ongoing support for the department’s Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) Initiative. The program was
authorized in 2019-21 and is intentionally designed to serve those experiencing chronic homelessness with permanent housing through
rental assistance and supportive services. Housing development is funded with state and federal affordable rental housing development
funding, including those reflected in the Capital Construction program unit. In this program unit, General Fund is used for the rent and
services, included in the ARB ask are operational staff role additions to scale with the work, continuing the current service levels with

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02

                                                                   276
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
incorporated increases necessary for changes in the market along with expansion of this work to add more permanent supportive housing
units for those experiencing chronic homelessness. In the ARB ask, we are also including an investment package to expand supportive
housing to work in partnership with the Oregon Health Authority and others to identify and implement strategies to provide supportive
housing rent assistance and services to meet the needs of populations at broader acuity levels to support those existing state systems who
may not be chronically homeless.
In addition to the PSH Initiative, OHCS Affordable Rental Housing Division also includes the HUD Contract Administration group,
overseeing federal HUD contracts for project-based rent assisted projects, along with the federal HUD 811 program which provides
resources to funded projects serving Oregon Health Authority and Department of Human Services clients. To support these programs,
the department has also launched Risk Mitigation funds which serve to off-set the insurance impacts associated with serving permanent
supportive housing populations; a 2025 policy package is proposed to expand this effort to a broader cross-section of affordable rental
housing. Only Federal Funds Non-Limited for the HUD Contract Administration are shown in the chart below for the 13-15 and 15-17
biennia. PSH was established in 2019-21. The chart below projects rent and services costs (General Fund) from PSH investments through
2023-25 ARB but does not assume additional investments in construction.

Program Description
The Project-Based Rent Assistance efforts of OHCS are three-fold: HUD Contract Administration (HCA), HUD 811 PRA (HUD 811),
and Permanent Supportive Housing Project-Based Rent Assistance (PSH PBRA). These three discrete efforts serve to provide rent
assistance in connection with the provision of housing.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02

                                                                   277
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
HCA serves as the state’s Performance Based Contract Administrator (PBCA) for project-based Section 8 housing. Each Section 8 project
has a Housing Assistance Payment (HAP) contract which provides the project-based subsidy. HCA performs contract administration
activities for approximately 254 contracts in Oregon. Contract administration includes subsidy payments to owners (pass-through funds),
tenant complaint resolution, HAP contract renewals, rent adjustments, on-site management reviews, and technical support to owners,
managing agents, site staff, and residents.
HUD 811 program resources are awarded to OHCS in order to set aside units in affordable housing projects whose capital costs are
funded through Federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credits, Federal HOME funds, or other state, Federal and local funding sources.
OHCS partners with the Oregon Health Authority and Department of Human Services to identify and refer extremely low-income people
with disabilities to HUD 811 units and ensure their access to long-term services and supports in the community.
PSH PBRA resources are programmed by OHCS into funded, publicly supported affordable housing in order to serve persons requiring
rental assistance and tenancy support services to maintain housing, such as those experiencing chronic homelessness; the PSH initiative
is currently focused on prioritizing PSH for those experiencing chronic homelessness that are referred through their local coordinated
entry process. To support these programs, the department has also launched Risk Mitigation funds which serve to off-set the insurance
impacts associated with serving permanent supportive housing populations; a 2025 policy package is proposed to expand this effort to a
broader cross-section of affordable rental housing.
Program Justification
OHCS’ Project-Based Rent Assistance Programs provide assistance to stabilize very low- and extremely low-income Oregonians to
ensure housing stability. These community members would be unlikely to find housing affordable to them outside of project-based rental
assistance. PSH rental assistance is a key element to successfully housing Oregonians experiencing chronic homelessness.

Funding Streams
Project-Based Rental Assistance Programs are funded by General Fund, Other Funds, and Federal Funds. Other funds are derived from
HUD Contract administration charges. Federal funds are for the HUD Contact Administration.

Comparison of 2025-27 Funding Proposal to 2023-25 Funding
OHCS’s funding request for 2025-27 increases funding for the Permanent Supportive Housing program as more units become occupied
and as programmatic policies need to update with market shifts in cost factors. In addition, Non-Limited Federal Funds are expected to
increase above standard inflation rates as rental costs continue to rise faster than incomes. The payments subsidize the difference between
market rate rents and 30 percent of the tenant’s income.


Project-Based Rental Assistance Programs Description
The Project-Based Rental Assistance (PBRA) programs include HUD Contract Administration (HCA), HUD 811 PRA (HUD 811), and
Permanent Supportive Housing Project-Based Rent Assistance (PSH PBRA). These three discrete efforts serve to provide rent assistance
in connection with the provision of housing.
The HUD Contract Administration section monitors the physical condition, management, and tenant eligibility of projects that receive
funding. A management review is conducted to ensure compliance with regulatory standards, federal regulations, and state rules. The
section also follows up on health and safety issues that are documented through HUD Real Estate Assessment Center (REAC) physical
inspections. Common areas, vacant units ready for occupancy, building exteriors, roofs and landscaping are inspected in conjunction
with representatives from the property management firm responsible for the day-to-day management of the projects.
OHCS serves as the Performance Based Contract Administrator (PBCA) for project-based Section 8 housing in Oregon. OHCS performs
contract administration activities for 252 contracts in HUD Section 8 properties across the state, which equates to 9,720 total units.
Contract administration includes subsidy payments to owners (pass-through funds), tenant complaint resolution, HAP contract renewals,
rent adjustments, on-site management reviews, and technical support to owners, managing agents, site staff, and residents. In addition,
OHCS helps provide information to persons seeking housing who might already be living in Section 8 housing, or who may be
experiencing housing problems.
OHCS receives federal HUD 811 program resources. Through this program, staff work to assign HUD 811 rent assistance to units,
specifically set-aside for extremely low-income people living with disabilities. OHCS coordinates tenant referrals through the Department

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02

                                                                    279
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
of Human Services as well as the Oregon Health Authority. Staff negotiate and oversee applications for HUD 811 resources to ensure
compliance with federal regulations.
PSH PBRA resources are awarded by OHCS in conjunction with development funds for publicly supported affordable housing. These
resources serve those requiring rental assistance and tenancy support services to maintain housing, such as those experiencing chronic
homelessness, which is the focus of the current PSH Initiative. This is a newer program for OHCS, established in the 2019-21 biennium.
The program framework was established in collaboration with advisory groups and the Housing Stability Council. OHCS staff began
making rent assistance payments during the 2021-23 biennium.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                 107BF02

                                                                 280
                                                       BUDGET NARRATIVE
Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level.
Phase-Outs are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27
biennium.

   •   Professional Services 6.8%

match to the agency funding splits for cost allocation purposes. This package also contains the fund shift for lease payments to align funding with the
agency funding.

Policy Package 090 Analyst Adjustments

Package Description

This package increases General Fund by $2.9 million and increases Other Funds expenditure limitation by $0.6 million by eliminating
inflation on certain services and supplies accounts; shifting two positions (1610 and 1611) from General Fund to Other Fund; and shifting
services and supplies for those positions from General Fund to Other Funds. The package also adds $3.5 million for Behavioral Health
housing supports to address challenges related to the Oregon State Hospital Mink-Bowman Order.

Purpose

To support a balanced budget and Governor priorities.

How Achieved

Adjustments to vacancy savings and inflation on services and supplies were made in executive branch agencies statewide.

The Governor’s Budget includes $3.5 million General Fund for behavioral health housing supports including rental assistance, application
fees, eviction prevention, and utilities. The funding is intended to support aid and assist patients assist individuals with behavioral health
issues to transition out of an acute care setting, making way for others in need of care through the Oregon State Hospital system.

Staffing Impact

Program Analyst 3, fund shift from General Fund to Other Funds

Program Analyst 1, fund shift from General Fund to Other Funds

Revenue Sources

General Funds: $2,873,318

Policy Package # 508 PBRA Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

For the Performance-Based Contract Administration program, adds a Program Analyst 1 moved from Affordable Rental Housing and
funded with Other Funds resource to support Permanent Supportive Housing assistance as more projects come on-line.

Extends a Program Analyst 2 previously authorized to support administration of HB 3395 (2023) for agriculture housing and
affordable homeownership loans. This position was shifted to Performance-Based Contract Administration program in error and
should be in Affordable Rental Housing. The remaining part of this policy package provides funding for costs allocated to support the
Central Services Division. This program's portion is represented as an interfund transfer out to support a proportionate share of Pkg
508.

Staffing Impact

Program Analyst 2, Permanent Full Time
Program Analyst 1, Permanent Full Time

Quantifying Results
As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly

around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Policy Package # 513 Permanent Supportive Housing – CSL Program Improvements

Package Description
This package addresses the complex needs of people experiencing chronic homelessness by proposing an increase to the services funding
component of the PSH Program in OHCS’s Affordable Rental Housing Division (ARH). The PSH program provides capital funding for
development of permanent housing along with long term project-based rental assistance and supportive services for tenants, which
enables housing providers to effectively serve Oregonians with histories of long-term homelessness and disabling conditions. This
complex bundling of resources has proven to be effective and should continue to be delivered through a coordinated process at OHCS.

This package prioritizes the improvement of the PSH program by addressing an outstanding need of OHCS-funded PSH projects:
insufficient resources for PSH-level services. The package proposes to increase the amount of services funding a PSH project can utilize
in order to ensure that levels of funding are appropriate to project need.

Purpose

Through ongoing engagement with PSH stakeholders, including developers, investors, funders, and service providers, OHCS has
identified that many PSH projects have insufficient resources for the ongoing operation of their PSH services program. Reports have
been published identifying the rising costs of PSH operations and the critical importance of properly resourcing PSH properties. Since
the inception of the PSH Program at OHCS in 2019, levels for services funding for PSH projects have not increased and projects
continue to be funded at $10,000 per unit per year (the estimated required per unit per annum amount for PSH generated from studies
conducted in 2016). Particularly for projects operating in the Metro region, PSH stakeholders are now reporting many PSH units
require $15,000 per unit per year or more to ensure their programs maintain appropriate staffing levels and effectively serve PSH
households who are in many cases presenting much higher acuity levels than eight years ago.

How Achieved

Increase total resource to support up to $15,000 per unit per year (PUPY) for services in OHCS-funded PSH Projects.

 Services funding for PSH will continue to be awarded through the ARH ORCA process to eligible project sponsors. Increases in
resources will be offered to all previously funded PSH projects along with projects to be awarded in 2025-27.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  299
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
Staffing Impact

No staff proposed directly in this package, however the position requested in package 508 OHCS Operation Support are in response to
the maintenance of effort staffing levels needed to support this work.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
By increasing the CSL levels for PSH services funding, OHCS will be able to stabilize services for all currently funded and future
funded PSH projects. Stabilization means the asset will be able to continue to deliver PSH for chronically homeless Oregonians for its
affordability period. Stabilization means the PSH programs operating at the PSH sites will be able to hire and retain more qualified
staff and offer more comprehensive services, resulting in more sustainable programming and increased housing retention and wellness
for tenants. Currently 832 PSH units are funded with PSH resources, with another 460 anticipated over 2025-2027. We currently are
seeing around an 82% retention rate. With increased resources, PSH projects can achieve more and increase the retention rate to
between 85% and 90%.
OHCS has also heard from stakeholders that without increased resources for PSH operations, service providers and property managers
are likely to stop signing on to these projects. By increasing funding, we can ensure PSH providers and managers continue to provide
critical partnerships in PSH development and operation.
Both of these outcomes drive towards meeting the Governor’s priorities of decreasing homelessness and increasing affordable housing
development.
Racial Equity Strategy
Homelessness disproportionally impacts households of color compared to white households, and data shows that without intentionality
in addressing racial issues in the homeless services sector, more white households are referred to PSH than BIPOC households. Much
of the work needing to be done exists within the CoCs, however, funding requirements can drive to better outcomes for households of
color in accessing PSH and achieving stability and wellness. Funding set-asides and priorities will continue to exist in the ORCA
process for culturally specific organizations which can also drive to better outcomes for BIPOC households. Increasing funding for all
PSH providers will benefit the funded CSOs and the households they serve, seeing increased funding for culturally specific services
and better outcomes for households of color living in PSH.
The ORCA also has equity and racial justice requirements for outreach, design, and operation of developments that all funded projects
must meet to ensure that developments are serving the needs of intended populations. This also serves to ensure that housing


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                 300
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
developed is accessible to persons who have historically been least likely to apply, including persons of color. Projects must also
discuss how specific project components are designed to maximize respect for and relevance to the beliefs, practices, and cultural
needs of the communities being served as well as the project’s capacity to ensure that tenant services are delivered in a culturally
responsive way. In addition, PSH projects are required to incorporate culturally specific services in developments either through
tenancy support services offered by the development’s service provider or through referral.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
PSH projects include accountability for racial equity approaches in the following ways:
   • Projects developed with PSH resources are required to meet MWESB goals
   • Culturally specific services and tenancy supports are included in the tenancy support services contract
   • Developments submit an annual report to OHCS discussing the measures in place to ensure that persons of color and
      underserved communities have equitable access to PSH units and the barriers that exist in making this happen
   • Annual onsite monitoring also tracks the actions being taken for equity in PSH units


All PSH projects funded by OHCS are required to utilize HMIS to track tenant retention, along with other outcomes such as exits, exit
destinations, and income attainment. Households’ racial and ethnicity information is collected in HMIS. Therefore, OHCS can
continue to evaluate improvements to access and retention for PSH households of color.
Administration
The PSH Program conducts outreach to CSO partners to join the Supportive Housing Institute, facilitating their eligibility for PSH
funding and increasing the level of CSO participation in the PSH Program. The PSH Program will continue to do this work and
continue to prioritize projects partnering with CSOs and focusing on serving households of color and/or other marginalized
communities.
OHCS has requirements in place for MWESB involvement in the construction of all units, including PSH units where services funding
will be used. MWESB involvement is tracked through regular reporting during construction. OHCS uses this data to determine if
developers are meeting requirements and if not, they are required to create a plan to meet requirements in future developments and in
some cases will be required by OHCS to utilize a third-party consultant to meet requirements.

Revenue Sources

General Fund: $8,550,000


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   301
                                BUDGET NARRATIVE




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget              107BF02
                                        302
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE

Policy Package # 516 Permanent Supportive Housing Expansion for Rent Assistance and
Services
Package Description
This package proposes an expansion of the Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) Program rent assistance and services funding
components by adding an additional 400 slots to the PSH Program’s current resources.

Purpose

PSH is affordable housing paired with comprehensive tenancy support services and rent assistance. It is designed to best serve
households exiting chronic homelessness (long term homelessness with the presence of complex disabilities and needs). The PSH
Program at OHCS provides development capital for PSH projects across Oregon, along with ongoing project-based rent assistance and
tenancy support services funding. The PSH Program continues to be oversubscribed as housing providers around the state work to
keep up with the need to support PSH-eligible populations. The call for more PSH has resulted in the increase of development capital
for the PSH Program ($50M to $80M in the 25-27 biennium). An increase in rent assistance and service resources is needed to match
the increase in units that come from more development funding to ensure all funded PSH projects have the resources needed to be
viable and successful for the long-term.

How Achieved
Add another 400 slots to the current resources to be offered via the ARH ORCA process.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
This policy package will serve an additional 400 PSH households through the PSH Program with project-based rent assistance and
PSH-level tenancy support services. With increases in PSH resources for projects, we expect to see an 85%-90% retention rate (an
increase from 82%) of permanent housing for PSH households, including for these additional 400 slots. Increasing the number of PSH


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                107BF02
                                                                303
                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
units brings the State closer to meeting the Governor’s priorities and OHCS’ goals of decreasing homelessness and increasing the
number of affordable rental housing units.

Racial Equity Strategy
Homelessness disproportionally impacts households of color compared to white households, and data shows that without intentionality
in addressing racial issues in the homeless services sector, more white households are referred to PSH than BIPOC households. Much
of the work needing to be done exists at the local level within homeless Continuums of Care (CoCs), however, funding requirements
can drive to better outcomes for households of color in accessing PSH and in achieving stability and wellness. Funding set-asides and
priorities are part of the ORCA process for culturally specific organizations (CSOs), which is the path for accessing these resources.
Increasing the amount of PSH resources will benefit the chronically homeless population, lead to reduced houselessness, is less costly
than providing services to households when they are unhoused and is morally right and just.
The ORCA also has equity and racial justice requirements for outreach, design, and operation of developments that all funded projects
must meet to ensure that developments are serving the needs of intended populations. This also serves to ensure that housing
developed is accessible to persons who have historically been least likely to apply, including persons of color. Projects must also
discuss how specific project components are designed to maximize respect for and relevance to the beliefs, practices, and cultural
needs of the communities being served as well as the project’s capacity to ensure that tenancy support services are delivered in a
culturally responsive way. In addition, PSH projects are required to incorporate culturally specific services in developments either
through tenancy support services offered by the development’s service provider or through referral.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
PSH projects include accountability for racial equity approaches in the following ways:
   • Projects developed with PSH resources are required to meet MWESB goals
   • Culturally specific services and tenancy supports are included in the tenancy support services contract
   • Developments submit an annual report to OHCS discussing the measures in place to ensure that persons of color and
      underserved communities have equitable access to PSH units and the barriers that exist in making this happen
   • Annual onsite monitoring also tracks the actions being taken for equity in PSH units
All PSH projects funded by OHCS are required to utilize HMIS to track tenant retention, along with other outcomes such as exits, exit
destinations, and income attainment. Households’ racial and ethnicity information is collected in HMIS. Therefore, OHCS can
continue to evaluate improvements to access and retention for PSH households of color.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                 304
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
Administration
The PSH Program conducts outreach to CSO partners to join the Supportive Housing Institute, facilitating their eligibility for PSH
funding and increasing the level of CSO participation in the PSH Program. The PSH Program will continue to do this work and
continue to prioritize projects partnering with CSOs and focusing on serving households of color and/or other marginalized
communities.
OHCS has requirements in place for MWESB involvement in the construction of all units, including PSH units where these additional
rent assistance and tenancy support services slots will be used. MWESB involvement is tracked through regular reporting during
construction. OHCS uses this data to determine if developers are meeting requirements and if not, they are required to create a plan to
meet requirements in future developments and in some cases will be required by OHCS to utilize a third-party consultant to meet
requirements.

Revenue Sources

General Funds: $2,500,000

Affordable Rental Housing (a.k.a. Multifamily) Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:   Healthy and Safe Communities, Housing Access & Affordability for All Oregonians
Program Contact:        Natasha Detweiler-Daby

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                     107BF02
                                                        317
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE

Program Overview
OHCS finances affordable rental housing for low and moderate income and at-risk Oregonians through the administration of federal
and state-funded affordable rental housing resources. The resources assist in the development of new affordable housing units, acquiring
market rate housing and converting it to affordable, acquisition and rehabilitation of existing affordable housing units, and preserving
affordable housing properties, including those with project-based federal rental subsidies.




Program Funding Request
Oregon Housing and Community Services’ (OHCS) funding for 2025-27 reflects the continuation of strategic investments in
demonstrated successes of the Article XI-Q bonds for capital construction, expansion and scaling of predevelopment investments and
land acquisition programs to meet needs in alignment with statewide housing needs, resources to expand the predictability around and
to respond to known demand for preservation resources to meet the need for expiring and at-risk properties, expansion of culturally
responsive property management supports and training, and ensuring broader reach of the new Co-Location of Affordable Rental
Housing with Early Learning and Day Care (CARE) Initiative.

Program Description
OHCS facilitates the increased availability of safe, decent, affordable housing for low- and moderate-income Oregonians by
administering federal and state-funded multifamily rental housing resources. These programs help fund the development of new
affordable units, the preservation of current units within the affordable housing inventory but at risk of loss and the acquisition and
rehabilitation of existing rental units; together these rental homes serve low- and moderate-income working families, formerly homeless,
special needs populations, veterans, and many others in need of housing. Funding occurs by combining resources that may include low-
income housing tax credits, low interest loans (including tax-exempt bond financing), grants, and tax incentives, which are almost always
combined with private-financing tools.
Resources are provided through a newly established rolling centralized application process. The application process incorporates policy
objectives including ensuring that funds are targeted for projects with strong affordability and linked to unique local community needs.
Projects are required to meet standards that include but are not limited to: equity and racial justice strategies, statewide housing plan
priorities, cost controls, and other policy and programmatic requirements as applicable. Funding is also tied to appropriate resident
services that are designed to ensure housing stability and meet the needs of the most vulnerable residents. Examples of resident services
include health care referral programs, after school activities, health care services, mental health, and meal programs.
Program Justification
Affordable rental housing programs help ensure that all Oregonians have access to housing at an affordable rent rate; housing stability
has been proven to provide broad benefits to families and communities, including reducing stress and financial hardship, supporting
educational achievement and job performance. In 2023 Governor Kotek declared a housing emergency and issued a series of Executive
Orders to support housing needs. Governor Kotek also established a housing production goal of 36,000 units annually of which 18,000
should be affordable to households at or below 80% Area median Income OHCS' affordable rental housing programs serve to increase
the supply of affordable rental housing for all Oregonians.

Program Performance
The number of units developed, rehabilitated, preserved, or acquired each year is the primary performance measure for Multifamily
Programs. The chart below outlines data from the biennium 2009-11 to present. In 2023, OHCS announced that it had met its five-year
goal in the Statewide Housing Plan to increase the affordable rental housing pipeline to 25,000 units. Governor Kotek set a new Goal
of 18,000 affordable housing units needed each year beginning in 2023 and OHCS has continued to work to streamline processes and
increase production as the Legislature has made resources available to support affordable housing development.

Even with the increases in resources over the past few biennia, the need for affordable rental housing units is significantly greater than
the resources available through OHCS. This is demonstrated by the very low vacancy rates at the projects funded by OHCS, the
oversubscription of funding requests in our offerings and by the long wait lists for subsidized housing and Section 8 project-based rental
assistance.

Funding Streams

Revenue sources for the Multifamily Division (Affordable Rental Housing) is General Fund, Other Funds and Federal Funds. Other
funds are derived from several funding sources. Including loan repayments from various programs; compliance, application, and
administration charges. As well as public purpose charges paid by PGE and PAC for the Housing Development Guarantee - Pre-
Development program. Revenue is received from document recording fees transferred in from Department of Revenue supports
General Housing Account Program (GHAP). The division receives interest earning from its various programs. Federal funds consist of
grants for National Housing Trust Fund and the Home Investment Partnership programs.


Comparison of Funding 2025 - 27 Proposal to 2023-25 Funding

OHCS’ 2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget increases funding above the Current Service Level for additional staffing, and one-
time General Fund and Article XI-Q bond investments to support affordable housing preservation, permanent supportive housing, Local
Innovation and Fast Track (LIFT) new unit development, and requests resources to support and sustain efforts for the co-location of
affordable rental housing with early learning and day care centers (CARE initiative) , Predevelopment, and Land Acquisition Programs.
Additionally, it seeks resources to expand the data and predictability for preservation resources along with capacity for expanding
property management workforce and tools for a growing affordable rental housing supply.

Affordable Rental Housing Programs Description
The Affordable Rental Housing Programs provide housing options for low and moderate income and at-risk Oregonians through
administration of federal and state-funded affordable rental housing resources. These resources assist in the development of new housing
units, acquisition and rehabilitation of existing housing units, and preserving affordable housing properties, including those with project-
based federal rental subsidies.
The need for affordable rental housing units is significantly greater than the resources available through OHCS. This is demonstrated
by the very low vacancy rates at the projects funded by OHCS, the oversubscription of funding requests in our offerings and by the long
wait lists for subsidized housing and Section 8 project-based rental assistance. The department’s efforts in funding new construction,
acquisition and rehabilitation, and preservation of affordable rental housing help address the significant need throughout the state.
OHCS may allocate multiple sources of federal and/or state resources to a given project that is seeking funds from the State, to maximize
leverage and ensure project viability. In exchange for the public investment in the construction, preservation or rehabilitation of rental
units, property owners are required to maintain long-term affordability of the units. This is documented through a regulatory agreement
that is recorded against the real property. Through the allocation of Federal Low Income Housing Tax Credits (LIHTC), low-interest
loan programs, grants, and other tax credits, OHCS works in cooperation with local partners and the private sector to invest resources
necessary to successfully develop and preserve affordable housing throughout Oregon.
Reduced borrowing costs to developers, through various development tools including but not limited to: low interest loans, gap funding,
equity leveraged through federal LIHTC, and other financing tools, can result in the ability to provide monthly rents lower than those
available in the private sector for qualified, low- and moderate-income tenants. Affordable rent provides individuals and families with
more residual income to cover critical household expenses. One prominent study found that “working families paying 30 percent or less
of their income for housing were able to dedicate more than twice as much of their income to health care and insurance as those paying
50 percent or more for housing.”
Given the regulatory compliance requirements that come with the federal and state resources, OHCS’ Portfolio Administration section
monitors the physical condition, management, and tenant eligibility of projects that have received funding. Properties provide reporting
to OHCS annually and are inspected at least every one to three years. A review of management is also conducted to ensure compliance
with regulatory standards, federal regulations, and state rules. Individual units, common areas, building exteriors, roofs and landscaping
are inspected in conjunction with representatives from the property management firm responsible for the day-to-day management of the
projects.
For affordable rental housing programs to be most effective, OHCS partners with individuals, community development corporations,
housing authorities, financial institutions, and other entities. Associated cost drivers for administration of these programs include
programmatic data tracking and reporting, compliance monitoring, financial review (both at the time of development and as asset
management through the regulatory compliance period), underwriting, contract renewals, and tenant complaint resolution. Limitations

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                    323
                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
of federal Private Activity Bond also impact affordable housing development and have further complicated efficiently leveraging
funds. Processes associated with affordable housing rental programs and policies are continuously under evaluation in an effort to
identify more efficient delivery methods, improve performance, and realize meaningful outcomes, given the scarce resources and high
demand.

Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level.
Phase-Outs are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27
biennium.

Policy Package # 508 ARH Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

For Affordable Rental Housing added six positions that were needed support the agency’s programs are listed below. This package
extends a Program Analyst 2 position previously authorized to support HB 3395 (2023) for Network for Oregon Affordable Housing
(NOAH). This position was moved to Project Based Contract Administration in error. The second part of this policy package provides
funding for costs allocated to support the Central Services Division. This program's portion is represented as an interfund transfer out
to support a proportionate share of Pkg 508.

Staffing Impact

Grants, Loans & Program Manager 1, Permanent Full Time
Compliance and Regulatory Manager 1, Permanent Full Time
Compliance and Regulatory Manager 2, Permanent Full Time
Consultant Advisor 2, Permanent Full Time

Quantifying Results

As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly
around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Policy Package # 509 Information Technology Support
Package Description -

This policy option package has two components - the Grant Management System and ProLink.

GMS: This policy option package will support modernization of grants management, improving the agency’s ability to deliver funds,
track and manage grants, and provide reports to track services and financial spending more effectively. A more comprehensive,
modern grants management system would enable the agency to receive and deliver funds more effectively and efficiently, allowing us
to provide service to our partners more easily. Current grant management processes are inconsistent and rely on a combination of
complex spreadsheets and proprietary applications. . This package will allow OHCS to create and implement a system that addresses
the challenges associated with delivering funds and ease our partners' burdens and challenges in reporting on their use of funds. The
evolving scope and scale of grants management necessitate . the modernization of proprietary software solutions on a cloud-based
platform leading to improved performance, better user experience, and enhanced security. ProLink: ProLink HFA is the established
data system of record for the Affordable Rental Housing Division. The plan is to keep this system in full use while supporting
improved functionality of ProLink HFA. This requires implementing needed system enhancements that will improve the user
experience and data quality overall. This package will allow OHCS to continue the use of the ARH data systems and continue to
address the challenges associated with delivering funds and monitoring partner performance while ensuring data integrity. The
growing scale of funds delivery for affordable rental housing requires the maintenance and enhancement of our established data
systems. This includes covering the known annual costs, intentional planning for known needed system enhancements and preparation
for contingencies that may arise.

Purpose

GMS: A priority in our Statewide Housing Plan is to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and
systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity. We have determined that
using disjointed grant management systems and spreadsheets is not equitable for partners across programmatic divisions of the
agency, and OHCS needs to invest in resources to remove the barriers by streamlining grant management. OHCS is not only growing
in agency staff, but also in the programs and dollars funneled through the agency. It is crucial that we invest in systems that can
adequately manage the increasing number and complexity of grants for the long term. New programs, including the emergency
response effort, rapid organizational growth rate, and an increasing number of partner organizations to deliver grants in alignment with
legislative direction, have highlighted the need to modernize and enhance how we manage the entire grant process.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  347
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Investment in our proprietary software will allow us to implement a comprehensive system to ensure our efforts remain consistent and
sustainable as we grow. We are fortunate to have skilled developers to provide much of our needs historically, but we must
proactively mitigate risks associated with the use aging architecture by shifting to a cloud platform that will allow for greater
flexibility in enhancements to the applications. This shift will allow for continuation of operations in the event of outages to the State
Network during an emergency. Unprecedented emergency response efforts have illustrated the need to be agile and adaptive. This
package will enable OHCS to build resiliency in such circumstances.

ProLink: Continued investment in the established data system will allow us to ensure our efforts remain consistent and sustainable as
we grow. We have been fortunate to have obtained ProLink HFA following direction from a Secretary of State audit finding to reduce
the use of ad hoc data tracking tools and custom systems that use aging architecture. Unprecedented emergency response efforts in
affordable rental housing production have illustrated the need to be agile and adaptive. This package will enable OHCS to maintain
resiliency in such circumstances.

How Achieved

GMS: OHCS will use existing funds from flexible resources and administrative allowances from program funds, if available, to
develop this system. Enterprise Information Services is aware of our decision to move from Oracle into the cloud platform This
package will enable many improvements in delivering essential housing-related grants to Oregonians in this biennium (25-27) and
beyond, including:

Outreach and engagement opportunities – Communication could be significantly streamlined, allowing more diverse stakeholders to
participate in various points of the granting process. Configurable portals would allow OHCS to collaborate more efficiently, and
marketing tools would enable us to reach more Oregonians in less time.

Standardization and automation opportunities – Several tasks are currently done manually in various systems that could be
standardized, which would reduce duplication of efforts and create a system of record, as well as reducing opportunities for errors.
Automating processes can free up staff to spend more time delivering services and less time compiling disjointed information for
review and analysis.

Increased compliance and transparency – Data could be more accessible via modern connections to other record systems with APIs.
Easily configurable permissions would give stakeholders appropriate views into relevant data and statuses. A modernized solution
would provide more opportunities to track regulatory compliance obligations effectively.

                                                                   348
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Improved planning capabilities – Better tools to manage resources, documents, and data would enable OHCS to better plan and act
quickly. This investment would also help us to identify, monitor, and mitigate risks associated with our core business processes.

Alignment with broader State of Oregon Enterprise Information Services (EIS) goals, strategies, and values - Process overviews and
reporting functionality will increase accountability, a core EIS value. Portals for internal and external users will make us more
customer-focused and collaborative. Adoption of a modernized grants management system is aligned with the cloud-first approach to
IT investments and will increase a data-informed culture.

Enhanced abilities to evaluate and measure grant performance – The desired solution would allow the ability to integrate desired
outcomes into applications, reports, and dashboards that display progress toward goals.

OHCS has developed an implementation plan for the grant management modernization in this investment request. To achieve these
goals, the following activities must take place:

   1.Appropriate resource allocation for project management and project members. This includes the
          a. initial project planning to deliver a lift and shift minimum viable product
          b. stakeholder engagement,
          c. agile development schedules
          d. Testing plans, QA
   2. Begin the process of enhancing the base software product in the final quarter of 2025. We will use the agile methodology to
       develop the software product with a heavy focus on user experience (UX) and functionality.

        to enter all compliance audit details and tenant file findings and then generate the report. This will improve the overall process

include scope research and costing estimates for each requested CR and project management by ProLink and Data Systems Team
from start to implementation and ongoing system management.

Staffing Impact

Quantifying Results

GMS: A new grants management system will allow OHCS to integrate internal systems to better determine who we are serving, the
services provided, and with what funding sources. This will allow OHCS to build the system infrastructure and improve the efficacy
of our programs. It will also provide the ability to track how quickly funds are disbursed to partners, which will enable us to evaluate
and measure grantee performance. In addition to internal efficiencies, the system will improve access to grants, uploading required
documentation, and reporting for grantees.

ProLink: This data system will allow OHCS to maintain internal systems and data integrity to better determine who we are serving,
the affordable rental housing needs, and with what funding sources. This will allow OHCS to build the system infrastructure and
improve the efficacy of our programs. It will also provide the ability to track how quickly funds are disbursed to partners, which will


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   351
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
enable us to evaluate and measure partner performance. In addition to internal efficiencies, the system will continue to improve
informative data for all OHCS purposes associated with rental housing production and maintenance

Equity and Racial Justice Impact

GMS: Equity and Racial Justice is a guiding principle of Oregon’s Statewide Housing Plan. A modern grants management system
would improve our ability to deliver on goals to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and
systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity. Our existing grants
management solutions are not designed to facilitate these goals, especially in a manner that reflects their urgency and importance. We
are not alone in these goals, and that is evidenced by the growing number of existing grants management software solutions which
contain components specifically tooled to identify and address inequities among various demographics. Better grant monitoring will
also improve accountability measures for grantees, allowing OHCS to identify and intervene if grantee service trends indicate a barrier
to delivery on these goals. We must have systems in place to collect appropriate information, track outcomes, and analyze
deficiencies. This package will give us the needed system improvements to do just that.

ProLink: Equity and Racial Justice is a guiding principle of Oregon’s Statewide Housing Plan. Maintaining and enhancing ProLink
HFA data systems will continue to improve our ability to deliver on goals to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and
addressing institutional and systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic
prosperity. This data system will also continue to improve accountability measures for grantees, allowing OHCS to identify and
intervene if delivery of funds for affordable rental housing trends indicate a barrier to delivery on these goals. We must have systems
in place to collect appropriate information, track outcomes, and analyze deficiencies. This package will give continue to provide
OHCS the needed system with improvements to do just that.
