Policy Package # 519 Preservation – Rental Risk of Loss, Expirations and Physical/Financial
Package Description
The State of Oregon is experiencing a housing crisis and has mobilized historic levels of state resources to address the profound, ongoing
need for affordable rental housing. As we continue developing new affordable housing at an unprecedented pace, it is essential and cost-
effective to ensure we preserve and maintain the current affordable housing stock.
Preserving existing rent-restricted housing that is affordable and well-maintained over the long term, is as important as building new
affordable housing. This package request proposes an increase to the State’s investment in preservation of affordable housing to better
meet the scale of current preservation needs, keeping thousands of rental housing units affordable and well maintained for decades to
come.

Purpose

This request is based on OHCS’ best estimate of the known preservation need, based upon data about projects that could lose
affordability restrictions or federal project-based rent assistance in the next few years, and the best information we have about projects
at risk of loss for other reasons. The $160 million request does not include manufactured home park preservation, which is reflected in
policy package #520.

How Achieved

OHCS’ ongoing preservation strategy aims to help its partners plan for three distinct sets of challenges related to the affordability of
subsidized multifamily rental properties, to match the growing demand for preservation resources throughout the state of Oregon:

      •   Projects with expiring federal rent assistance

Projects eligible under this category will require an extension or renewal of long-term project-based federal rent assistance contracts.
Based upon the most recent estimation, there remain 47 relevant projects and 1,363 relevant units across the state, that are at risk of
losing their federal rent assistance over the next 5 years. Federal rent assistance is one of the most valuable homeless prevention tools
the federal government provides and losing these developments also results in losing the rent assistance forever.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02

                                                                   354
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
      •   Portfolio Stabilization

In addition to affordable units under federal contract, there are additional affordable housing programs which currently support an
estimated 33 projects and 2,289 estimated units, which are projected to expire in the next 5 years. The requested dedicated
preservation funding prioritizes projects and units outside of federal project-based sources that are at risk of imminent loss.

      •   Affordable properties at risk of loss due to urgent physical or financial challenges

Eligible projects under this program are properties that require urgent repair needs or have experienced operational losses which can’t
be addressed through existing reserves or cash flow, and which often threatens the housing stability and/or health of tenant families.
Known projects statewide with significant deferred rehab and/or operational challenges include but are not limited to:

   ✓ 30 known public housing authority-owned properties overdue for rehab, and/or planned for recapitalization as part of the HUD
     RAD program, totaling nearly 1,500 units.
   ✓ 90 known aging affordable properties owned by nonprofit partners with serious rehab needs, totaling nearly 5,000 units.

OHCS documents the range and extent of subsidized affordable housing in Oregon and maintains availability of such data through the
updated preservation dashboard on the OHCS preservation website, and the Oregon Affordable Housing Inventory.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
OHCS estimates that dedicating $142 million toward properties losing affordability restrictions between 2026 – 2029 will preserve
roughly half of the for-profit owned properties: approximately $125,000/unit (1,150 units preserved).

With $38 million targeting properties that could lose federal project-based rent assistance, about 380 units PBRA will be preserved.

$170 million for PHA-owned and nonprofit-owned affordable properties with significant rehab needs is estimated to preserve 6,800
current units.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02

                                                                   355
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE

Equity and Racial Justice (ERJ) is a core priority in all OHCS programs, as expressed in the Statewide Housing Plan, which directs
OHCS to “Advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and systemic barriers that have created and
perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity.” By its nature, Preservation focuses on protecting existing
vulnerable tenants. Therefore, OHCS Preservation efforts will continue to incorporate sustained and thoughtful attention to ERJ as a
high priority.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes

The 2024 Preservation Framework focuses resources on the most vulnerable tenants, including those with severe housing burdens, and
those who identify as a racial or ethnic minority, disabled, or elderly. Baseline data around recent performance in these areas are now
being tracked. With the advent of the ORCA process, comes a new “managed Queue” approach, which collects improved owner and
tenant data to better understand the full range of potential preservation needs. In short, this new protocol collects more information on
specific urgent rehabilitation needs, impact to vulnerable populations, and prioritizes stakeholders that meet the definition of OHCS
culturally specific organizations (CSOs) and culturally responsive organizations (CROs).
        1. Impact on tenants: measuring and tracking how Preservation efforts benefit the housing stability of BIPOC tenants.
        2. Supporting and prioritizing CSOs and culturally responsive organizations CROs: making sure that organizations rooted in
            and serving BIPOC communities have equitable access to OHCS Preservation resources and are prioritized.
Administration
These additional resources will allow more projects to move through the ORCA process and receive OHCS capital resources, which
have MWESB requirements that all projects must meet.

Policy Package #520 Preservation - Manufactured Park
Package Description
Provide resources to Manufactured Parks at risk of loss. OHCS would work with a third party to support efforts to purchase parks
through a mission-based nonprofit or a resident cooperative. Manufactured Parks are an important source of naturally occurring
affordable housing and need to be preserved wherever possible.

Purpose

Manufactured home parks are one of the most prevalent sources of naturally occurring affordable housing in the state, but they are
often unregulated and driven by market forces that can lead to sale and redevelopment without support. Manufactured home parks are
particularly vulnerable as the homeowner does not own the land that their home is on, yet their home is generally not mobile.
Therefore, if a park owner decides to sell the land, the homeowner is extremely vulnerable. These resources allow OHCS to support
interventions that result in park cooperatives or sales to non-profit mission-based owners that seek to maintain the property for parks.
Often interventions include infrastructure upgrades and other health and safety interventions.

How Achieved

OHCS has financed the acquisition of manufactured home parks by residents of the park through nonprofits that have worked through
technical assistance and a cooperative model or ownership by a mission-based nonprofit that leads to longer term affordability with
greater regulation and restrictions in place to ensure the park remains affordable. This POP would provide resources specifically for
this purpose, ensuring expansion of this model and greater preservation of these critical assets statewide.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF2
                                                                  358
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
This policy package will help expand OHCS’s ability to fund the preservation of a critical and often overlooked affordable housing
stock to ensure it remains available, accessible, and affordable to low-income families.
Staff project a total of 7-10 parks, representing roughly 20-70 spaces each, could be preserved with this level of resource. These
estimates are based on past performance, total number of spaces in a given park, and infrastructure upgrade cost trends.
Racial Equity Strategy
By investing OHCS dollars into these properties that currently have no state regulation, oversight, or restrictions, we will be better
able to track what disparities exist in these types of properties and require both the high level and specific requirements around
equitable access that we do for all of our funding sources.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
Our agency collects baseline demographic information for all of our programs that have affordability restrictions and compliance
components. This includes a requirement to report race and ethnicity of tenants. By expanding our funding for MHPs, OHCS will
better be able to understand, track, and evaluate outcomes related to tenant demographics in these communities to an extent that is
challenging for this universe of property types given the lack of existing compliance or restrictions in place.
Administration

These dollars would be distributed through our general ORCA process, which incorporates standards around MWESB contracting and
has standards and incentives around working with culturally specific and responsive organizations, as well as set aside resources for
CSOs and nonprofits developing in rural areas.

Revenue Sources

Other Fund:
     Lottery Revenue Bonds: $25,000,000

Lottery Funds:
A corresponding policy package #520 in the Bond Activities and Debt Service program reflects the Lottery Funds needed to support
debt service and cost of issuance payments associated with this bond request.

Policy Package #530 Culturally Responsive Property Management
Package Description
This request is for $3M in grant resources to better meet the needs of Oregonians by fostering the growth and availability of Culturally
Responsive Property Management for affordable rental housing properties. The property management industry has navigated significant
challenges in recent years with capacity and resources for supporting tenants. The industry needs specific and deliberate investment to
build and support long term expansion in alignment with the Governor’s production goals established in Executive Order 23-04. It is
also critical that this expansion is done in a way that will center tenants and ensure responsive and supportive affordable rental housing
developments that will reduce tenant eviction and displacement.


Purpose

Culturally responsive property management (CR PM) is vital to the long-term success of publicly subsidized housing development
projects and the tenants who call them home. We know that renters belonging to BIPOC communities and Fair Housing protected
classes are more likely to be evicted than white renters in Oregon.1 We also know that many critical social determinants of health,
including economic stability, education, physical and mental health are directly impacted by access to safe and stable housing. CR PM
can help foster a sense of place and belonging for residents and strengthen relationships and understanding between tenants and
property managers. Residents who feel respected and part of the community in publicly supported housing are more likely to remain
in place, and property managers who feel supported with knowledge and tools may be more likely to seek to first understand rather
than punish.2

Additionally, stable occupancy, limited vacancies, and satisfied tenants are significant factors in the long-term financial sustainability
for publicly subsidized housing development projects. Excessive vacancies and Fair Housing violations can jeopardize the contractual
relationship between the Developer or Sponsor and their state, federal, and private funders.

This culturally responsive property management policy option package seeks to clarify and elevate the standards for culturally
responsive property management, facilitate the creation of CR PM training programs, increase available training opportunities, and


1 https://evictionresearch.net/oregon/#:~:text=1%20in%2030%20renting%20households,42%20Latine%20households%20(2.4%25)
2 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30506937/



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02
                                                                   361
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
ultimately reduce the number of evictions and punitive actions experienced by households who identify as BIPOC and protected
classes under Oregon and Federal fair housing rules.

The purpose of this policy option package:
•   Increase capacity for property management statewide
•   Reduce the number of evictions for people from BIPOC and other Fair Housing protected classes
•   Increase placemaking, sense of place, understanding and belonging for tenants from diverse cultures - features known to support
    mental, emotional, social, economic well being
•   Reduce tenant / property manager conflict and evictions stemming from cultural misunderstandings, biases, and
    miscommunication
•   Educate and equip property managers with training resources so they can better support tenants, identify and develop CR policies
    and practices, and have confidence in interactions with tenants, reduce chances of FH complaints and violations.
Stakeholders:

•   Developers of affordable housing, including culturally specific housing developers
•   Owners of affordable housing, including culturally specific housing owners and operators
•   Resident service providers including culturally specific service providers and culturally responsive service providers
•   Property managers
•   Consultants who focus on development of affordable rental housing and/or permanent supportive housing
•   Communities of color and tribal organizations
•   Interested legislators
•   Asset Management
•   EDI and HR/workforce (dev) stakeholders
•   HUD/RD guidance and resources
•   HUD FHEO (guidance/resources as we mention fair housing act protected classes).




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                   362
                                              BUDGET NARRATIVE
Stakeholder engagement driving request:

•   Housing Equity Capacity Building Strategy project findings– Meyer Memorial Trust, HDC, OHCS partnership project (info TBD)
•   Housing Stabilization Community Engagement – (info TBD)
•   Property Managers Working Group (info TBD)

How Achieved
The resources provided through this policy option package will be used to resources for property management investment and
capacity/technical assistance and training resources to create supports based on best practices, likely to include a live and on demand
training curriculum to be used for expanding trained culturally responsive property management.
    1. Identify best practices in CR PM
    2. Build capacity for all PMs to provide culturally responsive services
    3. Update state policies to better support CR PM
    4. Develop internal policies to encourage and/or expect CR PM
    5. Develop capacity so CSOs with PM arm can host/certify trainings?

Outcomes

resources. Internal policies revised and created, resulting in elevated importance and visibility of CR PM in rental housing development and
portfolio management resource programs. Meet immediate need by expediting initial support to new CR PM training programs that are currently
being built by nonprofit CSO Developer and Sponsor entities (eligible for GHAP CB) while legislative process proceeds.

                                                                     363

Expected long-term outcomes: Reduction in number of evictions and punitive action experienced by BIPOC, Fair Housing protected classes, and
tenants in Rural communities. Property managers are better equipped with education and training resources so they can better support tenants,
identify and develop CR policies and practices, and have confidence in interactions with tenants, reduce chances of FH complaints and violations.

Racial Equity Strategy
Racial, Fair Housing group, geographic, economic, and entity type disparities exist in relation to CR PM. This policy package seeks to address
them in the following ways:

•   Reduce the disparity in, as well as the raw number of, evictions and punitive action taken by PMs against tenants from BIPOC, Fair Housing
    groups, and Rural communities by investing in CR PM training programs and developing policies to support adoption of CR PM practices
        o Work closely with subject matter experts and stakeholder groups to inform the project and program
•   Increase workforce development opportunities and economic mobility for individuals from disproportionately impacted groups through
    intentional design of the CR PM grant investment
•   Identify and increase knowledge, clarity, understanding of OHCS expectations and metrics related to CR PM and ensure that development and
    portfolio resource applicants have the information they need to meet expectations
•   Increase internal understanding and analysis of evictions and punitive actions taken against tenants of publicly subsidized housing

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
   • This POP would help expand our ability to define, track, and evaluate this type of
      Identify and increase knowledge, clarity, understanding of OHCS expectations and metrics related to CR PM and ensure that development
      and portfolio resource applicants have the information they need to meet expectations
   • Increase internal understanding and analysis of evictions and punitive actions taken against tenants of publicly subsidized housing

with the intention of supporting existing CR PMs through support of their training programs.

Single Family Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area: Equitably supporting Oregonians to purchase, thrive and stay in their home
Program Contact: Keeble Giscombe




Program Overview
The Homeownership Division consists of four sections, each driven by the central goal of engaging low to moderate income Oregonians who wish to
attain or retain homeownership: Homeownership Programs, Manufactured & Marina Communities, Homeowner Assistance, and Homeownership
Lending. Collectively, these programs expand access to affordable homeownership through competitive market rate mortgages, down payment
assistance, and pre-purchase education and counseling, and by assisting homeowners to retain their homes through education, foreclosure counseling,
and health and safety repairs, including resources specific to manufactured housing. In addition, Homeownership Development Programs increase the
supply of affordable homes for purchase, addressing a dearth in affordable homes available throughout the state. These programs benefit homebuyers
and homeowners who are typically not served by traditional lenders, and who would not otherwise have access to the housing market and
homeownership services.


2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                        374
                                                 BUDGET NARRATIVE



Program Funding Request
OHCS’s funding for 2025-27 includes general fund requests to continue and expand Homeownership Programs. These include funds for the
Homeownership Development Incubator Program to spur the construction of new affordable homes for purchase throughout the state including on
Tribal land. OHCS is also requesting funds to continue replacing manufactured homes that are outdated and unsafe, and to continue the Foreclosure
Avoidance Counseling program which keeps people housed and stable. Additional funding requests include two types of down payment assistance
(DPA): DPA that is deployed by culturally responsive and rural organizations and DPA that is paired with OHCS lending products.

Program Description
To meet OHCS’ Statewide Housing Plan goal of closing Oregon’s racial homeownership gap, the Homeownership Division strives to leverage
homeownership as a powerful tool that can create stability, promote equity, and build generational wealth. The Division consists of four sections, each
driven by the central goal of supporting low- to moderate-income Oregonians to attain and retain homeownership. These sections expand access to
homeownership through funding the construction of affordable homes for purchase, financial products such as competitive market rate mortgages,
down payment assistance, and pre-purchase education and counseling. The Division assists homeowners to maintain and retain their homes through
post-purchase education, foreclosure counseling, foreclosure assistance, health and safety repairs, and resources specific to manufactured housing.
Using an equity and racial justice lens in all programming, policies, and operations, the Homeownership Division works to dismantle systemic housing
barriers and create opportunities for those underrepresented as homeowners in Oregon.
Through the Homeownership Programs Section, the department contracts with nonprofit homeownership centers, local governments, public housing
authorities, and nonprofit organizations that serve as a one stop shop for prospective homeowners and current homeowners. They provide down payment

2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                 107BF02
                                                                         375
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
assistance, homebuyer education, pre and post purchase counseling, foreclosure counseling, and health and safety repair funding. Our updated down
payment assistance program is available to first-time and first-generation homebuyers as well as veterans.
The Homeownership Development Program seeks to increase the supply of affordable homes for purchase by funding the development of housing that
is aligned with community needs. Through the Local Innovations and Fast Track program, Homeownership Development has funded nearly 1000
shared equity homes. Through its newer Homeownership Development Incubator Program, OHCS is able to fund homes on tribal land, pre-development
work, and support new innovative models of affordable homeownership.
Within the Homeownership Lending Section, OHCS offers the development and oversight of two mortgage lending programs: the Oregon Bond
Residential Loan Program and the Flex Lending Program. The Oregon Bond Residential Loan Program uses a network of lenders to finance residential
loans under the program guidelines. OHCS invests in and purchases these loans from the lenders using mortgage revenue bonds (see description in the
Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit). Economic conditions and financial markets affect the success of mortgage revenue bond financed
loans. The recent housing market and program requirements have impacted program production. Higher home prices make it difficult for first time
home buyers, the population served by this program, to purchase homes. OHCS' new Flex Lending program expands the reach of affordable
homeownership lending activities by pairing a flexible mortgage product with down payment assistance by leveraging mortgage-backed security
markets.
The Manufactured & Marina Communities Programs include the Manufactured Homes programs and the Manufactured and Marinas Communities
Resource Center (MMCRC). The Manufactured Home Programs identify creative solutions to expand housing options by preserving and improving
manufactured dwelling parks and collaborating with stakeholders to develop policies and resources that support manufactured housing residents. This
also includes the Manufactured Home Replacement program which provides affordable gap financing for manufactured homeowners to replacing aging
and inefficient manufactured homes. Mediation services are available for manufactured and marina community residents and owners through the
Manufactured and Marinas Communities Resource Center (MMCRC). The program provides impartial assistance to resolve disputes between park
owners and tenants outside of court by promoting cooperation, open communication, and a positive environment.
The Homeowner Assistance Section includes the Homeowner Assistance Fund (HAF), which provides financial assistance for low- to moderate-income
Oregonians who experienced financial hardship related to the coronavirus pandemic, preserving homeownership through mortgage reinstatement by
curing delinquent mortgage and/or assisting with future payments. HAF also connects struggling homeowners to housing counselors and legal aid
services. HAF replaces the very successful mortgage assistance program funded by the Hardest Hit Funds after the last recession.

Program Justification
Homeownership is one of the primary means through which families build wealth and achieve financial stability. However, discriminatory practices
such as redlining, predatory lending, and unequal access to credit have historically excluded many people of color from homeownership opportunities,
leading to significant racial wealth gaps. Promoting affordable homeownership not only enables households to build equity and intergenerational wealth
but also provides a stable and secure environment for raising families and participating in community life. By increasing homeownership rates among

2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                 107BF02
                                                                         376
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
all Oregonians, and in particular, people of color, OHCS’ programs can help to stabilize neighborhoods, reduce crime rates, and enhance property
values, leading to broader economic and social benefits.
Through these investments, OHCS expects to support over 12,000 Oregonians to achieve or maintain their dream of Homeownership. This investment
addresses the gap in supply of affordable homes, and then provides the resources (loan products, DPA, education, counseling) to help people get into
those homes and others in the communities that they want to live. Once they are in a home, OHCS foreclosure prevention resources, manufactured
home resources, and health and safety repairs help them remain in a safe in their home for decades or generation to come. These programs represent
key spokes of homeownership, and this biennium investment will keep the infrastructure that supports these programs going and allow OHCS to better
keep pace with unmet demand across the state.


Program Performance
Key success measures for Homeownership Program performance are the affordable homes for purchase built, number of residential loans financed, the
number of people accessing homeownership centers for homebuyer education, financial literacy counseling, receiving down payment assistance, and
foreclosure counseling, and the ability of homeownership programs to help reduce the minority homeownership gap. Performance data for the last four
biennia and current projections are outlined in the table below or OHCS key performance measures.

Enabling Legislation and Program Authorization
Neither federal law nor the Oregon Constitution mandates housing, homeownership, and/or homeownership retention. However, because housing and
homeownership are indicators of healthy, vital, and stable communities, federal and state policies encourage, promote, finance, and protect housing
and homeownership as follows:

        Authority                                                    Program                                                                 Legislation
   State                     Bond Financing—Residential Loan Program                                                          ORS 456
   State                     Increasing Homeownership, Retention                                                              ORS 456.550, 458.655
   State                     Marinas & Manufactured Communities                                                               ORS 446.515 – 446.547
   State                     Manufactured Home Decommissioning and Replacement Program                                        ORS 458.356-458.358
   State                     Manufactured Dwelling Park Preservation Loan Program                                             ORS 458.352
                                                                                                                              ORS 456.515 – 456.725 & ORS
   State                     Local Innovation & Fast Track
                                                                                                                              458.480-458.490

   Federal                   Neighborhood Stabilization Program                                                               Public Law 111-203

   Federal                   Homeowner Assistance Fund
                                                                                                                              117-2
Funding Streams
Homeownership Programs are funded by Other Funds and Federal Funds as outlined below: Funding for Single Family (Home Ownership) Division
includes General Fund, Other Funds and Federal Funds. Other fund revenue includes Construction Excise Taxes and Manufactured Home Parks annual
fees. The division receives revenue from loan repayments and interest earnings for the various programs. These programs also generate revenue from
the administrative charges which include application, loan origination and administration charges. Federal funds consist of grant for the Neighborhood
Stabilization program.

Comparison of 2025-27 Funding Proposal to 2023-25 Funding

2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                                     378
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
OHCS’s funding for 2025-27 increases above the Current Service Level for the manufactured home park replacement program, down payment funds
tied to OHCS lending programs, down payment funds deployed by culturally responsive and rural organizations, funds to increase the supply of
affordable homes for purchase through the Homeownership Development Incubator Program (HDIP) and LIFT, and foreclosure avoidance counseling.
These programs all existed in the last biennium but will rely on additional allocation to keep them running. This budget request adds an OPA3 position
for the HDIP program to provide outward facing technical assistance and internal operations and compliance support.

The Homeowner Assistance Fund (HAF) program anticipates making its final assistance payments in February 2025 and administratively winding up
in 2025-27; no additional funds are expected but there will be a five-year monitoring period after approved closure. HAF positions may continue into
2025-27 to close the program but will all transition except for one shared lien management position.


Homeownership Programs Description
The Homeownership Division administers federal and state funds to create opportunities for lower income Oregonians to purchase and stay in a home.
These programs expand access to affordable homeownership through competitive market rate residential loans as well as assisting homeowners in
retaining their homes through education, foreclosure counseling and financial assistance services. These programs benefit homebuyers with lower
incomes who might not otherwise have access to homeownership options and traditional mortgage services. Additional programs provide loans and
grants for manufactured dwelling park preservation and replacing older manufactured homes with newer, energy-efficient models. The section manages
the Oregon Bond Residential Loan Program, down payment assistance, homeownership education and counseling, health and safety repairs, and offers
training and technical assistance resources.
Affordable single-family loans financed through the Oregon Bond Residential Loan Program provide qualified first-time homebuyers with the
opportunity to move from being renters to homeowners. The program offers competitive market interest rates and cash assistance to help pay for closing
costs. When people advance through the housing continuum into homeownership, it helps to free up existing rental housing stock, thereby potentially
increasing affordable housing opportunities for Oregonians. Affordable, competitive market rate residential loans give homeowners the ability to build
wealth and achieve household stability, with an affordable monthly housing payment. Flex Lending, a new lending program, requires no state or federal
funds for baseline operation, but rather leverages OHCS’ unique authorization to access federal homeownership tools. Through its many potential
product offerings, it can reduce the cost of a mortgage or provide down payment assistance for homebuyers.
Access to affordable homeownership through new, energy-efficient manufactured homes provides Oregonians with opportunities for economic and
housing stability and improved health outcomes. The Manufactured Home Replacement program offers subordinate loans and grants for
decommissioning and disposal to low-income owners of manufactured homes who wish to replace their homes. In addition, short-term park preservation
financing enables nonprofit organizations and resident-owned cooperatives to preserve manufactured dwelling parks when permanent financing options
aren’t readily available.



2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                107BF02
                                                                         379
                                                 BUDGET NARRATIVE
Additionally, the Manufactured and Marinas Communities Resource Center (MMCRC) provides impartial assistance to resolve disputes between park
owners and tenants outside of court by promoting cooperation, open communication, and a positive environment. This support serves to create more
stable communities.
Homeownership centers help homeowners seeking information and resources to assist with maintaining their current living environment. The centers
assist prospective homeowners with homebuyer pre- and post-purchase education and down payment assistance, as well as financial literacy and
financial coaching. Homeowners unable to afford the costs of home maintenance can access funding for health and safety repairs, allowing homeowners
the opportunity to remain in a healthy and safe environment. Counselors within homeownership centers and other community organizations provide
foreclosure prevention counseling services and support during foreclosure mediation through the Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling Program (FAC).
For those at severe risk of losing their home, the Homeowner Assistance Fund (HAF) offers financial assistance for low to moderate income Oregonians
who experienced financial hardship related to the coronavirus pandemic. The program preserves homeownership through mortgage reinstatement by
curing a delinquent mortgage and/or assisting with future payments. HAF also connects struggling homeowners to housing counselors and legal aid
services. The HAF program replaces the successful mortgage assistance program, Oregon Homeownership Stabilization Initiative, which supported
17,200 homeowners and was funded by the Hardest Hit Funds after the last recession.
Lastly, OHCS programs not only provide a safety net of support for future and current homeowners but seeks to proactively increase the supply of
affordable homes by providing funding and technical assistance to homeownership developers. Our forthcoming Homeownership Development
Program will foster the creation of new and diverse types of affordable, energy efficient housing units for purchase that are aligned with local needs,
particularly for first generation homebuyers, communities of color, tribal nations, and rural communities.
OHCS works with partners to keep the delivery of Homeownership Programs cost-effective. For example, awarding grant agreements or contracts to
local providers is more affordable than creating an in-house method of delivery and ensures that resources are maximized to address unique community
needs. And, in 2021, we shifted many of our contracts with community providers to a pay for performance model to ensure that they are meeting the
desired funding outcomes.
Central to all Homeownership Programs is a goal of eliminating the racial homeownership gap in Oregon. In 2018, the Oregon Joint Interim Task Force
on Addressing Racial Disparities in Home Ownership (JARDHO) found that “Oregon’s communities of color do not have equal, fair, or equitable
access to homeownership.” Homeownership programs seek to change this by offering accessible and equitable programs, and by proactively funding
and supporting organizations that are culturally responsive and culturally specific. As laid out in the State Housing Plan, all Homeownership programs
aim to serve at least 30% Oregonians of color.




2025-2027 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                 107BF02
                                                                         380
                                                      BUDGET NARRATIVE
Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level.
Phase-Out’s are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27
biennium.

050 Fund Shift: This package contains adjustments to fund shift. This package was used to better align funding in the Central Services division to
match to the agency funding splits for cost allocation purposes. This package also contains the fund shift for lease payments to align funding with the
agency funding.

1843) from General Fund to Other Fund; and shifting services and supplies for those positions from General Fund to Other Funds. General

Policy Package # 518 Homeownership Development: LIFT and HDIP
Package Description

This package supports two Homeownership Development programs that seek to increase the supply of affordable homes for purchase in Oregon:
a) an allocation of $100M in Article XIQ Bonds for the Local Innovations and Fast Track (LIFT) Homeownership program; and b) an allocation of
$50M in general funds for the Homeownership Development Incubator Program (HDIP). Collectively, we expect that these two programs will
lead to the construction of nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase.

Purpose

According to the Oregon Housing Needs Analysis, Oregon needs to increase the housing supply by 550,000 units over the next 20 years to keep
pace with housing needs. Affordable units for purchase are an essential component of a healthy housing stock. Affordable homeownership
promotes community stability and continuity, as homeowners are more likely to stay in one place longer, fostering stronger, more cohesive
neighborhoods. By offering a pathway to economic mobility and stability, affordable homeownership reduces the burden on rental markets and
homelessness services, creating a more balanced and resilient housing ecosystem. Taking a multifaceted approach to tackling the state’s housing
shortage ensures that people at different stages of their housing journey have access to appropriate and sustainable housing options. Increasing the
supply of affordable homeownership opportunities that are designed to meet community needs will reduce disparities in homeownership and
wealth rates among people of color in Oregon.

Both LIFT and HDIP seek to spur greater density in urban areas, support development in rural areas, and increase housing stock in a way that is
informed by local communities. LIFT Homeownership supports the construction of new housing that typically remains permanently affordable.
Due to restrictions of Article XI-Q bonds used for LIFT Homeownership, a general fund resource is needed to ensure broader access to
homeownership development opportunities. HDIP supports housing development on tribal land, encourages generational wealth building among
communities that have been historically excluded from homeownership, and invests in new and innovative models of homeownership
development.

How Achieved

LIFT Homeownership: The objective of the LIFT Homeownership Program is to build new affordable homes for low-income households. With a
focus on increasing housing supply, LIFT funds can only be used for new construction and homes must be ready for sale within 36 months of
receiving a funding reservation. Utilizing Article XI-Q bond funding as a source for housing development, LIFT requires the state to have an
ownership or operational interest in any real property developed. For homeownership opportunities, this means that projects must use a shared
equity model such as a community land trust (CLT), condo, or leasehold property.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                        398
                                                 BUDGET NARRATIVE
Homeownership Development Incubator Program: HDIP increases the supply of affordable homes for purchase and supports homeownership
among communities that have been underrepresented as homeowners in Oregon through the following funding tracks:

      1. Grant Funds for Tribal and Sovereign Nations for the development of affordable homes for purchase and other homeownership-related
      projects.

      2. Pre-development & Capacity Building (PDCB) grants to build the capacity of organizations that are seeking to establish or scale
      operations and increase the pipeline of developers receiving OHCS funding.

      3. Development Subsidies to finance the affordability gap for new construction of affordable homes or the conversion of existing structures
      into new affordable homes for purchase. These awards fund innovative models of homeownership that don't qualify for LIFT, seek to build
      generational wealth, and serve households with incomes up to 120% AMI; and

      4. LIFT Supplemental Grants to provide further subsidy for culturally specific, small, emerging and rural developers accessing LIFT funds.

Staffing Impact

Operations & Policy Analyst 3, Permanent Full Time

Quantifying Results

This investment package aligns with the Governor’s housing production goals and contributes to long-term housing stability and homelessness
prevention.

LIFT: Since 2018, OHCS has completed six LIFT Homeownership NOFA cycles. It’s allocated nearly $90M in LIFT funds to support the
construction of 59 projects that will result in 894 affordable homes that will house an estimated 2,235 low-income Oregonians. Of the LIFT homes
that are complete and have homeowners, the majority are female-headed households with children.

Homeownership is currently redesigning LIFT HO in consultation with DOJ and a LIFT partner working group. We believe the upcoming
restructure will reduce barriers for nonprofit developers and construction lenders and facilitate greater access to LIFT, setting the stage for larger
program growth. With the assumption of an average per unit request of $150,000, we expect to subsidize the construction of 666 new affordable
homes for purchase with $100M in LIFT. This will create homes for an estimated 1,665 Oregonians.

HDIP: Through $40M legislative funding for HDIP in the 2021-2023 biennium, OHCS funded five tribal homeownership projects, 17 non-tribal
homeownership development projects, distributed 14 PDCB grants, and provided market cost offset funds to 11 LIFT projects that faced
unexpected costs due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This prior HDIP funding will lead to 318 new affordable homes by 2026, and more down the
line due to longer term efforts completed through the PDCB grants.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                          107BF02
                                                                          399
                                               BUDGET NARRATIVE
OHCS estimates that this general fund investment will lead to the development of over 300 new affordable homes within three years. At least 6
tribes will receive funding to create an estimated 31 new homes and support an additional 20 tribal members to become homeowners.
Additionally, OHCS will fund 20 unique organizations to scale homeownership development operations and/or conduct pre-development work to
start the process of getting shovel-ready so they qualify for development funding. This seed funding will broaden the state’s network of culturally
specific and emerging homeownership developers.

Together, this LIFT and HDIP investment will create nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase within three years, the majority of which
will remain affordable in perpetuity.

Racial Equity Strategy

Racial disparities in homeownership rates across Oregon are vast. Since 2013, the gap between Black and American Indian and Alaskan Native
homeownership rates and white homeownership rates has widened. Homeownership rates among other race and ethnicities remain far below white
homeownership rates (see chart below).

Housing costs have steadily been on the rise in Oregon. In 2023, the typical home in Oregon costs around $528,000 which is 24% more than the
US average, and 11% (or $52,000) higher than pre-pandemic sales prices. With interest rates at an all-time high, housing affordability is further
out of reach for many Oregonians, particularly Oregonians of color.

                         70.0%                                                                   62% 63% 60% 63%       64% 66%
                         60.0%                                                            51%
                                                                          45%          47%
                         50.0%                            44%       45%         46%
                                                                 40%
                         40.0%   35% 34%        34%    34%
                         30.0%               21%
                         20.0%
                         10.0%
                          0.0%
                                 Black or   Native   Some Hispanic or American   Two or    Total, All        Asian      White
                                  African Hawaiian other race Latino Indian and more races Races                        alone
                                 American and Other           origin   Alaska
                                            Pacific                    Native
                                           Islander

                                                                        2013    2022




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02
                                                                        400
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
OHCS’ Homeownership Development programs—LIFT and HDIP—seek to eliminate gaps in homeownership rates in Oregon. Using a targeted
universalism approach, our current goal is a 66% homeownership rate in Oregon across all races and ethnicities. OHCS’ Homeownership
Development programs work to achieve this through a multi-faceted approach:

   • Set-asides for Culturally Specific Developers. Culturally Specific developers reflect the communities they seek to serve and have lived
       experience and expertise in the needs of these communities. While developers must comply with Fair Housing laws, developers that take a
       culturally specific approach are best positioned to reach and support communities that have been under-represented as homeowners in
       Oregon.
   • HDIP Tribal Funding. With HDIP general funds, we will have a non-competitive set-aside for Tribal Homeownership projects. The
       parameters of this set aside were structured according to feedback from the tribes and will support development of homeownership
       opportunities for Tribal members.
   • Technical Assistance: OHCS has received feedback from culturally specific, small, and rural developers that navigating OHCS funding is a
       barrier. Through this investment, we will have staff dedicated to supporting these developers to plan their homeownership pipeline, apply,
       and utilize OHCS Homeownership Development, making our programs more accessible and inclusive.
   • Pre-development funding: Traditionally, by the time a developer is ready to apply for OHCS Homeownership Development funding, they
       have already invested substantial capital in the project to ensure that it is shovel-ready. This can be a barrier for culturally specific, small,
       and rural developers. Through HDIP general funds, OHCS will be able to offer resources to culturally specific, small, and rural developers
       to scale their capacity and cover the costs of pre-development needed before they can apply for LIFT or other development funding. This
       too will make our programs more accessible and inclusive.
   • Minimum requirements for equity and community engagement: Homeownership Development projects must meet minimum criteria in six
       different categories, including equity and community engagement. In this category, applicants must demonstrate an active commitment to
       diversity equity and inclusion. The applicant also must detail plans to engage and support community members and potential homebuyers
       throughout every stage of the pre- and post-purchase process.


Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes

OHCS tracks Homeownership Development racial equity outcomes using the following data points:

         •   Number of culturally specific developers funded
         •   Number of projects that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
         •   Number of homes that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
         •   Racial and demographic make-up of homeowners
         •   MWESB contracting in terms of dollars spent and percentage of contractors on a project



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                         107BF02
                                                                         401
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
With additional staff we will be able to expand our racial equity tracking. Additional data points may include (but aren’t limited to):

    • Culturally specific developers that received OHSC technical assistance and conversion to successful development applicants
    • Family wealth created through the affordable homeownership opportunity
    • Additional OHCS resources leveraged to reduce sales price and increase equity built in homes
    • Overall progress in reducing racial disparities in homeownership in Oregon

There will be set asides for culturally specific developers as well as support and evaluation criteria to ensure that all development is done in a
culturally responsive way. Additionally, developers are expected to meet MWESB goals for their region. They are evaluated on their MWESB
strategy and OHCS collects data on MWESB contracting and spending.

Revenue Sources

General Fund: $16,853,348

Other Funds:
      Transfer in from General Fund: $16,600,000

Policy Package # 511 Homeowner Assistance Fund
Package Description
This package continues four Homeowner Assistance Fund (HAF) positions into the next biennium to help wind down, report out, and
successfully close the existing federal program.

Purpose

OHCS expects to make final homeowner assistance (program) payments in the 2023-2025 biennium but will need to administer
program close out activities into the 2025-2027 biennium. OHCS is required to confirm and reconcile the application of all payments,
complete a final data compilation, submit a close out request to U.S. Treasury, close out grants and agreements with local partners,
vendors, and a network of national mortgage servicers, and prepare all electronic and paper files for monitoring, potential audits, and
eventual archiving.

How Achieved

These are the key positions needed to complete the above work in the event that it is not all completed by June 30, 2024.

Staffing Impact

Grants Loans & Programs Manager 2, Permanent Full Time
Operations & Policy Analyst 3, Permanent Full Time
Program Analyst 1, Permanent Full Time
Program Analyst 2, Permanent Full Time (X2)

Quantifying Results
Outcomes include U.S. Treasury approval of the HAF program closure and preparation to monitor HAF loans secured to homeowner
properties. This is the final phase of an existing program that has helped over 2,000 families avoid foreclosure and maintain
homeownership.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  406
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Racial disparities exist in homeownership rates across Oregon. HAF attempted to address these disparities by preserving
homeownership of all homeowners the most at risk of foreclosure and, as required by U.S. Treasury, targeting homeowners
historically prejudiced or left out of housing and mortgage markets.

Revenue Sources

Federal Funds: $7,763,877

Policy Package # 521 Down Payment Assistance Paired with OHCS Lending

Package Description
This investment will provide $7.5 million as a recurring source of Down Payment Assistance (DPA) funding outside OHCS’ current Home-Owner
Assistance Program (HOAP). The DPA funds will be administered by OHCS and utilized in conjunction with OHCS’s Lending programs. With
OHCS administering this resource and combining it with our mortgage lending program, funds will assist more homebuyers by addressing a key
barrier for mortgage ready Oregonians: capital for down payment assistance. Further, OHCS has developed its Focused Demographics DPA to
help close the race and ethnicity homeownership gap in Oregon: American Indian or Alaska Natives represent just 0.73% of Oregon homeowners,
African Americans represent only 0.89% of homeowners, and the Hispanic population represents 5.78% of homeowners (2015-19 ACS survey 5-
year estimates). Achieving homeownership provides long-term stability, generational wealth building and the opportunity to help address past
disparate treatment of some communities by the housing and lending industry.


Purpose
OHCS knows saving funds for the required down payment is the largest barrier to homeownership, especially for rent burdened first-time
homebuyers. Home prices in Oregon have continually been increasing at a rate of 10%-14% annually, correspondingly increasing the down
payment needed to buy a home. The DPA and Lending program parameters were developed by engaging stakeholders, experienced HFA’s who
have established lending programs, and analyzing the most recent HOAP DPA programmatic framework. DPA funds and a sustainable mortgage
will assist homebuyers attain housing stability so they may build generational wealth.
OHCS is requesting funds to provide DPA to homebuyers who use the department’s Lending programs. The DPA will be in the form of a
repayable second mortgage loan recycled and loaned again to future homebuyers creating an ongoing, revolving source of assistance. 100% of
appropriations and repayments are pledged for assistance to homeowners, no funding is needed for administration or program delivery due to the
fact that it will be utilized in conjunction with our existing loan programs.


How Achieved
OHCS developed the program parameters and delivery methods in the 2021-23 biennium, incorporating a vetted design whose purpose is to better
serve communities facing the most barriers to entry, those who wish to build generational wealth, yet lack DPA. These funds will provide
continued funding for the DPA program and help create a more robust revolving loan fund to be paired with our Lending programs.
Administering DPA funding in tandem with our Lending programs through its existing network of more than 30 approved mortgage lenders,
OHCS, as Oregon’s Housing Finance Agency (HFA), brings benefits available exclusively to HFA’s and without administrative or program
delivery funding costs. OHCS has developed the terms of the loans based on guidance from stakeholders and the Oregon Housing Stability

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                           107BF02
                                                                      418
                                               BUDGET NARRATIVE
Council. Stakeholder engagement has been used to ensure that the DPA program reflects the demographics of Oregon’s racial and ethnic
communities that have historically been prevented from homeownership.
DPA terms are based on income and demographics. Homebuyers whose income is at or below 80% receive a “silent second” mortgage which
requires no payments, has no interest. Incomes over 80% receive an amortizing second mortgage repaid over time. Our Focused Demographics
DPA product provides more DPA, more attractive terms, and is designed to capture homebuyers who have experienced the greatest barriers to
homeownership, communities of color.

The goal of this funding is to increase the impact of taxpayer dollars while allowing families to build equity. By further financing a revolving DPA
fund, OHCS can assist income-qualified homeowners to achieve their dreams now and in future years.
Staffing Impact
 The DPA Funds in this request will be in conjunction with OHCS lending programs and will not require additional staffing or administrative
costs.

Quantifying Results
This DPA package supports Governor Kotek’s Housing and Homelessness priority of expanding access to homeownership.
$25 million in DPA based on previous loan data would provide DPA for 1,920 households over the biennium. It is estimated that 979 would be
“silent” second DPA mortgages for borrowers at or below 80%AMI, and 941 would be amortizing second DPA mortgages. Monthly payments
from the amortizing second mortgages will be $106,374/mo. These funds will be reinvested to support additional DPA loans, approximately
8/month. Repayment of silent second mortgages, not taken into account in the above calculations, would increase the number of DPA loans
OHCS could provide.

Data provided by our research department shows a significant disparity in homeownership between communities of color and white Oregonians.
For Oregonians between 50% & 100% Area Median Income the homeownership gap is 16% (44% vs. 60%).

OHCS developed our Focused Demographics DPA product with the help of our research group to ensure we are using solid data and DOJ in order
to ensure compliance. The Focused Demographics DPA provides more DPA, more attractive terms, and is designed to capture homebuyers who
have experienced the greatest barriers to homeownership, communities of color.


OHCS is aware that disparities are exceptionally acute in Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal communities with because
of historic and current disinvestment, redlining policies, discriminatory lending practices, and neighborhood gentrification. These inequities result
in Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal communities lacking the resources and generational wealth to become first-time
homebuyers. The key to eliminating the racial homeownership gap is data. OHCS is consolidating systems and gathering more data on mortgage
loans in order to better understand who we serve and how we are serving. We are collecting XML files which will be able to provide greater insight
into borrowers. It is with the help of our research team, this data, Census data and American Community Survey data that we can identify and solve
for gaps in underrepresented populations.

Policy Package # 522 Down Payment Assistance for Culturally Responsive and Rural Organizations

Package Description
As home prices continue to trend upward and interest rates rise, many Oregon families and their communities are missing out on the
long-term stability and generational wealth building that homeownership provides. Down payment is the number one barrier to
homeownership for low- and moderate-income households. Reaching a 20% down payment removes the additional cost of mortgage
insurance, allowing funds to stretch further. This $30M investment will support Homeownership Centers and community-based
organizations that are rural and/or culturally responsive to deploy down payment assistance (DPA) in their communities with an end
goal of reducing the racial homeownership gap.

Purpose
Housing costs have steadily been on the rise in Oregon. In 2023, the typical home in Oregon costs around $528,000 which is 24%
more than the US average, and 11% (or $52,000) higher than pre-pandemic sales prices. With interest rates at an all-time high,
housing affordability is farther out of reach for many Oregonians. Additionally, due to the cost of living, tax rates, and wages, Oregon
is ranked as the fourth most difficult state to save money. Having enough money for a downpayment, let alone 20% to cut down on
mortgage insurance costs, is one of the top barriers to homeownership.

Racial disparities in homeownership rates across Oregon are vast. Since 2013, the gap between Black and American Indian and
Alaskan Native homeownership rates and white homeownership rates has widened. Homeownership rates among other race and
ethnicities remain far below white homeownership rates (see chart below). Sufficient and targeted down payment assistance resources
will be key to eliminating the racial homeownership gap.
 70.0%                                                              62% 63% 60% 63%     64% 66%
                                              45% 46%         51%

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                               107BF02
                                                                     422
                                              BUDGET NARRATIVE
How Achieved
Revenue primarily from document recording fees (DRF), but also construction excise taxes (for applicable counties) and general funds
for culturally responsive organizations have funded OHCS’ DPA Program. OHCS releases a competitive request for applications and
typically funds a broad network of community-based partners to provide DPA as part of a holistic suite of homebuyer services. This
was the case in 2023, when, using DRF funds and general funds for culturally responsive partners, OHCS allocated $22M in DPA to
21 organizations across the state. This DPA was deployed quickly by grantees, some within months, showing a huge need for
additional DPA resources across the state.

However, due to a slowed housing market, DRF revenue has decreased steadily since 2021 and OHCS will not have enough funds to
continue funding partners’ DPA programs. Partners’ contracts with OHCS expire in May 2025, and additional funding is needed to
keep these programs in operation. The included table shows HOAP DRF receipts from the past two biennia and projected forward.1

The DPA for culturally responsive organizations (“CRO DPA”) will enable OHCS to continue funding organizations that are reaching
homebuyers that represent demographics that have traditionally been excluded from homeownership, many of which have DPA
waitlists, as well expand our CRO and rural DPA partners.

The CRO DPA is offered in the form of a grant of up to $30,000 for first time homebuyers or up to $60,000 for first-generation
homebuyers or 20% of the purchase price (whatever is less). This DPA can be stacked with other sources such as construction excise
tax funds, individual development account savings, or DPA from local jurisdictions. The funds requested through this investment will
be similarly administered by OHCS, with potential changes to per homebuyer caps, and made available through culturally responsive
homeownership partners and organizations serving rural communities to allow low-income Oregonians better access to
homeownership and generational wealth building. OHCS will conduct extensive outreach to ensure that culturally responsive
organizations throughout the state that are not yet part of our network have access to this opportunity. Additionally, OHCS will
provides guidance and training to organizations new to offering DPA.


1 While DRF receipts will cover OHCS’ current commitments to DPA grantees and Homeownership Centers (HOCs), if the economy remains the

same, we don’t expect to start accumulating sufficient DRF revenue to provide funding for DPA until the 2027-2029 biennium. By the end of FY29
we expect our ending cash position for DPA to be $3.4M which, while insufficient to meet the demand, would be enough funding to forward
allocate through a competitive process. Separately, but also through DRF revenue, OHCS funds HOCs to offer pre and post-purchase education
and counseling, financial education, and other homebuyer services. These contracts typically last for five years. Given DRF estimates, OHCS
does not foresee having enough DRF revenue to extend the HOC contracts beyond their 12/2026 expiration date. By FY29, OHCS projects having
just under $2M in funds for HOCs which is enough to fund one year of services at the level at which they are currently funded. This leaves a two-
year gap in funding for the network of 15 HOCs that serve homeowners across the state.

                                                                      423

Staffing Impact

No requesting additional positions in this package.

Quantifying Results
DPA will help working families, first time homebuyers, first generation homebuyers, and low-income Oregonians. To date, our CRO
DPA has served over 100 homebuyers in less than a year. 63% of the funds went to people of color and 74% of the funds were to first
generation homebuyers (74% of whom were people of color). The average amount of DPA was $32,982 per homebuyer. Taking into
account rising home prices, we expect to serve 1100 homebuyers with this new funding.

Lack of sufficient savings to buy a home is exceptionally acute in Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal
communities due to historic and current disinvestment, redlining policies, discriminatory lending practices, and neighborhood
gentrification. These inequities result in Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal communities lacking the
resources and generational wealth to become homebuyers. With this investment, OHCS can adapt and expand its DPA program to
meet the increased needs of Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, and Tribal communities, supporting them to thrive
as homeowners in the communities they choose to live in.
This investment is targeted to rural and culturally specific organization to get more funds to community members experiencing
systemic barriers to homeownership by offering the resources and wrap around culturally responsive support needed to buy a home.
OHCS will work with these organizations to update and shape our DPA framework according to their community’s needs.
OHCS does extensive outcome tracking of DPA funds through quarterly reporting from grantees. This data is shared publicly on our
Homeownership Dashboard. We collect data on:
   •   Program outreach
   •   Number of clients served

                                                                 424
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
   •   Homebuyer demographics including race, ethnicity, gender, income, first time or first generation homebuyer, veteran status,
       household size
   •   Home price
   •   Mortgage amount and type
   •   DPA amount received and fund source
   •   Types and amounts of other sources needed to close
With this investment, we will include more qualitative data collection to capture homebuyer stories.

This funding will only go to culturally specific and culturally responsive community-based organizations as well as rural
organizations.

Policy Package # 525 Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling
Package Description
Foreclosure prevention is an essential tool for homelessness prevention. There is a strong need for sufficient funding to continue work
to reduce homelessness, eliminate racial disparities in housing, and ensure that homeowners can remain stable in their homes. This
package recommends $2.5 million in General Fund resources for OHCS’ existing Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling (FAC) program
to support homeowners across the State who are in danger of foreclosure. This investment will support a vast state-wide network of
certified foreclosure avoidance counselors who work with clients in jeopardy of losing their homes. This investment ensures that this
impactful work can continue.

Purpose

Having a counselor to navigate a scary and stressful process, explain bank and legal jargon, help homeowners avoid scams and
understand their rights makes the difference between staying in one’s home or losing it forever. Foreclosure is costly and disruptive
for families, lenders, and communities. Foreclosure counseling is a proven, structured, and low-cost intervention that can help
struggling homeowners avert the grave impact of foreclosure on their housing, economic, and mental health and get back on track with
their mortgage payments. Additionally, keeping people in their own home reduces pressure on the rest of the housing ecosystem.

During the 2021 and 2023 Legislative Sessions, the Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling program was funded through a budget note to
create more resources for homeownership centers to provide counseling to homeowners. This filled a gap in foreclosure prevention in
Oregon and helped homeowners stay in their homes, creating the Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling (FAC) program. When economic
times are good, the state disinvests in crisis programs like foreclosure prevention counseling due to a lack of need, depleting
infrastructure, institutional knowledge, and staff. Then when the economy turns down, crisis resources are stood back up anew. It
takes time to get the training and credentialing needed to do Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling. Stakeholders have highlighted the
need to continue funding programs like FAC even when the economy is good to maintain the strength of this safety net and create a
sustainable, right-sized system to support Oregon homeowners.

How Achieved

OHCS’ existing Foreclosure Avoidance Counseling (FAC) program provides reimbursement to agencies with foreclosure counselors
for each client who receives a series of in-depth counseling sessions. The program requires counselors to be certified to provide
foreclosure counseling and help homeowners navigate their best resources to avoid foreclosure. Through counseling, homeowners


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                 427
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
receive services that address default, foreclosure, forbearance, budget management. As a result, Oregonians can continue to live in
their homes and preserve equity in their property.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff are being requested in this package.

Quantifying Results

Outcomes
This program is expected to serve 1682 homeowners which is impactful, yet only a fraction of the need. According to data from
OHCS’ master servicer, foreclosure referrals have steadily increased since the two-year foreclosure moratorium was lifted in January
2022. In 2023, over 62,000 Oregonians were at severe risk of foreclosure (90+ day delinquencies) or faced foreclosure. If this rate
holds steady, we will only be able to serve 3% of those at risk of foreclosure.
Of the 800 clients served by FAC from March 2021 to October 2023, 57% completed counseling with a modified mortgage and only
2% of clients counseled ended up with a reported foreclosure (see chart below).




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  428
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Racial Equity Strategy

Foreclosure counseling is a racial equity strategy as homeowners who are Black, Indigenous, Latino/a/x, Asian, Pacific Islander, or a
member of a federally recognized Tribes typically lack savings and family wealth that are critical to buffering unexpected economic
shocks and experience higher rates of foreclosure. 32% of clients served by FAC are people of color. OHCS expects to maintain or
increase this percentage to ensure that Oregonians of color can stay in their homes, realize their home equity, and choose to pass it on
to the next generation.

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
OHCS tracks program outcomes through the Homeownership dashboard here. This data includes:
           •   Number of new homeowners who received services
           •   Demographic information of homeowners including:
                    i. Intake Date
                   ii. County
                 iii. Birthdate
                  iv. Gender
                   v. Veteran
                  vi. Race
                 vii. Ethnicity
                viii. Number in Household
                  ix. Household Income
                   x. Mortgage Balance
                  xi. Mortgage Payment Amount
           •   Homeowner cases closed and outcome of services

OHCS will continue to track this data by organization.
Administration
OHCS currently contracts with 16 community-based organizations across the state that have certified foreclosure counselors. Five of
these organizations are culturally specific. With this investment, we will seek to continue funding current grantees as well as broaden
our network of culturally specific FAC grantees.
