Policy Package # 527 Manufactured Home Replacement Program
Package Description
Tens of thousands of Oregonians with low-incomes living in manufactured housing are sliding deeper into poverty and closer to
homelessness due to substandard and unsafe housing conditions. In 2019, the Oregon Legislature passed House Bill 2896 to preserve
manufactured home parks in the state and create programs to provide loans to individuals to replace these inadequate and energy
inefficient homes. This bill was supported by diverse community members and organizations and provides for the Oregon Housing and
Community Services (OHCS) Manufactured Home Replacement Program (MHRP) that today can assist manufactured homeowners
with replacing their homes constructed prior to current federal standards with safe, new, and energy-efficient ones. From this piece of
legislation, OHCS created the Manufactured Home Replacement Program to incentivize manufactured homeowners, with grants and
forgivable loans, to replace their old and energy inefficient homes. Over the last two biennium, this popular and cost-effective program
has dramatically improved the lives and living condition of families across our state. MHRP request $2.5 million to continue this critical
work in order to prevent homelessness before it happens.

Purpose

Manufactured homes constructed prior to 1995 are prone to severe physical problems that can make them inadequate and unsafe. This
is especially problematic for Oregonians with low-income who have little choice other than to live in substandard housing
overburdened by energy costs. Currently, there are approximately 60,000 manufactured homes in Oregon in need of replacement that
are owned and occupied by individuals and families with low-incomes. There are 843 extreme cases of manufactured homes in 15 of
the 38 state-supported preserved, non-profit/cooperative owned manufactured home parks that were built before the introduction of
any federal HUD safety standards in 1976. The MHRP is a flexible program that works in conjunction with other grants and
replacement incentives. By design, it will provide assistance with the disposal of a current owner-occupied manufactured home, the
purchase of a new energy efficient manufactured home, and the addition work required to make a new manufactured home move-in-
ready. OHCS will assign a manufactured home replacement navigator to help eligible applicants through the process of planning the
project, identifying financial resources, and coordinating the different parts of the replacement process. This program is a guaranteed
win in the efforts to reduce homelessness and increase affordable housing for Oregonians with low incomes as all MHRP participating
households have income below 100% area median and 75% of program participants to date have had household incomes below 50%
area median income.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   431
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE

How Achieved
This is a continuation of our existing Manufactured Home Replacement Program, which provides grants and loans for the
decommission and replacement respectively of pre 1995 manufactured homes.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff is requested in this package.

Quantifying Results

This is plug and play program funds for a very popular and successful program that with $8.3M ($5.8 in 2019 & $2.5 in 2023)
replaced 70 homes, 34 of which were lost due to natural disaster. With the $5.8M from 2019 the MHRP replaced 50 manufactured
homes in 11 counties. Of those program participant: 22% were BIPOC households, 65% of were households under 60% AMI, 71% of
homes were outside of city centers. This biennium (23-25), due to high demand, the Manufactured Home Replacement Program
(MHRP) received over 100 applications and was fully allocated within 60 days. With the $2.5M from 2023 the program replaced 19
manufactured homes, throughout Oregon. The average cost of replacing a manufactured home is $131,000. An additional $2.5M
would allow the program to provide and estimated 15–19 low-income households the ability to live in safe, permanently stable,
affordable housing. Since program launch in 2019, 48% of MHRP homes replaced have been homes lost due to natural disaster and
22% of MHRP homes replaced have been in non-profit, preserved manufactured home parks where rent increases are controlled by
tenants and any homes sold must be sold to a low-income buyer. Forty income eligible applications are currently waitlisted from 23-
25 biennium. $2.5M would replace an estimated 15-19 homes statewide in 25-27.

Revenue Sources

General Fund: $2,500,000

Other Funds:
     Transfer in from General Fund: $2,500,000

Disaster Recovery and Resilience Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:                      Healthy and Safe Communities, A Thriving Statewide Economy
Program Contact:                           Natasha Detweiler-Daby, Interim Executive Leader for Disaster Recovery and Resilience (DRR)




*For 2021-23, programs’ actuals were recorded in Central Services. Displayed here for comparison purposes.

Program Overview
OHCS, through the Disaster Recovery and Resilience (DRR) division, was identified as the implementing agency for the $422M
Community Development Block Grant – Disaster Recovery (CDBG-DR) grant appropriated by Congress and granted by the Department
of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) in response to the 2020 Labor Day fires. The rules establishing the requirements to receive
funding were not published until February 2022. The ReOregon Action Plan, which is a blueprint for the award implementation, was
developed after undertaking extensive unmet needs and mitigation needs assessments, and through significant public engagement
sessions. The Action Plan was submitted to HUD for approval in June 2022 and was approved by HUD in September 2022. HUD
provided OHCS the $422M grant agreement in February 2023. In April 2023, OHCS executed a contract to build the system of record
for ReOregon and in September 2023, the contract for ReOregon program management (to administer the centralized Homeowner


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02
                                                                                              440
                                                       BUDGET NARRATIVE
Assistance and Reconstruction Program) was executed. ReOregon serves impacted individuals, families and communities in eight
counties - Clackamas, Douglas, Jackson, Klamath, Lane, Lincoln, Linn and Marion.


Program Funding Request
This package requests expenditure limitation for federal funds to complete staffing and carry out implementation of Community
Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery (CDBG-DR) work (AKA, “ReOregon”) in order to assist survivors of the 2020 Labor Day
fires who have not yet been able to rebuild or find safe, affordable homes, and support community recovery and disaster mitigation
efforts. Under certain circumstances, grantees can request an extension of the 6-year expenditure deadline. Core permanent staff for
DRR division were approved in the 2022 short session, as were a number of limited term positions. This package requests that all
budgeted limited duration positions be extended through the 2025-27 biennium to staff implementation of ReOregon programs and
administration.

Program Description
State-Funded Programs
During the 2021 session, the Oregon Legislature allocated $150.4 million, as part of House Bill 5006, to Oregon Housing and
Community Services to assist fire survivors and their communities with recovery from the 2020 Labor Day fires. The appropriation
included $100.4 million of General Fund and $50 million of Lottery-backed bond revenues. These funds were spent or committed to
wildfire recovery efforts and housing developments before the end of the 2021-23 state biennium. As of April 30, 2024, OHCS has
spent $98.0 million in what had been appropriated as General Fund and $24.7 million in Lottery Bond proceeds, for a total of
$122.7 million spent.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                                           441
                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
Approximately $36 million of the $150.4 million was allocated to the Wildfire Recovery and Resilience Account (WRRA) program to
fund local partners assisting fire-impacted households with recovery. The overarching goal of the program was to move participants
into safe, affordable intermediate or permanent housing. WRRA supported impacted households with resources in two main categories
– Rental assistance and related supports, and damaged housing replacement, rehabilitation and reconstruction. WRRA supported 450
households with intermediate/temporary housing over the life of the program and supported 867 households with permanent recovery.
Over 65% of the households supported were at 80% area median income or below.
OHCS staff have provided Lane, Marion, and Lincoln counties suballocations, all of which are under contract, and funds have been
disbursed to the counties. These funds, a total of $19 million across the three counties, are being granted for land acquisition,
infrastructure development, and other recovery activities. Counties have a deep understanding of the needs of their communities and
can move more nimbly to respond to new opportunities in recovery. Additionally, these suballocations were made to ensure rough
geographic equity of resources.

With the support of state wildfire funding, more than 100 units are expected to be move-in ready by the end of September 2024 across
Lincoln, Lane, and Jackson counties.

Federally-Funded Programs
ReOregon is a federally-funded program comprised of multiple, stand-alone programs that are outlined in the ReOregon Action Plan.
These programs include the Homeowner Assistance and Reconstruction Program (HARP), the Planning, Infrastructure and Economic
Revitalization (PIER) program, the Homeownership Opportunities Program (HOP), the Intermediate Housing Assistance (IHA)
program, Legal Services, Housing Recovery Services (HRS), and the Resilience Planning Program (RPP). Each program will include
a comprehensive policy manual. Each policy manual is tailored to the program, but, in general, will describe program eligibility,
award determinations, and appeal processes, when applicable. The larger, more complex programs, such as HARP and HOP, will also
incorporate Standard Operating Procedures that outline a detailed process for all steps of program implementation to ensure that
service received is equitable and consistent for all applicants.

ReOregon includes a robust System of Record (SOR) that was developed to collect and track data and manage all program activity for
the larger HARP, PIER and HOP programs, which will allow the measurement of progress towards milestones and key indicators.
The SOR includes a reporting function that OHCS can use to aggregate data that subgrantees and subrecipients will be required to
submit through the system. Within each key ReOregon program (PIER, HARP, HOP), the SOR will outline workflows and sections
for documentation and information upload that require routine quality assurance checks before files can advance between each process
step. HARP participants will be able to track the progress of their application through the SOR.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                107BF02
                                                                442
                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE

Program Justification
As the designated lead agency for State Recovery Function 5: Disaster Housing, OHCS is responsible for addressing pre-and post-
disaster housing issues, facilitating the delivery of state resources to assist local and tribal governments in the rehabilitation and
reconstruction of destroyed and damaged housing, and developing new accessible, long-term housing options. Prior to the 2020 Labor
Day fires, OHCS did not have a division dedicated to community recovery in the wake of devastating disasters. The Disaster Recovery
and Resilience division was established to not only respond to the 2020 Labor Day fires, but to also look at ways to support community
and housing resilience through planning resources and the incorporation of energy efficiency, fire hardening and other resilience
measures into reconstruction efforts.


Program Performance
HARP: To better determine remaining needs and identify eligible applicants, OHCS launched an eligibility questionnaire on March 25,
2024. As of July 27, 2024, 890 questionnaires were completed and to date, over 10% of households who submitted questionnaires are
primarily Spanish speakers. From the 890 questionnaires, 545 respondents were invited to apply when the application opened for Phase
1 (households at or under 80% area median income) on April 30, 2024. OHCS expects to complete review and processing of all eligible
applicants under Phase 1 between the end of the 2023-25 biennium and the beginning of the 2025-27 biennium. Phase 2 was launched
in June 2024. Under HARP, OHCS expects to provide awards during the 2023-25 biennium to reconstruct or replace 500 new homes,
based on current results from the HARP eligibility questionnaire, program estimates as reported to HUD and an average unit cost of
$250,000. OHCS expects that while the majority of awards for reconstruction/replacement of the 500 homes will be made in the 2023-
25 biennium, due to construction/replacement timelines, many of those homes will be ready for occupancy in the 2025-27 biennium.

HOP: With feedback from county partners in the impacted regions, OHCS redesigned HOP to include a down payment assistance
program, as well as incentives for the development/rehabilitation of multiple types of affordable housing, including single-family
stick-built or pre-fabricated structures; resident/housing authority/nonprofit-owned manufactured housing parks; and/or condominiums
for both homeownership and rental opportunities. In coordination with county representatives, OHCS has allocated funding for HOP
amongst impacted counties based on their percentage of housing damage, with a multiplier to account for increased construction costs
in rural areas. Depending on program design and based on an average unit cost of $300,000-$400,000, ReOregon expects to facilitate
the construction or purchase/rehabilitation of 100 homes in the 2025-27 biennium.


PIER: ReOregon launched the PIER program in the current biennium and PIER selection committees have been formed in each county
are currently reviewing and approving projects. To date, 16 projects have been selected by local selection committees and two of those


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                  107BF02
                                                                 443
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
projects are ready for award. DRR staff have conducted detailed budget reviews, duplication of benefits reviews, capacity and risk
assessments, and created training materials, standard operating procedures and an online toolkit for PIER subrecipients. Overall, OHCS
anticipates funding approximately nine projects at the end of the current biennium. Recognizing that complex projects may take multiple
years to complete, it is anticipated that the majority of project activities will take place in the 2025-27 biennium. Economic revitalization
projects or any project that has direct, individual beneficiaries will also generate data on number of people served.


IHA: OHCS has entered into an agreement with Oregon Department of Human Services for the administration of Disaster Case
Management services. The program provides assistance to those individuals and households who are awaiting permanent housing
options that will be provided by HARP and HOP programs described above. For the IHA program, OHCS anticipates serving about
215 households, on average, each month. All of this information will be able to be reviewed by race and ethnicity, income level, and
other demographic information. Currently, impacted households are being assisted with Oregon Emergency Rent Assistance Program
funds, and IHA will be launched as those funds are depleted to ensure there is no gap in services. Since IHA has a maximum limit of
24 months, by leveraging additional funding sources, overall assistance to impacted households can be stretched across additional
months. We expect the transition to IHA to take place during the current biennium (by January 2025), with services continuing into the
2025-27 biennium.

HRS: OHCS expects that the majority of activity under this program will take place during the current biennium and at the beginning
of the 2025-27 biennium when the program is launched at the beginning of 2025. The program expects to serve approximately 150
households in the current biennium.

Legal Services: OHCS expects that the majority of activity under this program will take place during the current biennium when the
program is launched at the beginning of 2025. The program expects to serve approximately 150 households, but the final number
served will be based on the need for legal assistance.


RPP: This program will be designed and launched near the end of the current biennium/beginning of the 2025-27 biennium. OHCS
expects to complete two projects under the program.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                        107BF02
                                                                    444
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE

Enabling Legislation and Program Authorization

The Disaster Relief Supplemental Appropriations Act, 2022 (Pub. L. 117– 43), approved September 30, 2021, makes available
$5,000,000,000 in Community Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery (CDBG–DR) funds. These CDBG–DR funds are for
necessary expenses for activities authorized under Title I of the Housing and Community Development Act of 1974 (42 U.S.C. 5301
et seq.) related to disaster relief, long-term recovery, restoration of infrastructure and housing, economic revitalization, and mitigation
in the most impacted and distressed areas resulting from a qualifying major disaster in 2020 or 2021. The United States Department of
Housing and Urban Development (HUD) allocated $422M in CDBG–DR funds from the Appropriations Act to OHCS-DRR to assist
in long term recovery from the Labor Day Fires disaster occurring in 2020. HUD notified the public of the allocations in press releases
and in a Federal Register notice, Allocations for Community Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery and Implementation of the
CDBG–DR Consolidated Waivers and Alternative Requirements Notice, which was published at 87 Fed. Reg. 6364 on February 3,
2022. In October 2021, OHCS was named by Gov. Brown the administering agency for the State of Oregon. The grant to OHCS was
executed on February 3, 2023.
Funding Streams

ReOregon programs are funded with General Fund and Federal Funds. The Community Development Block Grant – Disaster
Recovery federal grant is the primary source of funding. We have some carry forward other funds associated with Lottery Bond
Proceeds for Wildfire Recovery.
Comparison of 2025-27 Funding Proposal to 2023-25 Funding

ReOregon funding for the 2025-27 biennium remains the same as funding in the current biennium, as the CDBG-DR grant is a 6-year
grant. One-time funds of $150M (general funds and lottery-backed bonds) granted in 2021 by the state legislature for recovery from
the 2020 Labor Day fires are expected to be almost fully expended in the current biennium.

Disaster Recovery and Resilience Programs Description

Homeowner Assistance and Reconstruction Program (HARP)
HARP will provide assistance in the form of grants to eligible homeowners who experienced damage to their homes from the 2020
Wildfires and have remaining recovery needs after accounting for other duplicative benefits received.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02
                                                                    445
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
The program will fund eligible rehabilitation, reconstruction, acquisition, and replacement costs, including additional costs to comply
with federal, State, and local construction standards, such as replacing on-site residential infrastructure, complying with green building
standards, and ensuring that homes are accessible for individuals living with disabilities and senior residents. Eligible costs also
include elevation, fire hardening, and other program-required costs that will help protect homes from natural hazards faced in the fire-
impacted communities.

Homeownership Opportunities Program (HOP)
HOP is designed to support survivors who were renters at the time of the disaster. HOP will consist of a Down Payment Assistance
(DPA) program as well as Affordable Housing Development (AHD) with County options for homeownership and/or rental housing
developments.

Planning, Infrastructure and Economic Revitalization (PIER)
PIER will provide direct county allocations for local governments, non-profit organizations, school districts, public housing
authorities, and other public or quasi-public entities in the eight most impacted counties to address the unmet infrastructure, planning,
and economic revitalization needs that fall within their counties. While budgetary allocations will be made at the county level,
counties and organizations within the impacted areas will work together to define the specific projects or programs to be funded. Each
infrastructure, economic revitalization, and planning activity must clearly have a tie to revitalizing disaster impacted communities by
directly or indirectly supporting:
    •   New housing and/or replacement of damaged housing, and/or
    •   The mitigation of loss of life or property in the face of current and future natural hazards.

Intermediate Housing Assistance (IHA)
IHA provides assistance to eligible residents located in the 2020 Wildfire-impacted counties who lack the necessary resources or
support networks to obtain affordable rental housing or need alternative housing until permanent housing solutions are secured.

OHCS will provide grants to eligible subrecipients to provide:

•   Up to 24 months of rental, temporary relocation, and/or other intermediate housing assistance.
•   Housing navigation, case management, and support services to disaster-impacted residents.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                    446
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
Housing Recovery Services (HRS)
OHCS may administer the Housing and Recovery Services Program directly or by awarding grants to homeownership centers,
nonprofit organizations, or other qualified subrecipients to deliver housing and financial counseling and housing navigation services to
impacted residents to help in their transition to more permanent housing. Services also may be provided to small rental property
owners who provide affordable housing to income-qualified tenants. Services may include homeowner education, renter counseling,
homebuyer education, financial literacy, credit rehabilitation, debt management, budgeting, homelessness counseling, avoiding fraud
and scams, applying for public and private resources, foreclosure prevention strategies, and relocation counseling, among other
services tailored to fit the participants’ needs.

Legal Services
Through the Legal Services Program, OHCS will provide funding to qualified legal aid and/or legal services providers to deliver the
assistance necessary to help impacted residents transition to more permanent housing.

Resilience Planning Program (RPP)
Through the Resilience Planning Program, the State will carry out regional and statewide recovery, resilience and mitigation planning,
including, but not limited to, the following:
   •   Housing development strategies, including developing construction standards, facilitating the development of affordable
       housing and removing barriers to affordable housing
   •   Public land use and infrastructure policy and planning
   •   Public resilience and preparedness policy and planning
   •   Increasing resilience to the impacts of climate change
   •   Protecting public health
   •   Addressing environmental injustice
   •   Spurring economic growth and creating jobs




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                  447
                                                       BUDGET NARRATIVE
Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level. Phase-Out’s are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in
the 2025-27 biennium.
Phase-Out’s for the Disaster Recovery Division
   • Other Funds
          o Disaster Recovery & Resilience 21-23 Carry Forward- $27.1M
          o Lottery Revenue Bonds from 21-23 Carry Forward - $21.9M
   • Federal Funds
          o Community Development Block Grant - $137M

030 Inflation and Price List Adjustments
This package contains adjustments for inflation and other price list adjustments including State Government Service Charges and Usage Based Charges
as directed by Department of Administrative Services. Examples below.
   •   Standard Inflation 4.2%
   •   Professional Services 6.8%
   •   Attorney General 23.26%
   •   State Government Service Charges 33%

050 Fund Shift
This package contains adjustments to fund shift. This package was used to better align funding in the Central Services division to match to the agency
funding splits for cost allocation purposes. This package also contains the fund shift for lease payments to align funding with the agency funding.

Policy Package # 508 DRR Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

This program's portion of the agency-wide policy package is an intrafund transfer out to support a proportionate share of funding.

Staffing Impact

No additional positions in this program.

Quantifying Results
As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly
around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Policy Package # 510 Disaster Recovery & Resiliency Limitation
Package Description
This package requests $163,510,769 in Federal Funds expenditure limitation for staffing and to carry out implementation of the
Community Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery (CDBG-DR) work (aka “ReOregon”). The total awarded to Oregon is $422
million, and OHCS began expending funds in 2021-23. The state has six years to complete expenditure of the entire grant from the date
of grant execution on February 3, 2023. Core permanent staff for the Disaster Recovery and Resilience (DRR) division were approved
in the 2022 short session, as were a number of limited duration positions in DRR and support sections.
The package also included Other Funds limitation related to lottery bonds issued in 2021-23 for wildfire recovery. All funds were
committed to projects, but some of the funds were expected to be unspent at the end of the biennium. OHCS is requesting $7,309,722
in Other Funds limitation to complete these activities.

Purpose
This package is for expenditure limitation to use federal funds to (a) assist impacted households from the 2020 Labor Day Fires who
have not yet been able to rebuild or find safe, affordable homes, with a priority on low- and moderate-income households and (b) support
community recovery and disaster mitigation efforts.
The federal government appropriated funds Community Development Block Grant - Disaster Recovery in the fall of 2021, but rules
establishing the requirements to receive funding were not published until February 2022. A draft Action Plan outlining implementation
and distribution of the grant was made available for public comment in May 2022. OHCS conducted five public hearings and received
(and published responses to) nearly 200 comments from those hearings, focus groups, local government feedback, and an on-line survey.
Revisions were made to the plan based on public comment, and the OHCS Housing Stability Council approved the ReOregon Action
Plan on June 3, 2022. On June 8, 2022, OHCS submitted the plan to the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
and received approval in September 2022.
The ReOregon Action Plan describes how the state will spend the funds to support recovery from the 2020 Labor Day Fires in
Clackamas, Douglas, Jackson, Klamath, Lane, Lincoln, Linn, and Marion counties. The goal of ReOregon is to ensure that all fire-
impacted individuals and households have equitable access to the resources necessary to be housed safely, sustainably, permanently,
affordably, and in their housing of choice. There are no proof-of-citizenship or residency requirements, and OHCS will seek
opportunities to partner with culturally specific, community-based organizations to support survivors through application and recovery
processes.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                            107BF02
                                                                  465
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
ReOregon consists of the following seven programs:

Homeowner Assistance and Reconstruction Program (HARP) The Homeowner Assistance and Reconstruction Program will assist
homeowners in replacing or reconstructing homes that were destroyed by the 2020 fires. For homeowners that have not yet begun to
rebuild, OHCS will be providing resources to fund the Architectural & Engineering, Construction, and Construction Management
services to expedite and fully support survivors in their recovery journey. To support this work, OHCS is working to implement a
development program to finance the construction or manufactured home purchase, delivery, installation and finishing of homes for
eligible homeowners.

OHCS has phased application processing to ensure that traditionally vulnerable populations are able to equitably access resources. Phase
1 will be open to low- and moderate-income applicants (defined as those with household income at or below 80% of area median income)
to ensure this population’s needs are prioritized. The program will fund eligible rehabilitation, reconstruction, acquisition, replacement,
and temporary relocation costs. Homeowners who may have purchased sub-standard or unsafe housing or homes that remain vulnerable
to the impacts of future disasters will also be eligible for assistance.

HARP was designed to include an Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) Pilot Program, which sets aside $4 million for the construction of
ADUs on the property of reconstructed homes for the provision of affordable rental housing for low- and moderate-income (LMI)
Oregonians. This program will be designed after the Phase 1 launch of HARP in the current biennium, with launch of the pilot in the
2025-27 biennium.

Homeownership Opportunities Program (HOP) To address the impact of the 2020 Labor Day fires on rental housing stock, the state has
already invested heavily in new affordable housing projects in the impacted communities. To serve additional renters and expand
available affordable housing stock, the ReOregon program dedicates significant funding for the development of new homeownership
and affordable rental housing opportunities for renters displaced by the fires. Funding for the development of affordable housing
developments is being made available to counties to administer so that they can support projects that meet the specific needs of their
communities. The program also includes down payment assistance (DPA), which will be administered by DPA providers that serve the
impacted counties.

Intermediate Housing Assistance (IHA) This program will provide assistance to 2020 Labor Day fire-impacted owners and renters who
need intermediate housing until permanent, affordable housing solutions are secured. OHCS will provide grants to eligible subrecipients
to provide up to 24 months of rental, temporary relocation, and/or other intermediate housing assistance, such as funding housing
navigation, case management, and support services for disaster-impacted residents.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   466
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Planning, Infrastructure and Economic Revitalization (PIER) The program will provide direct grants to local governments and/or non-
profits in the eight most-impacted counties to address unmet recovery and mitigation needs. Within budgetary sub-allocations made at
the county level, counties, cities and recovery organizations within the impacted areas will work together to define the specific projects
or programs to be funded. The program allows for a wide range of CDBG-DR-eligible infrastructure, economic revitalization, mitigation,
and planning activities. Each activity must clearly have a tie to revitalizing disaster-impacted communities by directly or indirectly
supporting new housing and replacement of damaged housing, and/or the mitigation of loss of life or property in the face of future
natural hazards.

Housing and Recovery Services (HRS) OHCS will administer the Housing and Recovery Services Program by awarding grants to
homeownership centers, nonprofit organizations, or other qualified subrecipients to deliver housing and financial counseling and housing
navigation services to impacted residents to help in their transition to more permanent housing. Services will include homeowner
education, renter counseling, homebuyer education, financial literacy, credit rehabilitation, debt management, budgeting, homelessness
counseling, avoiding fraud and scams, applying for public and private resources, foreclosure prevention strategies, and relocation
counseling, among other services tailored to fit the participants’ needs. Disaster-impacted households are facing monumental challenges
and are making life-changing decisions related to housing and their household finances.

Legal Services Through the Legal Services Program, OHCS will provide funding to qualified legal aid and/or legal services providers
to deliver the assistance necessary to help impacted residents transition to more permanent housing. This will help survivors resolve
legal challenges, including replacing identification papers, insurance claims, unlawful evictions and foreclosures, title issues, and other
legal services related to recovery.

Resilience Planning Program (RPP) Through the Resilience Planning Program, the State will carry out regional and statewide recovery,
resilience and mitigation planning, including, but not limited to, the following:

    Housing development strategies, including developing construction standards, facilitating the development of affordable housing
     and removing barriers to affordable housing
    Public land use and infrastructure policy and planning
    Public resilience and preparedness policy and planning, particularly in the areas of increasing resilience to the impacts of climate
     change, protecting public health, and addressing environmental injustice
    Spurring economic growth and creating jobs.

The planning and technical assistance process is intended to be inclusive and reflective of those with lived disaster experience, housing
insecurity, and/or economic insecurity. The State will design and implement an inclusive planning process that incorporates feedback
and input in a manner that is equitable and representative of the residents living in the impacted areas.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   467
                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
How Achieved
OHCS’ ReOregon Action Plan was approved by HUD in September 2022 and the CDBG-DR grant was executed in February 2023.
DRR staff have developed, or are in the process of developing, extensive policy manuals for each of the seven programs under ReOregon,
as well as 89 (to date) Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for HARP. As of August 2024, DRR staff have launched PIER, HARP
and a subsection of the Housing Recovery Services program administered by ODHS.
The remaining programs will be launched at the end of the 2023-25 biennium/beginning of the 2025-27 biennium.

Quantifying Results
OHCS adopted two guiding policy pillars for the ReOregon Action Plan - Equity and Resilience. These policy pillars were adopted
because they respond directly to two ongoing crises in Oregon: an ongoing housing crisis, exacerbated by the disaster, that
disproportionately affects BIPOC communities, and the climate crisis, making future disasters more and more likely.
 OHCS has applied a Targeted Universalism approach to designing and implementing ReOregon programs. Within a Targeted
Universalism framework, universal goals are established for all groups concerned. The framework then uses targeted processes and
strategies to achieve those goals, based upon how different groups are situated within structures, culture, and across geographies to
obtain the universal goal. Targeted Universalism is a platform to operationalize programs that move all groups toward the universal
policy goal, as well as a way of communicating and publicly marketing such programs in an inclusive, bridging manner. It is an
approach that supports the needs of particular groups, including those in the majority, while reminding everyone that we are all part of
the same social and civic fabric. OHCS developed specific solutions to address unmet needs, incorporating those solutions into a
universal goal-oriented framework to equitably benefit all groups involved. In the case of the 2020 Labor Day fires, this means paying
particular attention to the needs of the Latine (Latine) community of Southern Oregon.
This programmatic approach includes, at a minimum:


    •   Funding Culturally Specific Organizations (CSOs)
           o OHCS has set up partnerships with CSOs to enable these organizations to continue and expand their disaster recovery
              work. We know that success in recovery will require working with these organizations. Many survivors, particularly
              those with painful memories of racial and other injustices inflicted by government organizations, will prefer to work
              with a trusted, familiar organization. OHCS will work with CSOs to provide robust, comprehensive assistance to
              culturally specific populations (in particular, the Latine community impacted by the Almeda Fire) as a fundamental
              strategy to meet the universal goals in the ReOregon Action Plan.

    •   Phasing of Application Processing
           o A very common outcome of disaster recovery operations is that higher income applicants tend to move through the
               system and receive assistance faster. This frequently leaves the most vulnerable populations behind. OHCS has phased
               applicant intake to focus on lower-income populations first and has paired this strategy with the CSO partnerships
               outlined above. This strategy will significantly increase the number of lower-income, vulnerable, and BIPOC survivors
               who receive help through recovery programs.



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                            107BF02
                                                                  469
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
    •   Robust Tracking
           o OHCS will use a custom “system of record” to manage all program activity. This system of record will securely house
               survivor data. Program applications will collect information on survivor race and ethnicity, among many other
               variables. By creating this robust system, OHCS will be able to track and report on benefits provided by race and
               ethnicity and will be able to track progress.

    •   Executing Agreements with ERJ Requirements
           o For any program that includes an implementation partner, subrecipient, or project sponsor, OHCS will include
               requirements around ERJ and Minority, Women, and Emerging Small Business (MWESB) practices in binding
               agreements. These requirements will include outreach and engagement criteria as well as reporting and performance
               tracking directions.

    •   Centering BIPOC, Latine, and Vulnerable Populations in Policy
           o OHCS will focus on establishing policies designed to ensure recovery programs center BIPOC, Latine, and Vulnerable
               Populations, and maximize benefits for low-income disaster survivors. Examples of this include, but are not limited
               to:
                   ▪ No citizenship requirements for program benefits
                   ▪ Prioritizing homeownership opportunities (helping renters become homeowners)
                   ▪ Providing zero-interest, forgivable, receding loans
                   ▪ Creating Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) opportunities for low-income families as a source of future revenue
                   ▪ Prioritizing resident-owned or co-op park models, and non-profit or governmental development partners to
                      ensure long-term affordability at preserved parks.

Objectives and Performance Measures
OHCS is required to track and report on projected outcomes (e.g., number of units rehabilitated, etc.) as well as program expenditures
for all CDBG-DR programs. In addition, OHCS must report on direct and indirect beneficiaries within HUD’s system of record –
Disaster Recovery Grants Reporting (DRGR) System. OHCS collects monthly reporting from subrecipients to inform the Quarterly
Performance Reports that must be submitted to HUD – these reports include a narrative description of project/program progress,
demographic information on beneficiaries, and expenditure reporting. For directly managed programs, OHCS will be collecting and
tracking applicant demographic information, program pipeline and applicant status, which will all be used to report to HUD and will
be used to measure progress against projected outcomes. The data from these reports and OHCS’s System of Record (SOR) can be



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                           107BF02
                                                                 470
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
aggregated to track and report out as needed, provided the reports do not include any personally identifiable or other sensitive
information.

HARP is designed to achieve equity and resilience goals, which are measured by the number of wildfire-impacted owner occupants
helped, with an initial priority given to low-income households who are not yet living in their permanent replacement/rehabilitated
home. The program construction standards will increase the resilience of the housing stock to future disasters; the structure of the
award (100% grant) helps homeowners return to affordable and sustainable housing situations. Within OHCS vendor and subrecipient
agreements, there are contractual requirements around tracking, reporting, and timelines for completing specific tasks, all of which
OHCS will use to track and analyze whether the programs are meeting the goals.


Data Analysis and Evaluation
For Subrecipient-managed programs (e.g., PIER, IHA, HOP) – OHCS will establish clear performance measures for each
project/subrecipient agreement, which will be used as the basis for award. Subrecipients are required to report monthly on the status of
their project or program, in line with the performance measures and beneficiary information included in their approved application
and/or subrecipient agreement.
For HARP - Equitable recovery will be measured by the number of households assisted and through an analysis of the demographics
information of those households assisted, to identify whether trends anywhere in the process that indicate whether we are or are not
meeting the program equity and resilience goals. Some of the elements require OHCS vendors and subrecipients to track data on
applicants include:
    - Outreach Numbers
    - Intake Numbers, with breakdown of the following types of information for program applicants:
            o Demographics
            o Income
            o Damaged housing type
            o Recovery status
            o Household composition
    - Eligibility determination outcomes and timeline
    - Award calculations and challenges resulting from duplication of benefits
    - Grant agreement timelines and obstacles
    - Timeline for completing construction/rehousing

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                             107BF02
                                                                   471
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
   -   Assistance provided to tenants eligible under the Uniform Relocation Act

For all programs, OHCS will use the data and information collected from OHCS and vendor program implementers, the information
in the SOR, and the OHCS monitoring team to determine whether program policies need to be updated/amended and/or if those
amendments trigger a need for amending the ReOregon Action Plan.


Quantifying Results
State-Funded Programs: During the 2021 session, the Oregon Legislature allocated $150.4 million, as part of House Bill 5006, to
Oregon Housing and Community Services to assist fire survivors and their communities with recovery from the 2020 Labor Day fires.
As of April 30, 2024, OHCS has spent $98.0 million in what had been appropriated as General Fund and $24.7 million in Lottery Bond
proceeds, for a total of $122.7 million spent. Approximately $36 million of the $150.4 million was allocated to the Wildfire Recovery
and Resilience Account (WRRA) program, which supported 450 households with intermediate/temporary housing over the life of the
program and supported 867 households with permanent recovery. Over 65% of the households supported were at 80% area median
income or below.
With the support of state wildfire funding, more than 100 units are expected to be move-in ready by the end of 2024 across Lincoln,
Lane, and Jackson counties.
ReOregon: Under the Homeowner Assistance and Reconstruction Program (HARP), OHCS expects to provide awards during the
2023-25 biennium to reconstruct or replace 500 new homes, based on current results from the HARP eligibility questionnaire,
program estimates as reported to HUD and an average unit cost of $250,000. OHCS expects that while the majority of awards for
reconstruction/replacement of the 500 homes will be made in the 2023-25 biennium, due to construction/replacement timelines, many
of those homes will be ready for occupancy in the 2025-27 biennium.

OHCS will allocate $89 million in funding under the Homeownership Opportunities Program (HOP) directly to counties or county-
approved subrecipients to support affordable housing development for fire-impacted renters. Depending on program design developed
by each county and based on an average unit cost of $300,000-$400,000, OHCS expects to facilitate the construction or
purchase/rehabilitation of 100 homes in the 2025-27 biennium.
To date, 16 Planning, Infrastructure and Economic Revitalization (PIER) projects have been selected by local selection committees
and two of those projects are ready for award. OHCS anticipates funding approximately nine projects at the end of the current biennium.
Recognizing that complex projects may take multiple years to complete, it is anticipated that the majority of project activities will take

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                             107BF02
                                                                   472
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
place in the 2025-27 biennium. Economic revitalization projects or any project that has direct, individual beneficiaries will also generate
data on number of people served.
OHCS anticipates serving about 215 households, on average, each month under the Intermediate Housing Assistance (IHA) program,
beginning in January 2025 and continuing to provide services into the 2025-27 biennium.
OHCS expects that the majority of activity under the Housing Recovery Services (HRS) program will take place during the current
biennium and at the beginning of the 2025-27 biennium when the program is launched at the beginning of 2025. The program expects
to serve approximately 150 households in the current biennium.

The majority of activity under Legal Services will take place during the current biennium when the program is launched at the
beginning of 2025. The program expects to serve approximately 150 households, but the final number served will be based on the
need for legal assistance.
The Resilience Planning Program (RPP) is expected to be launched near the end of the current biennium/beginning of the 2025-27
biennium. OHCS expects to complete two projects under RPP.

Central Services Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:                     Healthy and Safe Communities, A Thriving Statewide Economy
Program Contact:                          Caleb Yant, Deputy Director




*For 2021-23, Disaster Recovery & Resilience’s actuals were recorded in Central Services, but have been removed from this display.

Program Overview
The Central Services program unit provides essential leadership and business support crucial to achieving the vision of the agency: All Oregonians
have the opportunity to pursue prosperity and live free from poverty. Central Services includes the Director’s Office, the Research Office, the Public
Affairs Office, the Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Office, Human Resources Office, and the Central Services Division that consists of the Business
Services Section, the Chief Finance Section, the Procurement and Grant Compliance Section, the Accounting Section, Project Management office, and
Information Services.


Program Funding Request
OHCS added new positions to support operations in all program units, including creating a new Emergency Response and Resiliency Section. This
section will provide a centralized agency response to disasters that impact housing for Oregonians, to include the ongoing COVID-19 health pandemic.
In this biennium, the section will administer $90 million in General Fund and lottery bond proceeds to assist wildfire survivors.

*For 2021-23, Disaster Recovery & Resilience’s actuals were recorded in Central Services, but have been removed from this display.


Program Description
The Central Services program area represents the business support functions within the agency and includes 170.6 FTE. In order to provide clarity for
internal and external readers, the budget format features the key responsibility areas of the agency: Housing Stabilization Programs, Affordable Rental
Housing Programs, Homeownership Programs, the Homeownership Stabilization Initiative, and Bond Activities and Debt Service. Central Services
supports all of these program areas. Centralizing the administrative and operational functions for all areas of the agency enables greater efficiency and
facilitates better assessment of program delivery effectiveness. OHCS has leaned in and prioritized the emphasis of centering Diversity, Equity, and
Inclusion practices and procedures, the Central Service support is instrumental to the success of these priorities.
Central Services provides essential business services that support the agency’s leadership and workforce to achieve the Department’s mission: To
provide stable and affordable housing and engage leaders to develop integrated statewide policy that addresses poverty and provides opportunity for
Oregonians.
Program Justification
The Central Services program unit provides support to the entire agency, contributing to the agency’s work to support Oregonians through the housing
continuum. The guidance and support to the other program units at OHCS sets the stage for OHCS to identify program outcomes that are equitable
and effective, allowing for data driven policies, strategies, and decision-making. Central Services is responsible for ensuring the agency has sound
business processes and principles, and well-trained workforce to deliver the services. Central Services also helps promote the work of the agency to
key partners, ensure community needs are centered in programming and resources are leveraged with other public and private funds.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                                107BF02
                                                                                                           488
                                                BUDGET NARRATIVE
Program Performance
Performance measures within Central Services vary across business function, however common drivers include compliance with rules and regulations,
effective communication with stakeholders at all levels, effective business processes, and equitable distribution of resources to communities across
Oregon grounded in a racial justice lens. Relatedly, OHCS actively participates in statewide efforts to centralize work across the enterprise and with
other government entities to streamline administrative functions and be more efficient and effective.
Funding Streams
All funding sources in the agency contribute to Central Services program costs. Based on the agency’s HUD-approved cost allocation plan, the relative
benefit received by each program pays for the allocated share of costs that contribute toward the Central Services program area. The agency’s flexible
funding sources, derived mostly from self-supporting activities such as the bond-financed loan programs, administration of non-competitive federal
tax credits, and contract duties performed for HUD, subsidize programs that do not provide sufficient revenue to cover costs.
Comparison of 2025-27 Funding Proposal to 2023-25 Funding
The Central Services funding includes additional staff required for deliver on Diversity Equity and Inclusion objectives, emergency management and
strategic management requirements, data and research objectives, information technology support including project management efforts, HR support,
capital market executions and contracts and procurement support. OHCS received significant funding increases over the past three biennia, however
the increase in staff was proportionally less than the increase in funding. These positions are now required to effectively support the department’s
mission and goals at current service level funding.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                         489
                                                  BUDGET NARRATIVE
Central Services Programs Description
The Central Services program provides essential leadership and business support crucial to achieving the vision of the agency: All Oregonians have
the opportunity to pursue prosperity and live free from poverty. Central Services includes the Director’s and Deputy Director’s Offices; the Research
Office; the Public Affairs Division; the Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Office; Human Resources Office; and the Central Services Division that consists
of Business Services, the Controller’s Office (including Accounting, Budget, and Fiscal Monitoring & Compliance Sections), Procurement and
Contracts Section, Project Management Office, and Information Services.
Director’s & Deputy Director’s Offices
The Director’s and Deputy Director’s Offices provide overall leadership, policy direction, and administrative oversight for the department. The
Housing Stability Council consists of nine members who are appointed by the Governor. As established in statute, the role of the Council is summarized
through six themes:
       1. Helps establish strategic direction and policy framework for OHCS
       2. Helps the Director to foster constructive partnerships with other state agencies and key partners engaged in housing and community
          services
       3. Sets policy for and approves loans, grants, and funding awards
       4. Advises policymakers
       5. Informs the Director’s annual operating plan and biennial budget, and oversees OHCS operations through regular reports from the
          Director
       6. Advocates at all levels on behalf of the department and affordable housing
Research Division
The Research and Analysis section supports the entire agency and has team members focused on and embedded within specific program areas. The
team consists of research analysts, operations and policy analysts, and an economist. The outcome of the quantitative and qualitative analysis performed
by the research section serves as the basis for how a significant portion of OHCS’s resources are allocated and/or awarded. Additionally, the research
section leads efforts to share data across state agencies, with local governments, and with partner organizations, to enable better outcomes for vulnerable
Oregonians.

Strategic planning includes the creation and implementation of the 5-year Statewide Housing Plan and establishing and monitoring annual goals. A
key component of this planning is establishing outcomes and metrics by which the agency can measure the success of its programs.


Public Affairs Division


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                           490
                                                  BUDGET NARRATIVE
The Public Affairs Division consists of three teams, the Government Relations Section, the Communications Section, and the Engagement Team.
Public Affairs is the primary Division that engages with the public, elected officials and stakeholders to support legislative coordination, community
outreach, communications, rules coordination, and records management.
Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Division
The Equity, Diversity & Inclusion (EDI) Office shepherds the agency’s Equity and Racial Justice work by engaging, internally, with executive
leadership, management, and staff to develop and implement plans designed to meet the agency’s goal of a diverse and inclusive workforce and tracking
agency wide progress toward meeting the Equity and Racial Justice goals of the agency’s strategic plan, the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP). The
EDI Office also engages externally, with partners, stakeholders, and communities to ensure OHCS’ efforts improve service equity to Oregonians.
Lastly, the EDI Office provides vision, direction, counsel, and leadership to all employees in areas of equity, racial justice, diversity, and inclusion, in
their daily work, and in the programs and activities of the agency.
Human Resources Office
The Human Resources Office supports the agency by providing services such as employee recruitment and retention, employee training activities,
leadership development, and workforce planning.
Central Service Division
The Central Services Division is comprised of core sections that support the agency to achieve its mission. This includes accounting, finance, fiscal
compliance, procurement and grants, budget, information systems, and project management. Work performed in this division ensures equity,
accountability, transparency, stewardship, and security of state resources.
   •   The Business Services Section provides administrative and operational support activities such as facilities management, worksite
       safety/security, non-IT inventory, reception, travel coordination, and mail and shipping processing duties.
   •   The Controller’s Office provides services to the agency that include budgeting, accounting, and grants and procurement. The primary objectives
       of the Controller’s Office are effective stewardship of agency and federal funds, compliance with rules and regulations, and financial stability,
       including identification of opportunities to generate additional mission-driven funding.
   •   The Procurement and Contracts Section provides procurement and contract services for OHCS, an area of unique complexity given OHCS is
       statutorily provided with its own procurement authority.
   •   The Project Management Office (PMO) provides support for initiatives throughout OHCS. The office provides business analysts and project
       managers for both information technology projects and non-IT projects. This support provides efficiency and structure to usher projects from
       initiation to implementation. The PMO provides valuable services that ensure successful outcomes for agency initiatives.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                            491
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
  •   The Information Services Section supports the agency’s information technology needs, including network administration, application
      development, and desktop support functions.

Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services: This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development
a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-
ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will be 4.2 percent. This package includes the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided
by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs: Phased-In programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than
24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24 months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27
base budget level.
Phase-Out’s are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27
biennium.

Phase-In for the Central Services Division
   • General Funds
           o Research and Analysis section HPRL Economist - $16k

Phase-Out’s for the Central Services Division
   • General Funds
          o Oregon Housing Needs Assessment - $484k
          o Executive Oreder Administration Positions - $2.7
          o Administrative Services and Supplies - $119k
          o Technical Assistance & Language Access - $1.7M
   • Federal Funds
          o Administrative costs related to CDBG - $682k

030 Inflation and Price List Adjustments: This package contains adjustments for inflation and other price list adjustments including State
Government Service Charges and Usage Based Charges as directed by Department of Administrative Services. Examples below.
   •   Standard Inflation 4.2%
   •   Professional Services 6.8%
   •   Attorney General 23.26%
   •   State Government Service Charges 33%



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                  493                                                             107BF02
                                                       BUDGET NARRATIVE
050 Fund Shift: This package contains adjustments to fund shift. This package was used to better align funding in the Central Services division to
match to the agency funding splits for cost allocation purposes. This package also contains the fund shift for lease payments to align funding with the
agency funding.

Policy Package # 508 CSD Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

For Central Services, this package restores six permanent full-time positions to provide essential support for the proposed funding of
shelter maintenance, rehousing, rental assistance and eviction prevention; reauthorizes a limited duration full-time position previously
authorized to support HB 3395 for agriculture housing and affordable homeownership loans; and adds six positions to support
workload in the Director’s Office, finance, procurement, project management, and government relations. Moves on position from
Bond and Debt programs to Central Services.

Staffing Impact

Accountant 2, Permanent Full Time
Budget and Fiscal Administrator 3, Permanent Full Time

Quantifying Results
As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly
around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Policy Package # 509 Information Technology Support
Package Description -

This policy option package has two components - the Grant Management System and ProLink.

GMS: This policy option package will support modernization of grants management, improving the agency’s ability to deliver funds,
track and manage grants, and provide reports to track services and financial spending more effectively. A more comprehensive,
modern grants management system would enable the agency to receive and deliver funds more effectively and efficiently, allowing us
to provide service to our partners more easily. Current grant management processes are inconsistent and rely on a combination of
complex spreadsheets and proprietary applications. . This package will allow OHCS to create and implement a system that addresses
the challenges associated with delivering funds and ease our partners' burdens and challenges in reporting on their use of funds. The
evolving scope and scale of grants management necessitate . the modernization of proprietary software solutions on a cloud-based
platform leading to improved performance, better user experience, and enhanced security. ProLink: ProLink HFA is the established
data system of record for the Affordable Rental Housing Division. The plan is to keep this system in full use while supporting
improved functionality of ProLink HFA. This requires implementing needed system enhancements that will improve the user
experience and data quality overall. This package will allow OHCS to continue the use of the ARH data systems and continue to
address the challenges associated with delivering funds and monitoring partner performance while ensuring data integrity. The
growing scale of funds delivery for affordable rental housing requires the maintenance and enhancement of our established data
systems. This includes covering the known annual costs, intentional planning for known needed system enhancements and preparation
for contingencies that may arise.

Purpose

GMS: A priority in our Statewide Housing Plan is to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and
systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity. We have determined that
using disjointed grant management systems and spreadsheets is not equitable for partners across programmatic divisions of the
agency, and OHCS needs to invest in resources to remove the barriers by streamlining grant management. OHCS is not only growing
in agency staff, but also in the programs and dollars funneled through the agency. It is crucial that we invest in systems that can
adequately manage the increasing number and complexity of grants for the long term. New programs, including the emergency
response effort, rapid organizational growth rate, and an increasing number of partner organizations to deliver grants in alignment with
legislative direction, have highlighted the need to modernize and enhance how we manage the entire grant process.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  516
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Investment in our proprietary software will allow us to implement a comprehensive system to ensure our efforts remain consistent and
sustainable as we grow. We are fortunate to have skilled developers to provide much of our needs historically, but we must
proactively mitigate risks associated with the use aging architecture by shifting to a cloud platform that will allow for greater
flexibility in enhancements to the applications. This shift will allow for continuation of operations in the event of outages to the State
Network during an emergency. Unprecedented emergency response efforts have illustrated the need to be agile and adaptive. This
package will enable OHCS to build resiliency in such circumstances.

ProLink: Continued investment in the established data system will allow us to ensure our efforts remain consistent and sustainable as
we grow. We have been fortunate to have obtained ProLink HFA following direction from a Secretary of State audit finding to reduce
the use of ad hoc data tracking tools and custom systems that use aging architecture. Unprecedented emergency response efforts in
affordable rental housing production have illustrated the need to be agile and adaptive. This package will enable OHCS to maintain
resiliency in such circumstances.

How Achieved

GMS: OHCS will use existing funds from flexible resources and administrative allowances from program funds, if available, to
develop this system. Enterprise Information Services is aware of our decision to move from Oracle into the cloud platform This
package will enable many improvements in delivering essential housing-related grants to Oregonians in this biennium (25-27) and
beyond, including:

Outreach and engagement opportunities – Communication could be significantly streamlined, allowing more diverse stakeholders to
participate in various points of the granting process. Configurable portals would allow OHCS to collaborate more efficiently, and
marketing tools would enable us to reach more Oregonians in less time.

Standardization and automation opportunities – Several tasks are currently done manually in various systems that could be
standardized, which would reduce duplication of efforts and create a system of record, as well as reducing opportunities for errors.
Automating processes can free up staff to spend more time delivering services and less time compiling disjointed information for
review and analysis.

Increased compliance and transparency – Data could be more accessible via modern connections to other record systems with APIs.
Easily configurable permissions would give stakeholders appropriate views into relevant data and statuses. A modernized solution
would provide more opportunities to track regulatory compliance obligations effectively.

                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Improved planning capabilities – Better tools to manage resources, documents, and data would enable OHCS to better plan and act
quickly. This investment would also help us to identify, monitor, and mitigate risks associated with our core business processes.

Alignment with broader State of Oregon Enterprise Information Services (EIS) goals, strategies, and values - Process overviews and
reporting functionality will increase accountability, a core EIS value. Portals for internal and external users will make us more
customer-focused and collaborative. Adoption of a modernized grants management system is aligned with the cloud-first approach to
IT investments and will increase a data-informed culture.

Enhanced abilities to evaluate and measure grant performance – The desired solution would allow the ability to integrate desired
outcomes into applications, reports, and dashboards that display progress toward goals.

OHCS has developed an implementation plan for the grant management modernization in this investment request. To achieve these
goals, the following activities must take place:

   1.Appropriate resource allocation for project management and project members. This includes the
          a. initial project planning to deliver a lift and shift minimum viable product
          b. stakeholder engagement,
          c. agile development schedules
          d. Testing plans, QA
   2. Begin the process of enhancing the base software product in the final quarter of 2025. We will use the agile methodology to
       develop the software product with a heavy focus on user experience (UX) and functionality.


ProLink:

   • Continued, ongoing use of ProLink HFA-Maintenance of the system for ongoing and improved serviceability.
   • Compliance Audit Module Improvements-This module is currently designed to enter information following a compliance audit
        and tenant file review and then generate a report to be sent to owner/agents. It is our intent to submit a CR to ProLink to re-
        build and enhance this module to be more dynamic in function. This will remove the need for compliance staff to write a Word
        Doc report to be sent and then also follow up with data entry into ProLink. Modifying the module will allow compliance staff
        to enter all compliance audit details and tenant file findings and then generate the report. This will improve the overall process
        and improve efficiencies. In turn, the modification will allow accurate data input to the ProLink system for ongoing

services provided, and with what funding sources. This will allow OHCS to build the system infrastructure and improve the efficacy
of our programs. It will also provide the ability to track how quickly funds are disbursed to partners, which will enable us to evaluate
and measure grantee performance. In addition to internal efficiencies, the system will improve access to grants, uploading required
documentation, and reporting for grantees.
