Equity and Racial Justice Impact

GMS: Equity and Racial Justice is a guiding principle of Oregon’s Statewide Housing Plan. A modern grants management system
would improve our ability to deliver on goals to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and
systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity. Our existing grants
management solutions are not designed to facilitate these goals, especially in a manner that reflects their urgency and importance. We
are not alone in these goals, and that is evidenced by the growing number of existing grants management software solutions which
contain components specifically tooled to identify and address inequities among various demographics. Better grant monitoring will
also improve accountability measures for grantees, allowing OHCS to identify and intervene if grantee service trends indicate a barrier
to delivery on these goals. We must have systems in place to collect appropriate information, track outcomes, and analyze
deficiencies. This package will give us the needed system improvements to do just that.

ProLink: Equity and Racial Justice is a guiding principle of Oregon’s Statewide Housing Plan. Maintaining and enhancing ProLink
HFA data systems will continue to improve our ability to deliver on goals to advance equity and racial justice by identifying and
addressing institutional and systemic barriers that have created and perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic
prosperity. This data system will also continue to improve accountability measures for grantees, allowing OHCS to identify and
intervene if delivery of funds for affordable rental housing trends indicate a barrier to delivery on these goals. We must have systems
in place to collect appropriate information, track outcomes, and analyze deficiencies. This package will give continue to provide
OHCS the needed system with improvements to do just that.

Bond Activities and Debt Service Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:   Healthy and Safe Communities, A Thriving Statewide Economy
Program Contact:        Matthew Harris, Caleb Yant


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                107BF02
                                                      529
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE




Program Overview
Oregon Housing and Community Services’ (OHCS) bond-financed loan programs provide safe and affordable rental housing to low-
income Oregonians and provide opportunities for first-time homebuyers to finance their mortgages at below-market interest rates. Bond
related activities include disbursement of bond proceeds to finance multifamily and single-family mortgage loans, bond issuance costs,
administrative expenses related to outstanding debt, and asset-protection costs associated with foreclosures and acquired properties.
OHCS Debt Service activities represent expenditures that repay investors and other parties’ obligations owed on the outstanding debt
issued by the agency to finance various loan program activities.
Program Funding Request
The funding for 2025-27 includes eight positions (8.0 FTE) and related Services and Supplies, non-limited Other Funds for program
and debt service costs, General Fund and Lottery Funds for debt service on bonds issued in prior biennia, and costs of issuance for
additional General Obligation and Lottery bonds requested in policy packages.

Program Description
OHCS’ Debt Management Team is responsible for all activities related to the debt issuance and on-going administration of the
department’s various bond programs. This includes ensuring compliance with all federal tax regulations, state laws, bond covenants
and other contractual requirements related to the tax-exempt bonds issued by OHCS, including all reporting and continuing disclosure
requirements related to these bonds. The Debt Management Team is also responsible for investment activities related to all funds in
these various bond programs and oversees all loan servicing activities, including loan payment processing, foreclosure, mortgage
insurance claims processing and disposition of acquired properties for the mortgage loans financed by the department’s various bond
programs.
Bond-Related Activities provide the mechanism to expend funds related to OHCS bond financed loan programs. OHCS sells tax-exempt
bonds to investors and uses the proceeds to finance multifamily and single-family mortgage loans.
For multifamily housing bonds, projects financed with tax-exempt bonds must meet either of the following requirements: 1) 40 percent
of the project’s units at rents that would be affordable to persons at 60 percent of area-median income or less; or 2) 20 percent of the
project’s unit at rents that would be affordable to persons at 50 percent of area-median income or less. For single-family housing bonds,
this includes borrower eligibility requirements that generally restrict the program to owner-occupied properties, first-time homebuyers
with incomes that do not exceed area median income, and purchase price limits established by the federal government.
OHCS’ residential (single-family) loan program utilizes a network of local banks and mortgage companies located throughout Oregon
to reserve, underwrite and originate mortgage loans for eligible borrowers. Participation by these lenders is critical to the success of the
residential loan program. OHCS uses bond proceeds to purchase loans from these lenders which remain in OHCS’ single-family loan
portfolio.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                                 107BF02
                                                                         531
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Debt Service expenditures represent the repayment of amounts borrowed from investors, the proceeds from which provide the funding
for OHCS’ Bond-Related Activities. These expenditures represent the largest portion of OHCS’s budget each biennium.
OHCS sells tax-exempt bonds to investors and uses the proceeds to finance multifamily and residential (single-family) mortgage loans.
Investors play a key role in the success of this program. Since interest earned from these bonds is generally exempt from federal income
tax, investors are willing to accept lower interest yields on these investments. This results in reduced borrowing costs, which are passed
on to borrowers in the form of below-market interest rates on their loans.
As of July 15, 2024, OHCS had the following amounts of direct revenue bonds and Elderly and Disabled Housing Bonds outstanding:
                                       Indenture                                           Outstanding Bonds (as of July 15, 2024)
  Mortgage Revenue Bonds (Single-Family Mortgage Program)                                                               $1,002,445,000
  Housing Revenue Bonds (Single-Family Mortgage Program)                                                                            $0
  Multifamily Housing Revenue Bonds                                                                                        $18,060,000
     Total Direct Revenue Bonds                                                                                         $1,020,505,000
  Elderly and Disabled Housing Bonds                                                                                       $19,030,000
     Total State of Oregon General Obligation Bonds                                                                        $19,030,000
  Total Outstanding Bonds (excluding Pass Through Revenue Bonds)                                                        $1,039,535,000

Program Justification
OHCS bond activities help ensure all Oregonians have access to decent housing which meets their basic needs and allows them to reach
their full potential by providing the funding mechanism that creates affordable housing for low to median income Oregonians. The
program unit also protects OHCS bond programs and the reputation of all State of Oregon bond programs by managing the agency’s
debt service obligations. A default on OHCS’ debt service obligations would likely result in an immediate ratings downgrade, which
not only would preclude the agency from being able to issue future debt, but more importantly, would potentially cause the agency to
violate various covenants in bond documents and other related agreements, creating additional liability and other serious financial
conditions or result in costly litigation for these bond programs and the agency.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   532
                                                               BUDGET NARRATIVE
Program Performance
The primary performance measure for Bond Related Activities is the number of affordable housing units financed for multifamily and
single-family housing. The following table illustrates performance for the past ten fiscal years:

figures are based on loans closed in each fiscal year.
Enabling Legislation and Program Authorization
OHCS’ bond-financed loan programs are authorized throughout ORS Chapter 456. In addition, Article XI-I (2) of the Oregon
Constitution authorizes that bonds used to finance multifamily housing for elderly and disabled persons be issued as general obligation
bonds of the State of Oregon, and establishes a limit of Elderly and Disabled Housing Bonds that may be outstanding at any given time
in an amount equal to or not to exceed 0.5 percent of true cash value of all taxable property in the State (as of January 1, 2023, this
amount is about $5.116 billion).
ORS 456.661 establishes a limit for all outstanding direct revenue bonds issued by OHCS in an amount not to exceed $2.5 billion. ORS
456.692 exempts pass-through revenue bonds issued by OHCS from the outstanding debt limitation established by ORS 456.661.
Specific legislation passed each biennium establishes OHCS’ biennial new issuance limits by debt category and allocates federal “private
activity bond” authority to the agency for the following two calendar years. Debt service payments related to OHCS bond-financed loan
programs are considered Other Funds – Non-Limited for budgetary purposes.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                                       107BF02
                                                                                                533
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Funding Streams
Proceeds from the sale of OHCS bonds provide the funding stream for purchasing or financing loans and funding bond indenture required
reserves. All of OHCS’ bond-financed loan programs are self-supporting activities; as such, all debt service expenditures are funded
solely from borrower’s payments received on loans financed under each indenture, interest earnings on invested indenture funds and
reserves, proceeds from the sale of acquired properties and any recoveries from mortgage insurance related to these foreclosed properties.
No other state or federal revenues are received to support the debt service payments or other costs of these programs. Although restricted
by federal tax law and bond indenture restrictions, income earned over and above amounts needed to cover debt service and program
administrative expenses can be used to pay the general operating expenses of the agency. Loan purchases/financing, bond issuance
costs and asset protection are Other Funds (Non-Limited) expenditures and administrative expenses are Other Funds (Limited) in the
OHCS budget.
Successful financing of multifamily housing projects with tax-exempt bond proceeds usually requires the leveraging of multiple sources
of capital. In addition to the proceeds of tax-exempt bonds, most multifamily housing projects utilize other funding sources such as 4%
housing tax credits, state and federal grants, federal housing subsidies, local property tax abatement, subordinated debt or other funding
sources from local governments, deferred developer’s fees, and other capital contributions. For conduit bond issues, lenders are also
often able to take advantage of the Oregon Affordable Housing Tax Credit (OAHTC), a lenders tax credit, which helps them effectively
lower the interest offered to borrowers.
This program unit also has limitation for costs of issuance and debt service on general obligation and lottery bonds issued on OHCS’
behalf by the Department of Administrative Services. OHCS receives the proceeds from these bonds but does not perform any of the
work to manage these debts other than as required by the Department of Administrative Services.
Comparison of 2025-27 Funding Proposal to 2023-25 Funding
The Other Funds budget, including Limited, Non-Limited, and Debt Service, is stable compared to the 2023-25 budget. One additional
position is requested in 2025-27 due to capacity constraints as OHCS continues to issue high levels of conduit bonds and potentially
new multifamily direct revenue bonds. Lottery Funds Debt Service and General Fund Debt Service are adjusted for bonds issued in
previous biennia and additional bonds proposed in 2025-27.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   534
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Bond Activities and Debt Service Description
The Capital Markets and Debt Management section supports the organization by executing on a variety of municipal bond and secondary
capital market financial transactions. This section ensures OHCS retains its bond indenture ratings while providing critical funding to
agency programs on top of state and federal appropriations.
Investors play a key role in the success of this program. Since interest earned from these bonds is generally exempt from federal income
tax, investors are willing to accept lower interest yields on these investments. This results in reduced borrowing costs, which are passed
on to borrowers in the form of below-market interest rates on their loans.
OHCS uses two different approaches to finance multifamily housing projects with revenue bond loan programs. One approach involves
the issuance of direct revenue bonds in which OHCS underwrites multifamily housing projects and directly finances these projects with
bond proceeds. These loans remain in OHCS’ multifamily loan portfolio.
The other approach involves the issuance of pass-through (“conduit”) revenue bonds. These bonds are issued as “no obligation” debt
of OHCS and the State of Oregon, who are simply providing borrowers with access to lower financing rates in the tax-exempt market.
Most conduit revenue bonds are sold as private placements to large commercial banks. As the bond purchaser, these banks underwrite
the projects and negotiate specific transaction terms with the borrower. The success of this program is largely dependent upon the
capacity of commercial banks that operate in Oregon to participate as lenders.
Expenditures related to OHCS’ bond-financed loan programs include the following:
           •   Disbursement of lendable bond proceeds to purchase single-family loans and finance multifamily housing loans from
               non-conduit bond issues, as well as amounts disbursed to borrowers as down payment assistance generated from certain
               single-family bond structures.
           •   Bond issuance costs that include all charges for professional services (bond underwriters, attorneys, financial advisors,
               trustees, etc.) incurred when bonds are issued to assure compliance with all state, federal and investor requirements.
           •   Administrative expenses related to outstanding debt (trustee fees, State Treasury assessments, legal and financial advisory
               services, bond liquidity and remarketing fees, etc.) that are necessary to assure compliance with all covenants to bond
               holders and federal tax law requirements for the entire period that bonds remain outstanding.
           •   Asset protection expenses that include all costs associated with acquiring and maintaining foreclosed properties necessary
               to preserve OHCS’ claims to mortgage insurance proceeds, keep properties in marketable condition and prevent
               properties from becoming blights on the communities in which they are located.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   535
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
The success of new loan production in tax-exempt bond financed programs is largely affected by general economic conditions, and
current financial markets. The extended period of historically low conventional mortgage interest rates has continued to make financing
affordable housing at below-market rates with tax-exempt bond programs difficult.
For multifamily housing projects financed through OHCS bond programs, reduced borrowing costs to developers and federal tax law
affordability requirements result in decreased monthly rents for qualified tenants, as well as affordable housing opportunities for
vulnerable populations, such as elderly and disabled persons.
Single-family loans financed through OHCS bond programs provide qualified first-time homebuyers the opportunity to move from
being renters to homeowners. When persons advance through the housing continuum into home ownership, it helps to free up existing
multifamily housing stock, thereby increasing affordable housing opportunities for all Oregonians.
Both multifamily and single-family loan production also stimulates economic activity in communities and statewide. Some economic
models suggest that for every $1 million of bond proceeds used to finance multifamily or single-family loans, between 8.6 and 14.6
direct and indirect jobs are created or saved statewide. This includes jobs in construction, banking, real estate, and other related services.
Current market conditions require more complexity in bond structures that often impact the size and timing of issuance. Even with the
housing crisis and Great Recession, OHCS has continuously accepted single family loan reservations since November 20213.
Multifamily loan production had increased substantially during the 2019-21 biennium. Since the inception of OHCS’ multifamily
housing pass-through revenue bond (“conduit”) program in 2000, the program has continued to be a popular financing tool for borrowers
who might have otherwise used one of OHCS’ non-conduit multifamily bond programs. This is presently the financing tool of choice
for most borrowers who finance multifamily housing through OHCS.
Bond Debt Service
These loan programs provide safe and affordable rental housing to low-income Oregonians and provide opportunities for first-time
homebuyers to finance their mortgages at below-market interest rates.
To qualify for federal tax-exemption, these bonds are subject to various federal requirements. For multifamily housing bonds, projects
financed with tax-exempt bonds must meet either of the following requirements: 1) 40% of the project’s units at rents that would be
affordable to persons at 60% of area-median income or less; or 2) 20% of the project’s units at rents that would be affordable to persons
at 50% of area-median income or less. For single-family housing bonds, this includes borrower eligibility requirements that generally
restrict the program to owner-occupied properties, first-time homebuyers with incomes that don’t exceed area median income, and
purchase price limits established by the federal government. These federal tax requirements also include many provisions that affect
the structure of bonds and can impact the timing and amount of debt service payments.
Expenditures related to OHCS’ Debt Service activities include the following:


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                         107BF02
                                                                     536
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
           •   Regular scheduled principal and interest payments on all of the OHCS’ outstanding direct revenue bonds and Elderly and
               Disabled Housing Bonds (which are self-supporting State of Oregon general obligation bonds). This does not include
               any debt service payments for any of OHCS’ outstanding pass-through revenue bonds (“conduits”), as these represent
               “no-obligation” debt of OHCS and the State of Oregon, the debt service from which is paid to investors directly from
               program borrowers.
           •   Payments of principal and related accrued interest related to early redemption of OHCS’ outstanding direct revenue bonds
               and Elderly and Disabled Housing Bonds. These early redemptions of bonds can occur as the result of the refunding of
               current outstanding debt, prepayment of mortgage loans, excess bond-financed reserve (which occurs when outstanding
               bonds are paid down) and unexpended bond proceeds. Federal tax law related to housing bonds also has various
               restrictions which require bonds to be redeemed prior to maturity; these relate primarily to single-family loan
               prepayments received after ten years from the date of the original bond issue.
           •   Net interest payments owed to counterparties pursuant to interest-rate exchange agreements (“swaps”). Swaps are used
               by OHCS to effectively hedge interest rate risk related to the variable rate portion of the agency’s outstanding debt. These
               agreements comply with strict swap policies of the State Treasury and OHCS, both of which closely monitor these swap
               activities on an on-going basis. These net interest payments are processed and made in conjunction with the regularly
               scheduled principal and interest payments for certain OHCS bonds issued under the indentures for Mortgage Revenue
               Bonds (Single-Family Mortgage Program).
           •   Arbitrage rebate or yield reduction payments owed to the federal government. Federal tax law generally requires that
               any investments earnings related to proceeds of tax-exempt bonds that exceed the federal tax bond yield of a specific
               bond issue be repaid to the U.S. Treasury. For investment earnings related to proceeds of tax-exempt bonds in specific
               funds and accounts not subject to arbitrage rebate requirements, other federal restrictions may still require payments to
               the U.S. Treasury notwithstanding the absence of an arbitrage liability.
OHCS Debt Service expenditures can vary greatly between years and are largely affected by general economic conditions and current
financial markets. Debt service expenditures are impacted by both new bond issuance and early redemption of existing debt. During
periods of low interest rates, economic refunding opportunities can result in higher debt service expenditures as existing debt is paid off
and replaced with new, lower rate debt. Interest rate environments in which tax-exempt bond financing is favorable relative to
conventional market rate financing can result in increased tax-exempt debt issuance, which increases debt service expenditures in current
and future periods.
While Debt Service activities support OHCS’ Bond Related Activities, it is important to note that debt service expenditures extend up
to 40 years after the period in which units of affordable housing are financed.

Policy Package # 508 Bond & Debt Programs Portion of OHCS Operational Supports
Package Description
Agency-wide, this package reauthorizes three limited duration positions for additional months in the 2025-27 biennium; restores 24
positions that were authorized as permanent but were associated with the Governor’s Executive Orders on Homelessness and received
one-time funding; shifts funding for three positions to support manufactured housing; and requests 16 new positions to support affordable
rental housing, energy services, housing stabilization, and central support services.

Purpose
Provide essential staffing support for on-going priority affordable housing, homeownership, and homelessness prevention services.

How Achieved

Moves 2500089 Fiscal Analyst 3 to Central Services. The second part of this policy package provides funding for costs allocated to
support the Central Services Division. This program's portion is represented as an intrafund transfer out to support a proportionate
share of Pkg 508.

Staffing Impact

Moves position 2500089 Fiscal Analyst 3 to Central Services.

Quantifying Results
As OHCS works to be responsive to needs and to deliver resources and programs that ensure Oregonians are safely and stably housed,
we know we cannot do so without right-sized staffing to meet the diverse needs we see and hear about. This package allows us to
invest in our organizational health and ability to successfully meet the priorities of the Statewide Housing Plan (SWHP), particularly
around centering racial justice and equity in policymaking, program design and implementation, community engagement, and service
delivery, by ensuring we have the staff needed to operationalize our work.

Adequate staffing capacity across the agency allow equity goals to be achieved across agency work, including in central operations.

Policy Package #515: Rental Development: XI-Q for LIFT and PSH
Package Description
This package proposes to continue funding the development of affordable rental housing and permanent supportive housing through
Article XI-Q Bond investment. The Governor’s recommended budget includes $780 million total Article XI-Q bonds, of which $700
million will be allocated to the Local Innovation Fast Track (LIFT) program for affordable rental housing development and $80 million
of which will be used for development funding through the Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) program. Debt service payment for
Article XI-Q bonds are paid with General Fund resources.

Purpose

The need for housing and the capacity among affordable developing providers is high, so this package proposes to expand available
funding. The Article XI-Q Bond investment for affordable rental housing production proposed in this package aligns with Pillar 5 of
the Governor’s Housing Production Framework: “Funding and Programs Supporting Housing Production, Ongoing Funding for
Housing Development: A. Continue to maximize LIFT funding, low-income housing tax credits, and private activity bond allocations
for affordable rental and homeownership development.” LIFT program funding will continue to be the single most critical piece of the
agency’s efforts to quickly distribute resources for the development of more housing to meet the goals laid out in this framework as
well as the existing goals laid out in Executive Order 23-04.

Additionally, the Article XI-Q Bond investment for PSH development, coupled with proposals for services and rent assistance
funding, comprise a package that addresses the complex needs of unhoused persons who are unable to remain stably housed without
these supports. This program and the resources for it help to house some of the most challenging to house Oregonians through a
nationally recognized, evidence-based model. The State delivers this complex bundling of resources through a coordinated process at
OHCS. This package of proposals aligns with Pillar 2 of the Governor’s Housing Production Framework: “Core Program
Improvements, Permanent Supportive Housing.”

How Achieved

All funding for affordable rental housing is provided through the Oregon Centralized Application (ORCA), which makes OHCS’
funding process more efficient for both the agency and our development partners. In addition, the availability of Private Activity



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  554
                                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE
Bonds in the 2025-27 biennium means OHCS will once again have access to 4% LIHTC, which is often paired with XI-Q Bond
funding to maximize the impact of Federal resources that the state receives.

The XI-Q Bond investment for PSH development will be delivered in coordination with the funding for supportive services and rent
assistance that OHCS offers to ensure that tenants are being served in the holistic manner that the model calls for with as much fidelity
as possible.

For the Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit this reflects funds need for cost of issuance and debt service payments
associated with this bond request.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
From 2016 through 2023, OHCS delivered a total of $717,347,755 in LIFT funding across 6,723 units of affordable rental housing, for
an average of $106,701 in LIFT per unit.1 With a major caveat that costs are increasing across the board and that it will likely impact
per unit projections, the proposed $700 million in LIFT for rental housing and projecting the same average cost per unit forward
OHCS could support the development of approximately 4,500 total units of housing in the biennium. With the $80 million proposed
Article XI-Q Bond resources dedicated to PSH development which have higher per unit costs in general, OHCS anticipates that
approximately 500 units of PSH could be developed.
Racial Equity Strategy
This package will help address disparities in housing outcomes, including housing cost burden and homelessness, that exist for most
communities of color in Oregon.
Applicants seeking funding for rental development, including the funding proposed in this package, will do so through the ORCA,
which includes requirements and questions related to the Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing Plan; Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion
Agreement; equity and racial justice strategies; MWESB strategies; and engagement and community needs. This means all projects
seeking this funding will need to meet these racial equity-related requirements.

1 Affordable Rental Housing Dashboard | Tableau Public




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   555
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
Additionally, the ORCA involves funding set-asides and technical assistance for culturally specific organizations and Tribes, as well
as rural development by nonprofits. The ORCA prioritizes these entities because they have not received an equitable percentage of
resources from OHCS and other funders in the past, and they often must create projects with less capital, thinner margins, and greater
risks due to the needs in their tenant communities.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The ORCA collects more data on race and ethnicity for all funding applicants compared to previous funding rounds in several ways: a
clearer process to identify as a culturally specific organization; an option for applicants to note whether their project is owned by a
culturally specific organization or BIPOC-led entity; clear MWESB requirements; an enhanced Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion
Agreement; a question that asks about racial equity-specific organizational development; and an Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing
Plan that asks applicants to identify communities of color and other populations least likely to apply for housing and create strategies
to ensure people from those communities apply to live in their housing.
Additionally, the Affordable Rental Housing Division will be collecting continuous feedback on all aspects of the ORCA and will
iterate the ORCA as OHCS, and development teams continue to gain experience with it.
Administration
All applicants seeking capital funding through the ORCA will be required to meet regional MWESB requirements for percentage of
contracted work to these types of entities, as well as answer a set of questions about their strategies to ensure they do so. If applicants
have failed to meet MWESB requirements in the past, they will be required to work with an MWESB consultant. Additionally,
applicants must answer a set of questions relating to equity and racial justice strategies, and one of the strategies they can select from a
list of options relates to ownership by a culturally specific organization or BIPOC-led organization.

Revenue Sources

General Fund: $36,680,118

Other Funds:
     General Obligation Bonds: $5,420,000

Policy Package # 518 Homeownership Development: LIFT and HDIP
Package Description

This package supports two Homeownership Development programs that seek to increase the supply of affordable homes for purchase
in Oregon: a) an allocation of $100M in Article XIQ Bonds for the Local Innovations and Fast Track (LIFT) Homeownership
program; and b) an allocation of $50M in general funds for the Homeownership Development Incubator Program (HDIP).
Collectively, we expect that these two programs will lead to the construction of nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase.

Purpose

According to the Oregon Housing Needs Analysis, Oregon needs to increase the housing supply by 550,000 units over the next 20
years to keep pace with housing needs. Affordable units for purchase are an essential component of a healthy housing stock.
Affordable homeownership promotes community stability and continuity, as homeowners are more likely to stay in one place longer,
fostering stronger, more cohesive neighborhoods. By offering a pathway to economic mobility and stability, affordable
homeownership reduces the burden on rental markets and homelessness services, creating a more balanced and resilient housing
ecosystem. Taking a multifaceted approach to tackling the state’s housing shortage ensures that people at different stages of their
housing journey have access to appropriate and sustainable housing options. Increasing the supply of affordable homeownership
opportunities that are designed to meet community needs will reduce disparities in homeownership and wealth rates among people of
color in Oregon.

Both LIFT and HDIP seek to spur greater density in urban areas, support development in rural areas, and increase housing stock in a
way that is informed by local communities. LIFT Homeownership supports the construction of new housing that typically remains
permanently affordable. Due to restrictions of Article XI-Q bonds used for LIFT Homeownership, a general fund resource is needed to
ensure broader access to homeownership development opportunities. HDIP supports housing development on tribal land, encourages
generational wealth building among communities that have been historically excluded from homeownership, and invests in new and
innovative models of homeownership development.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                         107BF02
                                                                558
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
How Achieved

LIFT Homeownership: The objective of the LIFT Homeownership Program is to build new affordable homes for low-income
households. With a focus on increasing housing supply, LIFT funds can only be used for new construction and homes must be ready
for sale within 36 months of receiving a funding reservation. Utilizing Article XI-Q bond funding as a source for housing
development, LIFT requires the state to have an ownership or operational interest in any real property developed. For homeownership
opportunities, this means that projects must use a shared equity model such as a community land trust (CLT), condo, or leasehold
property.

Homeownership Development Incubator Program: HDIP increases the supply of affordable homes for purchase and supports
homeownership among communities that have been underrepresented as homeowners in Oregon through the following funding tracks:

1.    Grant Funds for Tribal and Sovereign Nations for the development of affordable homes for purchase and other homeownership-
related projects;
2.    Pre-development & Capacity Building (PDCB) grants to build the capacity of organizations that are seeking to establish or scale
operations and increase the pipeline of developers receiving OHCS funding;
3.    Development Subsidies to finance the affordability gap for new construction of affordable homes or the conversion of existing
structures into new affordable homes for purchase. These awards fund innovative models of homeownership that don't qualify for
LIFT, seek to build generational wealth, and serve households with incomes up to 120% AMI; and
4.    LIFT Supplemental Grants to provide further subsidy for culturally specific, small, emerging and rural developers accessing
LIFT funds.

For the Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit this reflects funds need for cost of issuance and debt service payments
associated with this bond request.

Staffing Impact

OHCS is requesting a Homeownership Development Technical Assistance & Operations Analyst (OPA3) to provide high-touch
technical assistance and support to applicants and potential applicants- particularly culturally specific and tribal developers- to
increase access to OHCS Homeownership Development programs. This position will develop the process for technical assistance and
support the development and maintenance of key operational systems and processes for Homeownership Development.

Operations & Policy Analyst 3, Permanent Full Time



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                          107BF02
                                                                 559
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
Quantifying Results
Outcomes
This investment package aligns with the Governor’s housing production goals and contributes to long-term housing stability and
homelessness prevention.

LIFT: Since 2018, OHCS has completed six LIFT Homeownership NOFA cycles. It’s allocated nearly $90M in LIFT funds to
support the construction of 59 projects that will result in 894 affordable homes that will house an estimated 2,235 low-income
Oregonians. Of the LIFT homes that are complete and have homeowners, the majority are female-headed households with children.

Homeownership is currently redesigning LIFT HO in consultation with DOJ and a LIFT partner working group. We believe the
upcoming restructure will reduce barriers for nonprofit developers and construction lenders and facilitate greater access to LIFT,
setting the stage for larger program growth. With the assumption of an average per unit request of $150,000, we expect to subsidize
the construction of 666 new affordable homes for purchase with $100M in LIFT. This will create homes for an estimated 1,665
Oregonians.

HDIP: Through $40M legislative funding for HDIP in the 2021-2023 biennium, OHCS funded five tribal homeownership projects, 17
non-tribal homeownership development projects, distributed 14 PDCB grants, and provided market cost offset funds to 11 LIFT
projects that faced unexpected costs due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This prior HDIP funding will lead to 318 new affordable homes
by 2026, and more down the line due to longer term efforts completed through the PDCB grants.

OHCS estimates that this general fund investment will lead to the development of over 300 new affordable homes within three years.
At least 6 tribes will receive funding to create an estimated 31 new homes and support an additional 20 tribal members to become
homeowners. Additionally, OHCS will fund 20 unique organizations to scale homeownership development operations and/or conduct
pre-development work to start the process of getting shovel-ready so they qualify for development funding. This seed funding will
broaden the state’s network of culturally specific and emerging homeownership developers.

Together, this LIFT and HDIP investment will create nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase within three years, the majority
of which will remain affordable in perpetuity.
Racial Equity Strategy
What are the racial disparities in the population that are intended to be served through this program (e.g., racial disparities in substance
use treatment access). How will this policy package, including the implementation of the policy package, address these disparities and
equitably serve Oregonians?


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                               107BF02
                                                                    560
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
Racial disparities in homeownership rates across Oregon are vast. Since 2013, the gap between Black and American Indian and
Alaskan Native homeownership rates and white homeownership rates has widened. Homeownership rates among other race and
ethnicities remain far below white homeownership rates (see chart below).

Housing costs have steadily been on the rise in Oregon. In 2023, the typical home in Oregon costs around $528,000 which is 24%
more than the US average, and 11% (or $52,000) higher than pre-pandemic sales prices. With interest rates at an all-time high,
housing affordability is further out of reach for many Oregonians, particularly Oregonians of color.

                                                               2013      2022




OHCS’ Homeownership Development programs—LIFT and HDIP—seek to eliminate gaps in homeownership rates in Oregon. Using
a targeted universalism approach, our current goal is a 66% homeownership rate in Oregon across all races and ethnicities. OHCS’
Homeownership Development programs work to achieve this through a multi-faceted approach:

   • Set-asides for Culturally Specific Developers. Culturally Specific developers reflect the communities they seek to serve and have
      lived experience and expertise in the needs of these communities. While developers must comply with Fair Housing laws,
      developers that take a culturally specific approach are best positioned to reach and support communities that have been under-
      represented as homeowners in Oregon.
   • HDIP Tribal Funding. With HDIP general funds, we will have a non-competitive set-aside for Tribal Homeownership projects.
      The parameters of this set aside were structured according to feedback from the tribes and will support development of
      homeownership opportunities for Tribal members.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   561
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
   • Technical Assistance: OHCS has received feedback from culturally specific, small, and rural developers that navigating OHCS
       funding is a barrier. Through this investment, we will have staff dedicated to supporting these developers to plan their
       homeownership pipeline, apply, and utilize OHCS Homeownership Development, making our programs more accessible and
       inclusive.
   • Pre-development funding: Traditionally, by the time a developer is ready to apply for OHCS Homeownership Development
       funding, they have already invested substantial capital in the project to ensure that it is shovel-ready. This can be a barrier for
       culturally specific, small, and rural developers. Through HDIP general funds, OHCS will be able to offer resources to
       culturally specific, small, and rural developers to scale their capacity and cover the costs of pre-development needed before
       they can apply for LIFT or other development funding. This too will make our programs more accessible and inclusive.
   • Minimum requirements for equity and community engagement: Homeownership Development projects must meet minimum
       criteria in six different categories, including equity and community engagement. In this category, applicants must demonstrate
       an active commitment to diversity equity and inclusion. The applicant also must detail plans to engage and support community
       members and potential homebuyers throughout every stage of the pre- and post-purchase process.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
OHCS tracks Homeownership Development racial equity outcomes using the following data points:

   • Number of culturally specific developers funded
   • Number of projects that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
   • Number of homes that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
   • Racial and demographic make-up of homeowners
   • MWESB contracting in terms of dollars spent and percentage of contractors on a project

With additional staff we will be able to expand our racial equity tracking. Additional data points may include (but aren’t limited to):

   • Culturally specific developers that received OHSC technical assistance and conversion to successful development applicants
   • Family wealth created through the affordable homeownership opportunity
   • Additional OHCS resources leveraged to reduce sales price and increase equity built in homes
   • Overall progress in reducing racial disparities in homeownership in Oregon
Administration
As mentioned above, there will be set asides for culturally specific developers as well as support and evaluation criteria to ensure that
all development is done in a culturally responsive way. Additionally, developers are expected to meet MWESB goals for their region.
They are evaluated on their MWESB strategy and OHCS collects data on MWESB contracting and spending.

Policy Package # 519 Preservation – Rental Risk of Loss, Expirations and Physical/Financial
Package Description
The State of Oregon is experiencing a housing crisis and has mobilized historic levels of state resources to address the profound, ongoing
need for affordable rental housing. As we continue developing new affordable housing at an unprecedented pace, it is essential and cost-
effective to ensure we preserve and maintain the current affordable housing stock.
Preserving existing rent-restricted housing that is affordable and well-maintained over the long term, is as important as building new
affordable housing. This package request proposes an increase to the State’s investment in preservation of affordable housing to better
meet the scale of current preservation needs, keeping thousands of rental housing units affordable and well maintained for decades to
come.

Purpose

This request is based on OHCS’ best estimate of the known preservation need, based upon data about projects that could lose
affordability restrictions or federal project-based rent assistance in the next few years, and the best information we have about projects
at risk of loss for other reasons. The $160 million recommended does not include manufactured home park preservation, which is
reflected in a separate package.

How Achieved

OHCS’ ongoing preservation strategy aims to help its partners plan for three distinct sets of challenges related to the affordability of
subsidized multifamily rental properties, to match the growing demand for preservation resources throughout the state of Oregon:

      •   Projects with expiring federal rent assistance

Projects eligible under this category will require an extension or renewal of long-term project-based federal rent assistance contracts.
Based upon the most recent estimation, there remain 47 relevant projects and 1,363 relevant units across the state, that are at risk of
losing their federal rent assistance over the next 5 years. Federal rent assistance is one of the most valuable homeless prevention tools
the federal government provides and losing these developments also results in losing the rent assistance forever.

      •   Portfolio Stabilization


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                      107BF02
                                                                   565
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
In addition to affordable units under federal contract, there are additional affordable housing programs which currently support an
estimated 33 projects and 2,289 estimated units, which are projected to expire in the next 5 years. The requested dedicated
preservation funding prioritizes projects and units outside of federal project-based sources that are at risk of imminent loss.

      •   Affordable properties at risk of loss due to urgent physical or financial challenges

Eligible projects under this program are properties that require urgent repair needs or have experienced operational losses which can’t
be addressed through existing reserves or cash flow, and which often threatens the housing stability and/or health of tenant families.
Known projects statewide with significant deferred rehab and/or operational challenges include but are not limited to:

   ✓ 30 known public housing authority-owned properties overdue for rehab, and/or planned for recapitalization as part of the HUD
     RAD program, totaling nearly 1,500 units.
   ✓ 90 known aging affordable properties owned by nonprofit partners with serious rehab needs, totaling nearly 5,000 units.

OHCS documents the range and extent of subsidized affordable housing in Oregon and maintains availability of such data through the
updated preservation dashboard on the OHCS preservation website, and the Oregon Affordable Housing Inventory.

For the Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit this reflects funds needed for cost of issuance and debt service payments
associated with this bond request.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
Equity and Racial Justice (ERJ) is a core priority in all OHCS programs, as expressed in the Statewide Housing Plan, which directs
OHCS to “Advance equity and racial justice by identifying and addressing institutional and systemic barriers that have created and
perpetuated patterns of disparity in housing and economic prosperity.” By its nature, Preservation focuses on protecting existing
vulnerable tenants. Therefore, OHCS Preservation efforts will continue to incorporate sustained and thoughtful attention to ERJ as a
high priority.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF02
                                                                   566
                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE

Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The 2024 Preservation Framework focuses resources on the most vulnerable tenants, including those with severe housing burdens, and
those who identify as a racial or ethnic minority, disabled, or elderly. Baseline data around recent performance in these areas are now
being tracked. With the advent of the ORCA process, comes a new “managed Queue” approach, which collects improved owner and
tenant data to better understand the full range of potential preservation needs. In short, this new protocol collects more information on
specific urgent rehabilitation needs, impact to vulnerable populations, and prioritizes stakeholders that meet the definition of OHCS
culturally specific organizations (CSOs) and culturally responsive organizations (CROs).
        1. Impact on tenants: measuring and tracking how Preservation efforts benefit the housing stability of BIPOC tenants.
        2. Supporting and prioritizing CSOs and culturally responsive organizations CROs: making sure that organizations rooted in
            and serving BIPOC communities have equitable access to OHCS Preservation resources and are prioritized.
Administration
These additional resources will allow more projects to move through the ORCA process and receive OHCS capital resources, which
have MWESB requirements that all projects must meet.

Policy Package #520 Preservation - Manufactured Park
Package Description
Provide resources to Manufactured Parks at risk of loss. OHCS would work with a third party to support efforts to purchase parks
through a mission-based nonprofit or a resident cooperative. Manufactured Parks are an important source of naturally occurring
affordable housing and need to be preserved wherever possible.

Purpose

Manufactured home parks are one of the most prevalent sources of naturally occurring affordable housing in the state, but they are
often unregulated and driven by market forces that can lead to sale and redevelopment without support. Manufactured home parks are
particularly vulnerable as the homeowner does not own the land that their home is on, yet their home is generally not mobile.
Therefore, if a park owner decides to sell the land, the homeowner is extremely vulnerable. These resources allow OHCS to support
interventions that result in park cooperatives or sales to non-profit mission-based owners that seek to maintain the property for parks.
Often interventions include infrastructure upgrades and other health and safety interventions.

How Achieved

OHCS has financed the acquisition of manufactured home parks by residents of the park through nonprofits that have worked through
technical assistance and a cooperative model or ownership by a mission-based nonprofit that leads to longer term affordability with
greater regulation and restrictions in place to ensure the park remains affordable. This policy option package would provide resources
specifically for this purpose, ensuring expansion of this model and greater preservation of these critical assets statewide.

For the Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit this reflects funds need for cost of issuance and debt service payments
associated with this bond request.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                    107BF2
                                                                   569
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE
Quantifying Results
Outcomes
This package will help expand OHCS’s ability to fund the preservation of a critical and often overlooked affordable housing stock to
ensure it remains available, accessible, and affordable to low-income families.
Racial Equity Strategy
By investing OHCS dollars into these properties that currently have no state regulation, oversight, or restrictions, we will be better
able to track what disparities exist in these types of properties and require both the high level and specific requirements around
equitable access that we do for all of our funding sources.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
Our agency collects baseline demographic information for all of our programs that have affordability restrictions and compliance
components. This includes a requirement to report race and ethnicity of tenants. By expanding our funding for MHPs, OHCS will
better be able to understand, track, and evaluate outcomes related to tenant demographics in these communities to an extent that is
challenging for this universe of property types given the lack of existing compliance or restrictions in place.
Administration
These dollars would be distributed through our general ORCA process, which incorporates standards around MWESB contracting and
has standards and incentives around working with culturally specific and responsive organizations, as well as set aside resources for
CSOs and nonprofits developing in rural areas.

Revenue Sources

Capital Construction

Capital Construction Executive Summary
Long Term Focus Area:  Healthy and Safe Communities, A Thriving Statewide Economy
Program Contact: Caleb Yant, Deputy Director




Program Overview
The Capital Construction program unit was created in 2015 to receive $40 million in proceeds from Article XI-Q bonds to create the
Local Innovation and Fast Track (LIFT) housing program. This was subsequently added to in 2017 with $80 million, and then expanded
in 2019 to fund both LIFT Affordable Rental and Homeownership development along with and the Permanent Supportive Housing
(PSH) affordable rental housing initiative for those experiencing chronic homelessness. Both programs were funded in 2019-21, 2021-
23, 2023-25 and additional investments are requested in 2025-27.
The LIFT Program's objective is to build new affordable housing and homeownership opportunities for low-income households,
especially families. LIFT Affordable Rental Housing resources are offered as loans to finance the creation of new affordable rental
housing units through the Affordable Rental Housing Divisions new Centralized Application process. The resources to develop PSH
units are also offered through this Centralized Application with a focus on serving those experiencing chronic homelessness by providing

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                            107BF02
                                                                  578
                                               BUDGET NARRATIVE
a housing unit, rent assistance and tenant support services to ensure ongoing housing affordability and tenancy. Affordable
Homeownership development is funded with LIFT resources along with the more flexible Homeownership Development Incubator
Program (HDIP) aiming to increase the supply of affordable homes for purchase by Oregonians with low to moderate incomes across
the state.
Program Funding Request
The 2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget includes $800 million in General Obligation bonds to continue the LIFT program and
$80 million in the PSH program.
                                                     Capital Construction
                                   19-21 Actuals    21-23 Actuals    23-25 LAB      25-27 GRB
                     Other Funds $    250,000,000 $    410,000,000 $  600,000,000 $   880,000,000
                     All Funds   $    250,000,000 $    410,000,000 $  600,000,000 $   880,000,000
                     Positions                   0                0              0               0
                   . FTE                      0.00             0.00           0.00            0.00

Program Description
LIFT allows Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) and its partners to add to the supply of affordable housing, especially
for historically underserved communities. At a time when Oregon has a significant shortage of affordable housing across the state, this
additional source of housing funds is a significant investment in response to the need. Permanent Supportive Housing provides rent-
assisted housing with tenancy supports to vulnerable households experiencing chronic homelessness.
Key to these programs is leveraging the use of the Article XI-Q bond funding for housing development; these funds require the state
meet constitutional requirements related to the ownership or operations of the property. OHCS has found success in strategies that
invest these funds as loans with corresponding operating controls in conjunction with distinct operational oversight authorities.
Program Justification
Development of affordable rental housing and homeownership programs helps ensure that all Oregonians have access to decent housing
which meets their basic needs. It allows them to reach their full potential by increasing the availability of affordable rental housing and
ensuring existing affordable rental housing stock is safe and decent, as well as reducing the housing cost burden for qualified tenants.
Program Performance
LIFT program results will be measured and assessed for performance. Currently identified outcome metrics for the LIFT program are:
   •   Increase in affordable housing inventory; measured by the number of new units built.
   •   More affordable rental housing units available in rural communities.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                              107BF02
                                                                   579
                                               BUDGET NARRATIVE
   •   More affordable rental housing units available that serve communities of color.
   •   More affordable homeownership units available in rural communities
   •   More affordable homeownership units available that serve communities of color
   •   Implement innovative and replicable cost containment strategies; measured through comparison of projects funded with LIFT
       proceeds to traditional multifamily housing costs, such as RS Means.
Permanent Supportive Housing will be measured by the number of new PSH units available to those experiencing chronic homelessness,
as referred through local coordinated entry systems. This collaboration will ensure that the state’s most vulnerable people, living without
housing, are provided safe, decent, and sanitary housing. Fundamental to all program efforts is a lens toward equity and racial justice,
ensuring that housing opportunities reflect the populations that have been historically under-represented in our public system supports.
Enabling Legislation and Program Authorization
ORS 458.485 – Local Innovation and Fast Track Housing
Funding Streams
Proceeds from the sale of Article XI-Q General Obligation bonds provide the funding for the LIFT program and the Permanent
Supportive Housing initiative. General Fund is appropriated to OHCS in order to make regular debt service payments on outstanding
bonds, shown in the Bond Activities and Debt Service program unit.

                                                        BUDGET NARRATIVE
Essential Packages
010 Vacancy Factor and Non-ORPICS Personal Services
This package contains adjustments for vacancy savings. In 2025-27 budget development a new method was implemented to bring the vacancy savings
for an agency to 5 percent of the current service level for salaries and wages. The non-ORPICS generated personal services inflation adjustment will
be 4.2 percent. This package include the Pension Obligation Bonds adjustment provided by the Department of Administrative Services Chief Financial
Office.

020 Phase-In/Phase-Out Programs and One Time Costs
Phased-in programs include new programs and expansions programs funded for less than 24 months during the prior biennium but require a full 24
months in the next biennium and reflect the added cost of the program above the 2025-27 base budget level.

Phase-outs are the result of decreased costs from the elimination of pilot or other programs and other one-time costs not funded in the 2025-27 biennium.

Phase-Out’s for the Capital Construction Division
   • Other Funds
          o Local Innovation Fast-Track Housing Programs - $440M
          o Permanent Supportive Housing - $160M

030 Inflation and Price List Adjustments
This package contains adjustments for inflation and other price list adjustments including State Government Service Charges and Usage Based Charges
as directed by Department of Administrative Services. Examples below.
   •   Standard Inflation 4.2%
   •   Professional Services 6.8%
   •   Attorney General 23.26%
   •   State Government Service Charges 33%

050 Fund Shift
This package contains adjustments to fund shift. This package was used to better align funding in the Central Services division to match to the agency
funding splits for cost allocation purposes. This package also contains the fund shift for lease payments to align funding with the agency funding.

Policy Package #515: Rental Development: XI-Q for LIFT and PSH
Package Description
This policy package proposes to continue funding the development of affordable rental housing and permanent supportive housing
through Article XI-Q Bond investment. The Governor’s recommended budget includes requests $780 million total Article XI-Q bonds,
of which $700 million will be allocated to the Local Innovation Fast Track (LIFT) program for affordable rental housing development
and $80 million of which will be used for development funding through the Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) program. Debt service
payments for Article XI-Q bonds are paid with General Fund resources.

Purpose

The need for housing and the capacity among affordable developing providers are high, so this policy package proposes to expand
available funding. The Article XI-Q Bond investment for affordable rental housing production proposed in this policy package aligns
with Pillar 5 of the Governor’s Housing Production Framework: “Funding and Programs Supporting Housing Production, Ongoing
Funding for Housing Development: A. Continue to maximize LIFT funding, low-income housing tax credits, and private activity bond
allocations for affordable rental and homeownership development.” LIFT program funding will continue to be the single most critical
piece of the agency’s efforts to quickly distribute resources for the development of more housing to meet the goals laid out in this
framework as well as the existing goals laid out in Executive Order 23-04.

Additionally, the Article XI-Q Bond investment for PSH development, coupled with proposals for services and rent assistance
funding, comprise a package that addresses the complex needs of unhoused persons who are unable to remain stably housed without
these supports. This program and the resources for it help to house some of the most challenging to house Oregonians through a
nationally recognized, evidence-based model. The State delivers this complex bundling of resources through a coordinated process at
OHCS. This package of proposals aligns with Pillar 2 of the Governor’s Housing Production Framework: “Core Program
Improvements, Permanent Supportive Housing.”

How Achieved
All funding for affordable rental housing is provided through the Oregon Centralized Application (ORCA), which makes OHCS’
funding process more efficient for both the agency and our development partners. In addition, the availability of Private Activity
Bonds in the 2025-27 biennium means OHCS will once again have access to 4% LIHTC, which is often paired with XI-Q Bond
funding to maximize the impact of Federal resources that the state receives.


2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                   107BF02
                                                                  583
                                                         BUDGET NARRATIVE

The XI-Q Bond investment for PSH development will be delivered in coordination with the funding for supportive services and rent
assistance that OHCS offers to ensure that tenants are being served in the holistic manner that the model calls for with as much fidelity
as possible.

Staffing Impact

No additional staff requested in this package.

Quantifying Results
Outcomes
From 2016 through 2023, OHCS delivered a total of $717,347,755 in LIFT funding across 6,723 units of affordable rental housing, for
an average of $106,701 in LIFT per unit.1 With a major caveat that costs are increasing across the board and that it will likely impact
per unit projections, the proposed $700 million in LIFT for rental housing and projecting the same average cost per unit forward
OHCS could support the development of approximately 4,500 total units of housing in the biennium. With the $80 million proposed
Article XI-Q Bond resources dedicated to PSH development which have higher per unit costs in general, OHCS anticipates that
approximately 500 units of PSH could be developed.
Racial Equity Strategy
This policy package will help address disparities in housing outcomes, including housing cost burden and homelessness, that exist for
most communities of color in Oregon.

Applicants seeking funding for rental development, including the funding proposed in this policy package, will do so through the
ORCA, which includes requirements and questions related to the Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing Plan; Diversity, Equity, and
Inclusion Agreement; equity and racial justice strategies; MWESB strategies; and engagement and community needs. This means all
projects seeking this funding will need to meet these racial equity-related requirements.
Additionally, the ORCA involves funding set-asides and technical assistance for culturally specific organizations and Tribes, as well
as rural development by nonprofits. The ORCA prioritizes these entities because they have not received an equitable percentage of




1 Affordable Rental Housing Dashboard | Tableau Public




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                                     107BF02
                                                                   584
                                             BUDGET NARRATIVE
resources from OHCS and other funders in the past, and they often must create projects with less capital, thinner margins, and greater
risks due to the needs in their tenant communities.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
The ORCA collects more data on race and ethnicity for all funding applicants compared to previous funding rounds in several ways: a
clearer process to identify as a culturally specific organization; an option for applicants to note whether their project is owned by a
culturally specific organization or BIPOC-led entity; clear MWESB requirements; an enhanced Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion
Agreement; a question that asks about racial equity-specific organizational development; and an Affirmative Fair Housing Marketing
Plan that asks applicants to identify communities of color and other populations least likely to apply for housing and create strategies
to ensure people from those communities apply to live in their housing.
Additionally, the Affordable Rental Housing Division will be collecting continuous feedback on all aspects of the ORCA and will
iterate the ORCA as OHCS, and development teams continue to gain experience with it.
Administration
All applicants seeking capital funding through the ORCA will be required to meet regional MWESB requirements for percentage of
contracted work to these types of entities, as well as answer a set of questions about their strategies to ensure they do so. If applicants
have failed to meet MWESB requirements in the past, they will be required to work with an MWESB consultant. Additionally,
applicants must answer a set of questions relating to equity and racial justice strategies, and one of the strategies they can select from a
list of options relates to ownership by a culturally specific organization or BIPOC-led organization.

Revenue Sources

Other Fund Capital Construction:
     General Obligation Bonds: $780,000,000

A corresponding policy package #515 in the Bond Activities and Debt Service program reflects the General Funds needed to support
debt service payments associated with this bond request.
