                                        586
                                          BUDGET NARRATIVE

Policy Package # 518 Homeownership Development: LIFT and HDIP
Package Description

This package supports two Homeownership Development programs that seek to increase the supply of affordable homes for purchase
in Oregon: a) an allocation of $100M in Article XIQ Bonds for the Local Innovations and Fast Track (LIFT) Homeownership
program; and b) an allocation of $50M in general funds for the Homeownership Development Incubator Program (HDIP).
Collectively, we expect that these two programs will lead to the construction of nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase.

Purpose

According to the Oregon Housing Needs Analysis, Oregon needs to increase the housing supply by 550,000 units over the next 20
years to keep pace with housing needs. Affordable units for purchase are an essential component of a healthy housing stock.
Affordable homeownership promotes community stability and continuity, as homeowners are more likely to stay in one place longer,
fostering stronger, more cohesive neighborhoods. By offering a pathway to economic mobility and stability, affordable
homeownership reduces the burden on rental markets and homelessness services, creating a more balanced and resilient housing
ecosystem. Taking a multifaceted approach to tackling the state’s housing shortage ensures that people at different stages of their
housing journey have access to appropriate and sustainable housing options. Increasing the supply of affordable homeownership
opportunities that are designed to meet community needs will reduce disparities in homeownership and wealth rates among people of
color in Oregon.

Both LIFT and HDIP seek to spur greater density in urban areas, support development in rural areas, and increase housing stock in a
way that is informed by local communities. LIFT Homeownership supports the construction of new housing that typically remains
permanently affordable. Due to restrictions of Article XI-Q bonds used for LIFT Homeownership, a general fund resource is needed to
ensure broader access to homeownership development opportunities. HDIP supports housing development on tribal land, encourages
generational wealth building among communities that have been historically excluded from homeownership, and invests in new and
innovative models of homeownership development.




2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                         107BF02
                                                                587
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
How Achieved

LIFT Homeownership: The objective of the LIFT Homeownership Program is to build new affordable homes for low-income
households. With a focus on increasing housing supply, LIFT funds can only be used for new construction and homes must be ready
for sale within 36 months of receiving a funding reservation. Utilizing Article XI-Q bond funding as a source for housing
development, LIFT requires the state to have an ownership or operational interest in any real property developed. For homeownership
opportunities, this means that projects must use a shared equity model such as a community land trust (CLT), condo, or leasehold
property.

Homeownership Development Incubator Program: HDIP increases the supply of affordable homes for purchase and supports
homeownership among communities that have been underrepresented as homeowners in Oregon through the following funding tracks:

1.    Grant Funds for Tribal and Sovereign Nations for the development of affordable homes for purchase and other homeownership-
related projects;

2.   Pre-development & Capacity Building (PDCB) grants to build the capacity of organizations that are seeking to establish or scale
operations and increase the pipeline of developers receiving OHCS funding;

3.    Development Subsidies to finance the affordability gap for new construction of affordable homes or the conversion of existing
structures into new affordable homes for purchase. These awards fund innovative models of homeownership that don't qualify for
LIFT, seek to build generational wealth, and serve households with incomes up to 120% AMI; and

4.   LIFT Supplemental Grants to provide further subsidy for culturally specific, small, emerging and rural developers accessing
LIFT funds.

Staffing Impact

OHCS is requesting a Homeownership Development Technical Assistance & Operations Analyst (OPA3) to provide high-touch
technical assistance and support to applicants and potential applicants- particularly culturally specific and tribal developers- to
increase access to OHCS Homeownership Development programs. This position will develop the process for technical assistance and
support the development and maintenance of key operational systems and processes for Homeownership Development.

Operations & Policy Analyst 3, Permanent Full Time



2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                          107BF02
                                                                 588
                                           BUDGET NARRATIVE
Quantifying Results
Outcomes
This investment package aligns with the Governor’s housing production goals and contributes to long-term housing stability and
homelessness prevention.

LIFT: Since 2018, OHCS has completed six LIFT Homeownership NOFA cycles. It’s allocated nearly $90M in LIFT funds to
support the construction of 59 projects that will result in 894 affordable homes that will house an estimated 2,235 low-income
Oregonians. Of the LIFT homes that are complete and have homeowners, the majority are female-headed households with children.

Homeownership is currently redesigning LIFT HO in consultation with DOJ and a LIFT partner working group. We believe the
upcoming restructure will reduce barriers for nonprofit developers and construction lenders and facilitate greater access to LIFT,
setting the stage for larger program growth. With the assumption of an average per unit request of $150,000, we expect to subsidize
the construction of 666 new affordable homes for purchase with $100M in LIFT. This will create homes for an estimated 1,665
Oregonians.

HDIP: Through $40M legislative funding for HDIP in the 2021-2023 biennium, OHCS funded five tribal homeownership projects, 17
non-tribal homeownership development projects, distributed 14 PDCB grants, and provided market cost offset funds to 11 LIFT
projects that faced unexpected costs due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This prior HDIP funding will lead to 318 new affordable homes
by 2026, and more down the line due to longer term efforts completed through the PDCB grants.

OHCS estimates that this general fund investment will lead to the development of over 300 new affordable homes within three years.
At least 6 tribes will receive funding to create an estimated 31 new homes and support an additional 20 tribal members to become
homeowners. Additionally, OHCS will fund 20 unique organizations to scale homeownership development operations and/or conduct
pre-development work to start the process of getting shovel-ready so they qualify for development funding. This seed funding will
broaden the state’s network of culturally specific and emerging homeownership developers.

Together, this LIFT and HDIP investment will create nearly 1000 new affordable homes for purchase within three years, the majority
of which will remain affordable in perpetuity.

Racial Equity Strategy
Racial disparities in homeownership rates across Oregon are vast. Since 2013, the gap between Black and American Indian and
Alaskan Native homeownership rates and white homeownership rates has widened. Homeownership rates among other race and
ethnicities remain far below white homeownership rates (see chart below).

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                          107BF02
                                                                 589
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
Housing costs have steadily been on the rise in Oregon. In 2023, the typical home in Oregon costs around $528,000 which is 24%
more than the US average, and 11% (or $52,000) higher than pre-pandemic sales prices. With interest rates at an all-time high,
housing affordability is further out of reach for many Oregonians, particularly Oregonians of color.

                                             2013     2022




OHCS’ Homeownership Development programs—LIFT and HDIP—seek to eliminate gaps in homeownership rates in Oregon. Using
a targeted universalism approach, our current goal is a 66% homeownership rate in Oregon across all races and ethnicities. OHCS’
Homeownership Development programs work to achieve this through a multi-faceted approach:

   • Set-asides for Culturally Specific Developers. Culturally Specific developers reflect the communities they seek to serve and have
      lived experience and expertise in the needs of these communities. While developers must comply with Fair Housing laws,
      developers that take a culturally specific approach are best positioned to reach and support communities that have been under-
      represented as homeowners in Oregon.
   • HDIP Tribal Funding. With HDIP general funds, we will have a non-competitive set-aside for Tribal Homeownership projects.
      The parameters of this set aside were structured according to feedback from the tribes and will support development of
      homeownership opportunities for Tribal members.
   • Technical Assistance: OHCS has received feedback from culturally specific, small, and rural developers that navigating OHCS
      funding is a barrier. Through this investment, we will have staff dedicated to supporting these developers to plan their
      homeownership pipeline, apply, and utilize OHCS Homeownership Development, making our programs more accessible and
      inclusive.

2025-27 Governor’s Recommended Budget                                                                          107BF02
                                                                      590
                                            BUDGET NARRATIVE
   • Pre-development funding: Traditionally, by the time a developer is ready to apply for OHCS Homeownership Development
       funding, they have already invested substantial capital in the project to ensure that it is shovel-ready. This can be a barrier for
       culturally specific, small, and rural developers. Through HDIP general funds, OHCS will be able to offer resources to
       culturally specific, small, and rural developers to scale their capacity and cover the costs of pre-development needed before
       they can apply for LIFT or other development funding. This too will make our programs more accessible and inclusive.
   • Minimum requirements for equity and community engagement: Homeownership Development projects must meet minimum
       criteria in six different categories, including equity and community engagement. In this category, applicants must demonstrate
       an active commitment to diversity equity and inclusion. The applicant also must detail plans to engage and support community
       members and potential homebuyers throughout every stage of the pre- and post-purchase process.
Accountability to Racially Equitable Outcomes
OHCS tracks Homeownership Development racial equity outcomes using the following data points:

   • Number of culturally specific developers funded
   • Number of projects that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
   • Number of homes that have been informed by and are centered around a culturally specific community
   • Racial and demographic make-up of homeowners
   • MWESB contracting in terms of dollars spent and percentage of contractors on a project

With additional staff we will be able to expand our racial equity tracking. Additional data points may include (but aren’t limited to):

   • Culturally specific developers that received OHSC technical assistance and conversion to successful development applicants
   • Family wealth created through the affordable homeownership opportunity
   • Additional OHCS resources leveraged to reduce sales price and increase equity built in homes
   • Overall progress in reducing racial disparities in homeownership in Oregon
Administration
As mentioned above, there will be set asides for culturally specific developers as well as support and evaluation criteria to ensure that
all development is done in a culturally responsive way. Additionally, developers are expected to meet MWESB goals for their region.
They are evaluated on their MWESB strategy and OHCS collects data on MWESB contracting and spending.

OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                                                            | Page 3 of 17
                                                                           609
Executive Summary
The mission of Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) is to provide stable and affordable housing
and engage leaders, to develop an integrated statewide policy that addresses poverty and provides
opportunity for Oregonians. This mission is accomplished through the administration of programs that
provide housing stabilization – from preventing and ending homelessness, assisting with utilities to keep
someone stable, to financing multifamily affordable housing, to encouraging homeownership. These
programs are delivered primarily through grants with community-based service providers, mainly local
government and nonprofit entities, which sets the foundation for the Grants Management System (GMS)
project.

The GMS project will provide OHCS with a solution to the various technological challenges and risks
currently facing the agency. The goal of the project is to provide a single solution to administer grants
throughout the agency from application to review to award and reporting. This modern platform will
facilitate better data for analysis, improved productivity and reliability, and a unified user experience for
both staff and applicants.

The current fractured grants processes are not adequate to address the needs of the agency, applicants, and
grantees; processes are decentralized with a lack of standardization, and interagency communication is
insufficient. These current processes put OHCS at risk. There is no single source of truth, transparency is
limited, and reporting is a manual process, making compliance, flexibility, and customer service tedious and
inefficient. Implementing a modern grants management solution and unifying business processes will allow
OHCS to improve its use of technology and mitigate these risks, while achieving operational and
organizational goals. It will help reduce administrative burden on staff, improve data accuracy, and ensure
compliance with funding guidelines as well as:

    •   Manage the very complex and administratively burdensome process of integrating both grantee and
        grantor requirements into a seamless process, including managing subrecipients.
    •   Ensure accountability and alignment of grant activities relative to organizational priorities, with
        reliable tracking of grant goals and performance metrics.
    •   Standardize data collection and reporting processes across OHCS, helping drive transparency,
        producing more timely and accurate reporting, and helping comply with federal grant requirements,
        such as the Uniform Guidance (2 CFR 200), DATA Act, GONE Act and GREAT Act.
    •   Provide early warnings and timely responses to relevant funding opportunities with configurable
        real-time email alerts.
As Oregon’s housing finance agency, grants management will continue to be the core of much of OHCS’
operations. This business case demonstrates and provides the context for the challenges OHCS faces in the
absence of a technology solution to automate processes and standardize the grants management
administration.

Start and End Estimate: April 2024 – June 2027
Project Duration: 3 years



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                       | Page 4 of 17
                                                         610
Budget: Current estimates for project costs for the total project budget are:
Table 1 Budget estimates

   Software Licensing              $1,500,000

  Contracts/Services               $1,000,000

  Implementation/Training          $2,500,000

  Support Agreement                $200,000

  Total                            $5,200,000




1.0 Overview and Background

1.1 Background
The grants management processes at OHCS are disjointed, cumbersome, and provide a non-intuitive
experience for staff, who depend on these systems for their daily work. The current business processes
often rely on manual spreadsheet management (i.e., Excel, Smartsheet) or inhouse developed systems and
require staff to spend substantial time and attention manually re-entering data into several systems that do
not sync. This is an impediment to productivity and customer service. These current processes place OHCS at
a disadvantage when administering grants, and current standards for data integrity and reporting are behind
today’s standards. Access to data and reporting on statistics, tracking, and reporting on Key Performance
Metrics, and ensuring reporting requirements from awardees are completed is limited and often requires
manual processing.

1.2 Scope Statement
The OHCS mission to provide stable and affordable housing and engage leaders and develop an integrated
statewide policy that addresses poverty and provide opportunities for Oregonians. This project will replace
and modernize the existing systems and processes used across OHCS for grants administration. The Grants
Management System project covers all the processes included in the procurement, configuration, testing,
training and change management of a new grants management solution. This will include the following
activities and deliverables:

    •     Implementation of the new solution(s).

    •     All activities related to the release of the Request for Proposal/Request for Quote to procure the
          solution(s) and select an implementation vendor.

    •     Performing all verification testing for the new system – business functions, user acceptance,
          integration, regression, and security.
    •     Project specific change management.



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                      | Page 5 of 17
                                                         611
     •    Technology and business process training for OHCS staff.
     •    Grants management system guidelines and policies.

     •    Project related communications.
The scope of the project does not include:

     •    Change management and business process support for employees external to OHCS.
     •    Business process training for processes outside of OHCS or the grants management system.
     •    Implementation of any tools not related to OHCS’ grants management workflows.

1.4 Services for Procurement
OHCS is requesting funds for independent quality management services and organizational change
management and will contract with iQMS and OCM vendors in spring of 2025. An overview of their roles and
responsibilities is detailed below:

   •   Independent Quality Management Services (iQMS) This vendor will provide planning and quality
       control deliverables, and continuous periodic reports throughout the lifetime of the project. Key
       deliverables include:
           o   Initial Risk Assessment
           o   Quality Standards Report
           o   Quality Control Checklists
           o   Quality Management Plan
           o   Baseline Quality Management Services Work Plan
           o   Lessons Learned Report
           o   Quality Control Review Report
           o   Periodic Quality Status Reports
           o   Quarterly Quality Assurance (QA) Status Reports
   •   Organizational Change Management: Leads efforts to work with all affected users and leadership to
       support culture change and ensure they are prepared for the significant impact any new processes
       will make on their day-to-day work and facilitate the adoption of new systems and solutions. Key
       deliverables will include:
           o   Organizational Change Management Plan
           o   Change Readiness Assessment
           o   Change Impact Assessment
           o   Quarterly Readiness Assessment Reports
           o   Communication Plan
           o   Training Plan

2.0 Overview and Objectives

2.1 Current State
To assist in identifying the current state of grants management and reporting, the project team created and
executed an information gathering survey and met with several divisions across the agency. This initial work
helped provide a deeper understanding of the business users current processes and needs as they relate to
the lifecycle of grants management and administration.

An initial survey was sent to selected staff. This information, combined with the GMS team’s continued
efforts to review documentation, interview stakeholders, and identify requirements has produced the
information in the table below. This information supports the need for a modern technology solution.
Table 3 Survey Observations


 Area                         Observations
 Technology                     •   23 different systems are used across divisions and roles to complete day-
                                    to-day grants management and administration work.
                                •   Lack of an application portal.
                                •   No integration between current systems.
 Process Automations            •   There are many manual processes.

                                •   Automation for data entry, workflow triggers, and communication with
                                    applicants and grantees are priorities for users.
 Modernization                  •   Survey respondents noted pain points with current workflows, including
                                    lack of functionality and inconsistent user experience.
                                •   Reporting and dashboarding capabilities are needed.
 Trainings and Process          •   Consistent training across the agency for grants administration
 Documentation


2.2.1 Current Systems
The software tools used by OHCS’ grants administration divisions and staff range from inhouse developed
applications to spreadsheets and tables. There are limitations to all these solutions, and they have created
the disjointed, cumbersome, and an often-unproductive experience for users. Many processes are manual,
which increases costs, and reduces productivity, performance, efficiency, and the quality of service provided
to Oregon agencies, applicants, and grantees. An overview of the systems is included below1:




OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                     | Page 8 of 17
                                                         614
Table 4 OHCS Grants Systems In Use




1
 Functionality: is the system meeting business needs?
Supportability: is OHCS able to support the system and/or receive adequate vendor support?
Security: can the system meet OHCS’ level of security requirements?
Performance: how does the system help staff complete their work quickly and efficiently?
Stability: how susceptible is the system to downtime and failures?
Red: high risk | Amber: medium risk | Green: low or no risk



2.2 Opportunity for Change
As illustrated above, OHCS employees use several systems to perform their normal day-to-day job functions.
Because these systems are not inter-related, staff are required to take multiple steps to complete simple or
routine tasks. With increasing workloads, including the expansion of resources, growth and diversification of
grantees, and rise in programmatic offerings, the lack of a modern solution exponentially reduces
efficiencies and increases the potential for errors. In addition, applicants must submit multiple applications
for grants and experience inconsistent processes from OHCS divisions. This creates inefficiencies and
productivity issues that can be addressed and resolved.

We have an opportunity to make a difference in how OHCS administers grants and handles some case
management work through the successful execution of this project. Merely replacing current systems is not
a viable solution. This modernization involves the adoption of new technologies, and upgrading systems and
software to meet the modern day needs of the business. This project will involve developing new
capabilities as well as taking the opportunity to redefine and reevaluate organizational processes. To
maximize this effort, OHCS’ requirements must be defined and developed with the experience of the core
project team.

Upon the successful execution of this project, OHCS will implement a new solution that includes an applicant
portal, case management, reporting and communication workflows by using a modern, streamlined, cloud-
based system. This will not only increase staff satisfaction with new and improved technology to complete


OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                    | Page 9 of 17
                                                           615
their work but will also lead to improved business processes, enhanced productivity, and better customer
service.

2.3 Future State
As envisioned by OHCS leadership and the GMS team, this project will contribute to agency goals by:

    •    Delivering a comprehensive solution that supports the OHCS mission and needs.
    •    Streamlining or modifying business processes and improving efficiencies.

    •    Improving the user experience by providing an integrated solution that is easy to use.
    •    Reducing data silos, manual processes and increasing collaboration across divisions.

    •    Ensuring stakeholder participation from planning through roll out, empowering them to be a part of
         and ready for the solution.
    •    Having a training plan in place so staff will know how to use the new solution to do their work.
OHCS intends to develop a formal RFP or RFQ with clearly defined requirements that will fulfill the current
and foreseeable future grants administration needs at OHCS. OHCS will implement a technology solution
that unifies the user experience, provides the agency with a suite of reports and data analytics, and equips
OHCS for continued success. The selected solution will significantly enhance interactions with grantee
organizations, such as Community Action Agencies, Continuums of Care, and community development
organizations. It will allow for greater collaboration internally and externally, improve the ability to measure
grantee performance, and better fulfill reporting obligations.

grants administration lifecycle and improve access to reports and data. The table below identifies benefits

4.0 Assumptions and Constraints

4.1 Assumptions
   •   The quantity of grants administered and awarded will increase based on historical data and current
       trends.

   •   OHCS will continue to use Microsoft products (Outlook, Word, Teams, PowerPoint, Excel, and
       SharePoint).
   •   OHCS leadership maintains an active interest and engagement with the project, its objectives, and
       success.
   •   The project will obtain full funding as requested to meet project objectives.
   •   OHCS stakeholders will be engaged and participate fully throughout the project’s lifecycle.
   •   This is a Stage Gate project and will follow the Enterprise Information Services (EIS) Stage Gate
       process.
   •   The selected system(s) will meet current and future business and IT security requirements.
   •   Business processes will change to avoid customizations and reduce system complexity.
   •   There will be an annual subscription for the solution.




OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                    | Page 12 of 17
                                                      618
    •   Subscription for the solution will contain sufficient Service Level Agreements and allow for
        negotiated future configurations.

    •   There may not be one solution that meets all the agency’s requirements.
    •   The selected solution will replace (in part or in whole) some existing systems, such as DISH and FPS,
        which are developed in-house at OHCS.

4.2 Constraints
    •   The system solution must be compatible with existing OHCS, Federal and State government systems.

    •   Ancillary systems must be considered during selection and implementation, potentially constraining
        the project schedule and resources.

    •   Current ancillary systems may require upgrades, or some ancillary systems may no longer be
        necessary following the implementation of the system.
    •   Business processes will need to adapt to fit the solution.
    •   OHCS may be unable to find a single solution that meets all its requirements and, instead, may have
        to adopt individual systems to meet the business need.

4.3 Requirements
The requirements stated below are a partial set of high-level requirements and apply to all potential systems
or solutions that might be selected for this project. The requirements below are mandatory. These represent
the project team’s current understanding of the requirements. These will be updated and managed through
the formal requirements management process.

    •   View and print reports.
    •   Identify trends.
    •   Flexible and intuitive system.
    •   Consistent navigation through system.

    •   Supports current web browser versions and window sizes.
    •   Role-based permissions.

    •   Event driven triggers/notifications.
    •   Applicant portal.
    •   Support administrators and staff.

    •   Compliant with state & federal accessibility laws.
    •   Redundant servers & systems located in the United States.
    •   Complies with State or Oregon data security standards.



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                   | Page 13 of 17
                                                        619
    •    Compatible with MS Office and SharePoint Online.
    •    Compatible with Microsoft Edge, Google Chrome & Firefox.

    •    24/7 support availability.

    •    Education & training for onboarding & after implementation.
    •    Support storage and indexing of applicant records.
    •    Workflow management.
    •    Auto-schedule deadlines.

    •    Contac information management.
    •    Filter/sort by custom fields.
    •    Automated/customizable reminders.
    •    Autofill forms.

    •    Create/use document templates.

5.0 Alternatives
The objective of the project is to procure an integrated solution that satisfies the agency’s grants
administration needs. The following are possible alternatives, including the recommended approach.
Table 6 Alternatives Summary

 Alternative                             Cost     Benefit              Risk             Schedule
 Commercial Off the Shelf
                                         $        Most beneficial      Low risk         Medium timeline
 Solution (COTS)
 Expand Existing Tools                   $$       Not beneficial       Medium risk      Medium timeline
 In-House Custom-Built Solution          $$$      Medium benefit       High risk        Long timeline
 Do Nothing                              $$$      Not beneficial       High risk        Short timeline



5.1 Alternative #1: Commercial Off the Shelf Solution (COTS)
Through conducted market research, OHCS is confident that a single solution or unified toolset exists that
will meet the needs of the grants administration process. Funding for the resources necessary to prepare for
implementation was requested and received as part of the 2023-25 Legislative Session, and the GMS project
is initiating the Stage Gate process with Enterprise Information Services (EIS) by submitting an Information
Technology Investment (ITI) form, Initial Complexity Assessment, Charter, and a Business Case for the
project. The team will work closely with procurement to post a Request for Information and determine the
procurement approach.



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                 | Page 14 of 17
                                                       620
The project managers for the GMS project are in the process of developing a detailed project schedule with
a start date of April 2024 and an end date of June 2027.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule make this the preferred alternative for
OHCS. A COTS solution brings a ready-made cloud-based solution which requires minimal configuration, is
scalable, and offers increased security. This solution offers a low-code/no-code burden on the agency for
continued configuration and development. Because there are existing examples of COTS solutions that have
been implemented in the grants administration industry, risk of a solution not meeting the needs of OHCS is
mitigated. A COTS solution avoids cost-uncertainty and accelerates the overall project schedule. These
factors demonstrate to OHCS there is a reliable solution that will meet or exceed the needs of the project,
while spending less money to implement a dependable solution in less time.

5.2 Alternative #2: Expand Existing Tools
OHCS could consider expanding the existing in-house tools. However, this alternative would not create a
seamless user experience. The user would continue to have to use multiple systems to accomplish daily
tasks, even if the platforms are improved. This approach does not reduce the number of systems needing IT
support nor does it solve the existing problem of manual tasks and duplicative data entry.

OHCS believes any potential benefits under Alternative #2 are substantially outweighed by the opportunity
cost of Alternative #1. Expanding existing tools would miss the opportunity to achieve efficiencies possible
with a single solution.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be an unacceptable
alternative for OHCS. Expanding existing tools would likely increase costs due to staff time, the need for new
infrastructure, and management of multiple projects, and would most certainly extend the schedule.
However, expanding existing tools would allow for tailoring to the unique needs of each division and
section. This siloed approach runs a substantially higher risk of failing to achieve the Agency’s vision of
procuring technology that will create a simpler, more intuitive experience through a truly integrated system.

5.3 Alternative #3: In-House Custom-Built Solution
OHCS does not believe the In-House Custom-Built Solution is feasible. A complex enterprise-level solution is
needed, and the cost and timeline would be prohibitive to develop it in house. Developing software in-
house at reasonable cost with the limited OHCS IT development staff would entail multiple years of
development time. An accelerated schedule would require substantially scaled-up IT staff, along with
hardware, etc.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be a disfavored alternative for
OHCS. A custom-built solution would theoretically allow OHCS maximum flexibility in building a solution
exactly to their needs. However, it would be the costliest, most time-intensive, and the scale required would
expose the agency to the most cost risk and technical risk. Additionally, there would be tremendous efforts
to build in back-end functionality that is not mission focused. In other words, an in-house custom-build
presents the greatest risk, most unpredictable cost landscape, longest timeline, while imposing permanent
IT staffing increases.



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                   | Page 15 of 17
                                                        621
5.4. Alternative #4: Do Nothing
OHCS does not recommend the do nothing alternative. Interviews with end users expressed dissatisfaction
with the current tools and frustration with the number of manual and repetitive processes, the need to
switch between systems to complete tasks, and a lack of interconnection and data sharing among current
tools.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be the least acceptable
alternative. Maintaining the status quo would expose the Agency to an extremely high level of known and
unknown risks. If the current systems become temporarily or permanently inoperable, business processes
would be forced into a completely manual mode, with an immediate and massive adverse impact on
delivery of grant services. The do-nothing solution presents by far the greatest risk, and virtually guarantees
the worst outcome: an expensive, non-integrated siloed system or set of systems.

6.0 Conclusion
As detailed above, OHCS’ current grants management tools are not meeting the needs of the agency. OHCS
requires a solution with broad and configurable functionality, suited for a large, diverse set of grants with
varying requirements and accounting methodologies.

There are several grant management software solutions in the marketplace. These range from free, open-
source applications suited to small philanthropic organizations, to bespoke enterprise-scaled systems for
complex organizations with greater than one thousand users. Some grant management software is meant to
be turn-key for specific use cases, while others are designed to be highly configurable and require more
systems administration. Solutions based on a Customer Relationship Management (CRM) platform may
provide more opportunities than a traditional grants management software to build out the system in a
highly configurable way. However, this flexibility may come with increased developer skills requirements for
implementation and for configuration when changes are required, even when the solutions are low or no-
code.

OHCS’ requires a solution with a variety of essential functions to support the entire lifecycle of a grant and
the ability to integrate with systems for email, accounting, document storage, Customer Relationship
Management (CRM) software, business intelligence or data visualization software, and possibly existing in-
house developed applications.

This project aligns with the State of Oregon Diversity Equity and Inclusion Plan’s racial equity goal to
“Improve equitable access to services, programs and resources, including education, health, housing, human
services, environmental justice, criminal justice and economic opportunities,” by ensuring OHCS’ technology
systems are modern, secure, accessible, and serve the needs of those who serve on behalf of all Oregonians.
It also aligns with the State Enterprise Information Services’ (EIS) strategies on Cloud computing, data
security, enhanced governance, and utilization of data as a strategic decision-making tool, by showing clear
intent to procure or reconfigure system replacements that meet these requirements.

Based on the cost, benefits, risks, and schedule detailed above, OHCS recommends approval of alternative 1,
COTS solution, to be funded through General Funds at an estimated program cost of $5,200,000. The project



OHCS/Grants Management System | 25-27 GMS Business Case v1.0                                    | Page 16 of 17
                                                        622
team is ready to launch the Grants Management System Project with next steps that include a formal
procurement process during the 25/27 biennium to acquire the solution.

OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                                                                 | Page 4 of 21
                                                                            627
Executive Summary
The mission of Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) is to provide stable and affordable housing and
engage leaders to develop an integrated statewide policy that addresses poverty and provides opportunity for
Oregonians. This mission is accomplished through the administration of programs that provide housing
stabilization – from preventing and ending homelessness, assisting with utilities to keep someone stable, to
financing multifamily affordable housing, to encouraging homeownership. These programs are delivered
primarily through grants with community-based service providers, mainly local government and nonprofit
entities.

In addition to funding critical nonprofit services, constituents regularly engage with OHCS staff, who must be
knowledgeable about available statewide resources, with an imperative need to deliver timely and accurate
information.

Due to significant increase in staffing and available resources without the infrastructure and technology to
support these, the Customer Relationship Management (CRM) project seeks to provide OHCS with a solution to
the various technological challenges and risks currently facing the agency. The goal of this project is to centralize
recordkeeping and communication for agency staff, modernizing data collection and increasing efficiency,
accuracy, and quality of customer service. This modern platform will facilitate better data for analysis, improved
productivity and reliability, and a unified user experience for staff, stakeholders, grantees, and community
members.

The current fractured communication process, involving siloed individual emails and phone calls, do not
adequately address the needs of agency staff, grantees, and community. Communication and recordkeeping are
decentralized and inaccessible by agency staff to make informed decisions and provide efficient customer
service. Current interagency communication is severely insufficient to meet the needs of the community and
grantees, leading to increased agency risk. As communication is not centralized and with significant increases in
new agency staff, staff cannot refer to institutional knowledge and has challenges providing accurate and timely
information to grantees and constituents alike. There is no single source of record, transparency is limited, and
reporting is a manual process, all of which makes compliance, flexibility, and customer service tedious and
inefficient. Implementing a modern CRM solution and unifying business processes will allow OHCS to improve its
use of technology and mitigate these risks, while achieving operational and organizational goals. It will help
reduce administrative burden on staff, improve data accuracy, and ensure compliance with funding guidelines as
well as:

    •   Manage communication with grantees, stakeholders, and community members.

    •   Ensure accountability and alignment of communication relative to organizational priorities, with reliable,
        efficient, and timely customer service.

    •   Standardize recordkeeping across OHCS, helping drive transparency, ensuring compliance across
        programs, producing more timely and accurate communication, increasing access to informed
        communication, and helping comply with federal grant requirements, such as the Uniform Guidance (2
        CFR 200), DATA Act, GONE Act and GREAT Act.
    •   Provide staff reminders via configurable real-time email alerts to respond to inquiries/requests.


OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                      | Page 5 of 21
                                                        628
As Oregon’s housing finance agency, communication is essential to upholding and maintaining a strong, credible
reputation to best serve the needs of Oregonians. will continue to be the core of much of OHCS’ operations. This
business case demonstrates and provides the context for the challenges OHCS faces in the absence of a
technology solution to centralize communication and recordkeeping in day-to-day administration.

Start and End Estimate: July 2025 – June 2027
Project Duration: 2 years

Budget: Current estimates for project costs for the total project budget are:

 Software Licensing             $1.25M (5-year total)

 Implementation/Training        $750K

 Total                          $2M



Preliminary budget estimates are derived from market research from a previous project that reviewed the CRM,
Microsoft Dynamics. Further market research and a formal solicitation will provide more clarity on budgetary
figures, following potential legislative approval.

1.0 Overview and Background
1.1 Background
According to Kotler and Keller (2016), customer relationship management (CRM) software consists of managing
and personalizing customer data, encompassing all aspects of customer and business interactions to increase
customer loyalty and provide quality customer service. Within government, federal, state, and local government
partners, Continuums of Care (CoCs), nonprofit entities, Community Action Agencies (CAAs), and more may
constitute as customers. As discussed by Pang and Norris (2002), government has launched efforts to improve
serving the public and increase customer service efficiency, particularly for operations with proximity to the
public. Through CRM tools, government may leverage technology by utilizing automated phone systems with
support for transferring calls and inquiries; implementing websites, portals, and payment systems to allow
customers to address needs; utilizing business intelligence and data analytics to garner insights and patterns on
customers; and overall ensure customization of goods and services (Pang & Norris, 2002).

Unfortunately, a centralized Customer Relationship Management (CRM) platform does not currently exist at
OHCS. Communication at OHCS is disjointed, siloed, cumbersome, and inconsistent between grantees, agency
partners, community members, teams, and departments. Without a centralized system of record for
communication combined with extensive agency growth of over 50% since 2019, staff lacks access to informed
systems of record, losing critical agency institutional knowledge regarding grantees, agency partners, staff, and
the community. Without access to a CRM, all parties have suffered via decreased quality customer service, due
to the lack of internal coordination, collaboration, and efficiency.

Existing business processes rely on individual emails and phone calls; if recordkeeping exists, these may include
manual spreadsheet management (i.e., Excel, Smartsheet) or other inefficient in-house tracking systems,



OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                    | Page 6 of 21
                                                        629
requiring substantial staff time and attention to upkeep and operating within siloes agencywide. Without a CRM
to manage and coordinate customer relationships and case management, OHCS experiences increased risk and
decreased credibility, due to the lack of a system of communication record. The lack of a CRM impedes
productivity and quality customer service while increasing agency risk. The successful implementation of a CRM
will require significant process engineering that is currently not in place. The current lack of processes places
OHCS at a significant disadvantage and high risk, in stark contrast with current business standards.

1.2 Scope Statement
This project will modernize communication systems to reflect current business standards, increase consistency
of agency messaging by staff to agencies/grantees and the public, increase agencywide collaboration, and
decrease agency risk by maintaining an accessible record of communication and institutional knowledge.

The CRM project covers all the processes involved in procurement, configuration, testing, training, and change
management of implementing a CRM solution. This will include the following activities and deliverables:

    •   Implementation of the new solution(s).
    •   All activities related to the release of the Request for Proposal/Request for Quote to procure the
        solution(s) and select an implementation vendor.
    •   Performing all verification testing for the new system – business functions, user acceptance, integration,
        regression, and security.
    •   Project specific change management.
    •   Technology and business process training.
    •   Customer relationship management system guidelines, policies, and procedures.
    •   Project-related communication.
    •   Business process reengineering
The scope of the project does not include:

    •   Change management and business process support for employees external to OHCS.
    •   Implementation of any tools not related to OHCS’ customer relationship/case management workflows.

1.3 Requirements
The requirements stated below are a partial set of high-level requirements and apply to all potential systems or
solutions that might be selected for this project. These represent the project team’s current understanding of
the requirements. These will be updated and managed through the formal requirements management process.

    •   Provide case management tools,
    •   Document and track customer inquiries (calls, emails, inquiries, etc.) and current customer contact
        information into a singular contact file,
    •   Triage cases, prioritizing and categorizing time-sensitive issues (e.g., eviction, foreclosure, etc.),


OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                         | Page 7 of 21
                                                         630
   •   Assign inquiries to staff based on subject matter expertise/role via request type,
   •   Ability to search for customers in database,

   •   Control access to confidential information via role-based access control (RBAC) permissions,

   •   View and print reports,
   •   Identify trends,
   •   Flexible and intuitive system,
   •   Consistent navigation through system,

   •   Supports current web browser versions and window sizes,
   •   Event driven triggers/notifications,
   •   Customer portal,
   •   Support administrators and agencywide staff,

   •   Compliant with state & federal accessibility laws,
   •   Redundant servers & systems located in the United States,
   •   Complies with State or Oregon data security standards,
   •   Compatible with MS Office (including Outlook), SharePoint Online, and grant management solution
       (preferred),

   •   Compatible with Microsoft Edge, Google Chrome & Firefox,

   •   24/7 support availability,
   •   Education & training for onboarding & after implementation,

   •   Support storage and indexing of applicant records,
   •   Workflow management,
   •   Auto-schedule deadlines,
   •   Contact information management,

   •   Filter/sort by custom fields/categories,
   •   Automated/customizable reminders,
   •   Autofill forms, and

   •   Create/use document/correspondence templates.

2.0 Overview and Objectives
2.1 Current State
To assist in identifying the current state of customer relationship management, the project team has initially
worked with Public Affairs and Ombudsperson staff to elicit the business requirements for an agencywide CRM
to enhance communication, coordination, and collaboration while improving overall customer experience. This
initial work helped provide a deeper understanding of the business users’ current processes and needs as they
relate to the communication among staff, agencies/grantees, and the community. Additional elicitation work is
required to ensure all impacted business areas are represented in the requirements. At present, the subsections
below provide a current state description that illustrates the need for an agencywide CRM.

 Area                     Observations
 Technology                   •   Lack of centralized communication leads to duplicative efforts by staff.
                              •   Inability to access communication records increases agency risk.
 Process Automations          •   Most processes are manual and siloed.

                              •   Records are currently not being centrally created or maintained.
                              •   Communication records are not immediately accessible to all agency staff to
                                  make informed decisions, decreasing staff’s efficiency.
                              •   Automation for data entry, workflow triggers, and communication with

                              •   Lack of incidence tracking and resolution.

2.1.2 Current Systems & Processes
OHCS staff utilizes individual emails and phone calls, tracking these on occasion through siloed, inhouse developed
applications like spreadsheets. Limitations exist to these current systems, lacking in staff accessibility and creating
information silos, resulting in a disjointed, cumbersome, and inefficient experience for staff and customers alike.
Processes are primarily manual, increasing cost while reducing productivity, performance, efficiency, and the
quality of service provided to Oregon agencies, applicants, and grantees.

Figure 1 below illustrates the current state, in which a customer may raise a concern to a variety of initial contact
points, all of which are siloed in their downstream processes.
Figure 1




Figure 2 and Figure 3 represent recurring chaotic and disjointed real world scenarios, resulting in decreased
quality customer service, customer satisfaction, and overall efficiency, which could be improved through the
implementation of a CRM. As described in detail in Figure 2 and Figure 3, the current state is insufficient to
handle customer inquiries, provide quality customer service, maintain and refer to critical records, and allow
staff the ability to perform duties effectively, increasing risk of damaging agency reputation.

2.2 Opportunity for Change & Business Drivers
As discussed above, OHCS employees do not have access to a centralized CRM to record, track, and monitor
communication. Because communication is not centralized and without access to a historical record, staff lack
the ability to make informed decisions. With increasing workloads, the lack of a modern solution exponentially
reduces efficiencies, decreases staff agency to efficiently perform their duties, and increases the potential for
errors. In addition, a lack of centralized communication inhibits customer service, as a customer may have to
contact multiple personnel to resolve an inquiry. A lack of a CRM also results in the potential of staff providing
inconsistent, unreliable information. A CRM would address and resolve existing inefficiencies and productivity
issues through centralizing communication and recordkeeping throughout the agency, aligning with industry
best practices for government to ensure customers accuracy and timely delivery of services (Kumar, 2009).

We have an opportunity to make a difference in how OHCS communicates with stakeholders and the public. A
CRM will enable staff to more effectively handle communication and case management, breaking existing silos
within the agency by increasing the accessibility of information via the successful execution of this project. As no
current system is presently in place to allow the accessibility of information, a CRM must be considered as a
viable opportunity to improve communication, increase efficiency, and reduce agency risk. This modernization
involves the adoption of new technologies, upgrading systems and software to meet the modern day needs and
standards of the business. This project will involve developing new capabilities as well as taking the opportunity
to redefine and reevaluate organizational processes. To maximize this effort, OHCS’ requirements must be
defined and developed with the experience of the core project team.
Table 1 - Business Drivers

                                    documentation of other employees to conduct an accurate and fair review.

2.3 Future State
As envisioned by OHCS leadership, this project will contribute to agency goals by:

    •   Delivering a comprehensive solution that supports the OHCS mission and needs,

    •   Streamlining or modifying business processes and improving efficiencies,
    •   Improving the user experience by providing an integrated solution that is easy to use,
    •   Reducing information and data silos, manual processes, and increasing collaboration and access to
        information across divisions,
    •   Ensuring stakeholder participation from planning through roll out, empowering them to be a part of and
        ready for the solution, and
    •   Having a training plan in place so staff will know how to use the new solution to do their work.
OHCS intends to develop a formal RFP or RFQ with clearly defined requirements that will fulfill the current and
foreseeable future CRM needs at OHCS. OHCS will implement a technology solution that unifies the user
experience, provides the agency with a suite of reports and data analytics, and equips OHCS for continued
success.

The selected solution will significantly enhance interactions with grantee organizations, such as Community
Action Agencies, Continuums of Care, and community development organizations, along with the community at-
large. It will allow for greater collaboration internally and externally, increase access to information sharing,
improve the ability to provide quality customer service, and better serve constituents and grantees alike.

3.0 Measurable Business Benefits
A successful implementation requires a solution that delivers a modern system to support all aspects of the
communication lifecycle, enabling information sharing agencywide and also allowing access to reports and data.
The table below identifies benefits that will be achieved and their measurement for success.

4.0 Assumptions and Constraints
4.1 Assumptions
   •   The quality of communication will improve based on increased access to recordkeeping.

   •   OHCS will continue to use Microsoft products (Outlook, Word, Teams, PowerPoint, Excel, and
       SharePoint). There is potential for integration or interface between these products and the desired
       solution.

   •   The need for an agencywide Grants Management System does not negate the need for a standalone
       CRM. Not all Customer Relationship Mgmt. activities would be captured inside a Grants Management
       System. However, there may be value in having an interface between these two systems to share
       contact record information and associated data.


OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                 | Page 15 of 21
                                                     638
    •   OHCS leadership maintains an active interest and engagement with the project, its objectives, and
        success.

    •   The project will obtain full funding as requested to meet project objectives.
    •   OHCS stakeholders will be engaged and participate fully throughout the project’s lifecycle.
    •   This is a Stage Gate project and will follow the Enterprise Information Services (EIS) Stage Gate process.
    •   The selected system(s) will meet current and future business and IT security requirements.
    •   Business processes will change to avoid customizations and reduce system complexity.

    •   There will be annual ongoing maintenance costs for the solution.
    •   Subscription for the solution will contain sufficient Service Level Agreements and allow for negotiated
        future configurations.

    •   There may not be one solution that meets all the agency’s requirements.
    •   The selected solution will replace (in part or in whole) some existing systems, such as spreadsheets
        currently used to manage customer relationships.

4.2 Constraints
    •   The system solution must be compatible with existing OHCS, Federal and State government systems.
    •   Existing customer relationship data is not formatted or standardized in a way that would lend itself to
        ingestion into a new system easily. If ingestion of existing data is desired, the cost would need to be
        compared against the potential value.
    •   Business processes will need to adapt to fit the solution.

5.0 Alternatives
The objective of the project is to procure an integrated solution that satisfies the agency’s customer relationship
management needs. The following are possible alternatives, including the recommended approach.

 Alternative                          Cost         Benefit              Risk               Schedule
 Commercial Off the Shelf                          High potential
                                      $                                 Low risk           Medium timeline
 Solution (COTS)                                   Benefit
 Commercial Off the Shelf
                                                   High potential
 Solution (COTS) with Case            $$                                High risk          Medium timeline
                                                   Benefit
 Management Services
 In-House Custom-Built Solution       $$$          Medium benefit       High risk          Long timeline
 Do Nothing                           $$$          Not beneficial       High risk          Short timeline

5.1 Alternative #1: Commercial Off the Shelf Solution (COTS)



OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                   | Page 16 of 21
                                                        639
Through conducted market research, OHCS is confident that a single solution or unified toolset exists that will
meet the recordkeeping needs of staff. Funding for the resources necessary to prepare for implementation is
being requested as part of the 2025-27 Legislative Session. Pending funding approval, this project will initiate
the Stage Gate process with Enterprise Information Services (EIS) by submitting an Information Technology
Investment (ITI) form, Initial Complexity Assessment, Charter, and solidified Business Case for the project. The
team will work closely with procurement to post a Request for Information and determine the procurement
approach.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule make this the preferred alternative for OHCS.
A COTS solution brings a ready-made cloud-based solution which requires minimal configuration, is scalable, and
offers increased security. This solution offers a low-code/no-code burden on the agency for continued
configuration and development. Because there are existing examples of COTS solutions that have been
implemented in the industry, risk of a solution not meeting the needs of OHCS is mitigated. A COTS solution
avoids cost-uncertainty and accelerates the overall project schedule. These factors demonstrate to OHCS there
is a reliable solution that will meet or exceed the needs of the project, while spending less money to implement
a dependable solution in less time.

5.2 Alternative #2: COTS with Case Management Services
OHCS could consider a COTS included with a third-party providing case management services. While this would
reduce the demand on agency staff, the reliance on a third-party to provide case management could lead to
potentially unreliable communication. However, this alternative would not create a seamless user experience, as
there are instances (such as working directly with grantees) where staff is required to interact and provide
responses to ensure accuracy with program regulations and compliance. While this approach would assist with
incoming customer contact from the community, it would not assist with day-to-day grantee management and
relationship.

OHCS believes any potential benefits under Alternative #2 are substantially outweighed by increased risk, in
contrast to Alternative #1. Relying on a third party to provide case management would increase agency risk and
be non-advisable.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be an unacceptable alternative for
OHCS. Relying on a third party for general inquiries and case management would likely increase costs and would
most certainly extend the schedule, to ensure the third party provides accurate and reliable information.
However, this would increase staff capacity through managing general inquiries. This approach runs a
substantially higher risk of failing to achieve the Agency’s vision through engaging with a third party to provide
general case management and potential of risking agency reputation, if incorrect resources are provided.

5.3 Alternative #3: In-House Custom-Built Solution
OHCS does not believe the In-House Custom-Built Solution is feasible. A complex enterprise-level solution is
needed, and the cost and timeline would be prohibitive to develop it in house. Developing software in-house at
a reasonable cost with the limited OHCS IT development staff would entail multiple years of development time.
An accelerated schedule would require substantially scaled-up IT staff, along with hardware, etc.




OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                   | Page 17 of 21
                                                       640
Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be a disfavored alternative for
OHCS. A custom-built solution would theoretically allow OHCS maximum flexibility in building a solution exactly
to their needs. However, it would be the costliest, most time-intensive, and the scale required would expose the
agency to the most cost risk and technical risk. Additionally, there would be tremendous efforts to build in back-
end functionality that is not mission focused. An in-house custom-build presents the greatest risk, most
unpredictable cost landscape, longest timeline, while imposing permanent IT staffing increases to maintain the
solution.

5.4. Alternative #4: Do Nothing
OHCS does not recommend the do nothing alternative. Interviews with end users expressed severe
dissatisfaction with the current tools, frustration due to the lack of accessible recordkeeping (resulting in the
loss of institutional knowledge), challenge with providing consistent and reliable customer service, and risk
increased by increasing agency personnel.

Implementation considerations of cost, benefit, risk, and schedule lead this to be the least acceptable
alternative. Maintaining the status quo would continue to exponentially expose the Agency to an extremely high
level of known and unknown risks with new staff lacking reliable recordkeeping as a reference. Business
processes continue to be siloed by forced into being solely individual, with an immediate and massive adverse
impact on delivery of customer service. The do-nothing solution presents by far the greatest risk, and virtually
guarantees the worst outcome: an expensive, continually non-integrated siloed system or set of systems that
threatens the success of OHCS’ mission.

6.0 Conclusion
As detailed above, OHCS does not currently meet industry standards or business needs by utilizing a CRM, which
would modernize and improve the experience for customers and staff. OHCS requires a solution with broad and
configurable functionality, suited for a large, diverse set of divisions with varying requirements, yet enabling
staff to exercise personal agency to serve grantee, stakeholders, and the community.

Improving customer experience by CRM may also assist with increasing government reputation, strengthening
relationships with constituents, and increasing loyalty, further solidifying credibility and trust while encouraging
organizations/businesses and citizens to stay within the region instead of migrating (Da Silva & Batista, 2007).

There are several CRM solutions in the marketplace. These range from free, open-source applications suited to
small philanthropic organizations, to bespoke enterprise-scaled systems for complex organizations with greater
than one thousand users. Some CRM software is meant to be turn-key for specific use cases (i.e., healthcare,
ensuring compliance with HIPA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) requirements), while others
are designed to be highly configurable and require more systems administration. If a CRM platform allows build
out the system in a highly configurable way, this flexibility may come with increased cost via developer skills
requirements for implementation and for configuration when changes are required, even when the solutions are
low or no-code.

OHCS requires a solution with a variety of essential functions to support customer care and the ability to
integrate with systems such as email (if available), business intelligence or data visualization software, and
possibly other existing in-house developed applications.


OHCS/Customer Relationship Management (CRM) System| 25-27 CRM Business Case v0.1                     | Page 18 of 21
                                                         641
This project aligns with the State of Oregon Diversity Equity and Inclusion Plan’s racial equity goal to “Improve
equitable access to services, programs and resources, including education, health, housing, human services,
environmental justice, criminal justice and economic opportunities,” by ensuring OHCS’ technology systems are
modern, secure, accessible, and serve the needs of those who serve on behalf of all Oregonians. It also aligns
with the State Enterprise Information Services’ (EIS) strategies on Cloud computing, data security, enhanced
governance, and utilization of data as a strategic decision-making tool, by showing clear intent to procure or
reconfigure system replacements that meet these requirements.

Based on the cost, benefits, risks, and schedule detailed above, OHCS recommends approval of alternative 1,
COTS solution, to be funded through General Funds at an estimated program cost of $2,000,000. Pending
funding approval, the project team is ready to launch the CRM Project with next steps that include a formal
procurement process during the 25/27 biennium to acquire the solution.

Upon the successful execution of this project, OHCS will implement a new solution that potentially includes an
applicant portal, case management, reporting and communication workflows by using a modern, streamlined,
cloud-based system. This will increase not only staff satisfaction but customer satisfaction, assisting with
building and maintaining critical relationships, with new and improved technology to complete their work
leading to improved business processes, enhanced productivity, increased access to information, and better
customer service.

 Term                 Definition

 Customer             Software that consists of managing and personalizing customer data, encompassing
 Relationship         all aspects of customer and business interactions to increase customer loyalty and
 Management (CRM)     provide quality customer service.

 Modernization        The practice of leveraging technology to meet expanding business needs and goals to
                      exist and compete in today's digital environment.



 Solution             A system or collection of systems that satisfies the overall objective(s) of the project.



 System               A collection of software and/or application functionalities working together to
                      produce an outcome. This may be a Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS) System, or an
                      integrated custom system. For example, a system may be comprised of a single COTS
                      technology or many COTS technologies working together to produce a set of
                      functionalities.
